<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Faraday's First Law of Electromagnetic Induction:** Whenever the magnetic flux linking with a conductor or coil changes, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in the conductor. The induced EMF exists only as long as the change in flux continues.
>
> **Faraday's Second Law of Electromagnetic Induction:** The magnitude of the induced EMF in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linkages with respect to time.

**Mathematical Expression (Faraday's Second Law):**

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

Where:
- $e$ = induced EMF in **volts (V)**
- $N$ = number of turns in the coil
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux in **webers (Wb)**
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**
- Negative sign = **Lenz's Law** (opposes the cause producing it)

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine pushing a magnet into a coil of wire — a current flows even though the coil has no battery. Why? Because the magnetic "lines of force" (flux) threading through the coil **change** as the magnet moves, and nature "resists" this change by producing a voltage. This is exactly how every generator, transformer, wireless charger, and induction cooktop in the world works.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lenz's Law (Companion to Faraday's Law):** The induced EMF always opposes the cause that produces it. This is why the sign in the equation is negative — it enforces **conservation of energy**. Without Lenz's law, perpetual motion machines would be possible.

---

## 1.2 Generation of Alternating Voltage — Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition**
> **Alternating Voltage (AC Voltage):** A time-varying voltage whose polarity (positive/negative) reverses periodically with time, and whose magnitude also changes in a defined manner — most commonly **sinusoidally**.

An alternating voltage is generated when a **conductor (or coil) rotates in a uniform magnetic field**, OR when a **stationary coil experiences a rotating magnetic field**. In both cases, the *flux linkage* with the coil changes continuously, and by **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
Think of swinging a flashlight beam across a wall — the *bright spot* moves left-right, periodically going bright → dim → bright. Similarly, the induced EMF in a rotating coil goes through positive peak → zero → negative peak → zero, smoothly and continuously. This smooth, repeating swing is called a **sinusoidal waveform**.

---

## 1.3 Elementary Generator

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition**
> **Elementary Generator:** A single-loop rectangular conductor rotating about its own axis in a uniform magnetic field, with the two ends connected to slip rings and brushes to deliver the induced AC voltage to an external load.

The elementary generator is the **simplest conceptual AC source** — it consists of:
- A **rectangular coil (ABCD)** of $N$ turns
- A **uniform magnetic field** (provided by N–S poles)
- **Slip rings** (two) connected to the coil ends
- **Brushes** (carbon/copper) that ride on the slip rings to deliver output

As the coil rotates at an angular velocity $\omega$, the flux through it changes, and an **alternating EMF** is induced.

---

## 1.4 Representation of AC Voltage and Current

AC quantities can be represented in three equivalent ways:

1. **Waveform (Time-domain) graph** — $v(t)$ vs $t$
2. **Phasor (Rotating vector) diagram** — magnitude and phase angle
3. **Symbolic / Complex notation** — $V = V_m \angle \phi$ or $V = V_{rms} \angle \phi$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Favourite:** When asked to "represent an AC voltage", examiners expect the sinusoidal expression: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ along with the waveform sketch and a phasor diagram.

---

## 1.5 Sinusoidal Waveforms — Key Parameters

A sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is described by:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|-----------|--------|-----------|------|
| **Peak Value** | $V_m$ | Maximum instantaneous value | V (or A for current) |
| **Instantaneous Value** | $v(t), i(t)$ | Value at any time $t$ | V, A |
| **Period** | $T$ | Time for one complete cycle | seconds (s) |
| **Frequency** | $f$ | Number of cycles per second | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| **Angular Frequency** | $\omega$ | Rate of change of phase angle | rad/s |
| **Phase Angle** | $\phi$ | Angular displacement from reference | degrees / radians |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Frequency ↔ Frequency Relationship:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ (in rad/s) and $T = 1/f$ (in seconds). These are the **two most-asked relations** in KTU Part A questions.

---

## 1.6 Average Value, RMS Value, and Form Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Average Value (Mean Value) of AC:** The arithmetic average of all instantaneous values of a waveform over one complete cycle. For a pure sinusoid (symmetrical), the average over a **full cycle is zero** — so the **half-cycle average** is used: $V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$.

> **RMS Value (Root Mean Square):** The DC equivalent value of the AC quantity — i.e., the value of DC that would produce the **same heating effect** in a resistor. For a sinusoid: $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$.

> **Form Factor ($K_f$):** Ratio of RMS value to Average value — $K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$. For a pure sine wave: $K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$.

> **Peak Factor (Crest Factor) $K_p$:** Ratio of peak value to RMS value — $K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ for sinusoid.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy
If you have a 230 V AC wall socket at home, that **230 V is the RMS value** — not the peak! The actual peak voltage is $V_m = 230 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V. The RMS concept exists because **AC is measured by its heating effect**, just like DC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Faraday's Laws — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step logic of electromagnetic induction:**

1. **Condition for induction:** A conductor must *cut* magnetic flux lines OR the flux linking a coil must *change* with time.
2. **Magnitude of induced EMF** depends on:
   - Number of turns $N$
   - Strength of the magnetic field $B$ (or flux $\Phi$)
   - Speed of motion / rotation ($\omega$)
3. **Direction of induced current** is given by **Lenz's Law** (opposes the cause) — equivalently, by **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** (for generators).
4. **Nature of induced EMF**:
   - **Dynamically induced EMF** — conductor moves in a static field (e.g., generators).
   - **Statically induced EMF** — field changes around a stationary conductor (e.g., transformers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Out of every 5 board questions on this module, at least **1 question (often 7–14 marks)** is dedicated to Faraday's Law, Lenz's Law statement, or deriving the elementary generator EMF.

---

## 2.2 Generation of Alternating Voltage — Physics Behind It

In an elementary single-turn coil of area $A$ rotating in a uniform magnetic field $B$ at angular speed $\omega$:

- At any time $t$, the angle between the **normal to the coil** and the **magnetic field** is $\theta = \omega t$.
- The flux linking the coil at time $t$ is:

$$\Phi(t) = B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

- By Faraday's Law, the induced EMF in $N$ turns is:

$$e(t) = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[B A \cos(\omega t)\right] = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

- Defining **maximum EMF** $E_m = N B A \omega$:

$$\boxed{e(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)}$$

This is the fundamental equation of all sinusoidal AC generation.

---

## 2.3 Elementary Generator — Component-by-Component Logic

| Component | Function | Engineering Use |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Magnetic field system (N–S poles)** | Provides uniform flux density $B$ | Field winding or permanent magnets in real machines |
| **Armature coil (ABCD)** | Rotates in the field, cuts flux | Laminated silicon steel core in real machines |
| **Slip rings** | Provide continuous electrical contact as coil rotates | Made of brass/bronze in real alternators |
| **Brushes** | Transfer current between rotating slip rings and stationary load | Carbon or copper-graphite |
| **Shaft (prime mover)** | Mechanical input (turbine, engine) rotates the coil | Coupled to mechanical prime mover |

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip Rings vs. Commutator:** In an **AC generator (alternator)**, two **separate slip rings** are used (one per coil end) — the output is naturally alternating. In a **DC generator**, a **split-ring commutator** rectifies the output to unidirectional.

---

## 2.4 Sinusoidal Waveform — Parameter Analysis

A pure sinusoidal waveform: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$

**Frequency, Angular Frequency, and Period — Interrelations:**

| Relation | Formula | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Period → Frequency | $f = \frac{1}{T}$ | $T$ in seconds → $f$ in Hz |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | In rad/s |
| Instantaneous phase | $\theta = \omega t + \phi$ | In radians |
| Cycle time | $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | One complete revolution |

**Why is $\omega$ in rad/s and not rev/s?**
Because in $e = E_m \sin(\omega t)$, the argument of sine must be in **radians**. The factor $2\pi$ converts one revolution (360° = $2\pi$ rad) into radians.

---

## 2.5 Average Value — Detailed Reasoning

**Definition:** The average of instantaneous values over a specified interval.

**For a pure sinusoid over a full cycle:** 

$$V_{avg, full} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m \sin(\omega t)\, dt = 0$$

(Sine is symmetric about zero — positive and negative halves cancel.)

**For a half-cycle (0 to $T/2$ or 0 to $\pi$):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos\theta\right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cdot 2 = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**Numerical value:** $V_{avg} = 0.6366\, V_m$ (≈ 63.66% of peak).

---

## 2.6 RMS Value — Detailed Reasoning

**Definition:** RMS = Root of the Mean of the Squares of instantaneous values.

**Mathematical statement:**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$$

**For a sinusoid $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, dt}$$

Using $\sin^2 x = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}$:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{T}\int_0^T \frac{1-\cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, dt} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Numerical value:** $V_{rms} = 0.7071\, V_m$ (≈ 70.71% of peak).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Every AC voltmeter and ammeter displays **RMS** values, not peak. The 230 V / 110 V mains, the 415 V three-phase industrial supply, and instrument ratings are all in **RMS**.

---

## 2.7 Form Factor and Peak Factor

| Factor | Definition | Formula | Sinusoid Value |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Form Factor** $K_f$ | $\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | $\dfrac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.11$ |
| **Peak (Crest) Factor** $K_p$ | $\dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}}$ | $\sqrt{2} = 1.414$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Use of Form Factor:** In rectifier and transformer design, $K_f$ tells you how much the *average* (used in DC output after rectification) differs from the *RMS* (used for heating/insulation). A higher form factor means a "peaky" waveform.

---

> **This table is the only cheat-sheet you need before the KTU exam for this module. Memorize every cell.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | KTU Frequency |

| 3 | Instantaneous EMF (generator) | $e = E_m \sin(\omega t)$ | V | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Max EMF | $E_m = N B A \omega = N B A \cdot 2\pi f$ | V | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Frequency of generated EMF | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120}$ | Hz | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Concept | Real-World Application |
|---------|----------------------|
| Faraday's Law | Transformers, induction motors, generators, wireless charging, induction cooktops, MRI machines |
| Elementary Generator | Hydro generators, thermal power station alternators, wind turbines |
| RMS Value | All AC instrument ratings, household mains, industrial power distribution |
| Form Factor | Rectifier design, transformer insulation sizing, power quality analysis |
| Frequency | Power-grid synchronization (50 Hz India, 60 Hz USA), audio engineering (20 Hz–20 kHz) |

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Induced EMF in an Elementary Single-Turn Coil

**Setup:** A rectangular coil of area $A$, with $N$ turns, rotating in a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ with angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Establish the flux linkage as a function of time.**

Let $\theta$ be the angle between the **normal to the plane of the coil** and the **magnetic field direction** at any instant. As the coil rotates uniformly:

$$\theta = \omega t$$

The magnetic flux through a single turn at time $t$ is:

$$\Phi(t) = B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\theta) = B A \cos(\omega t)$$

**Text Row 1:** The cosine appears because the flux through the coil is maximum when the coil's plane is perpendicular to the field (normal aligned with field), and zero when the plane is parallel.

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction.**

For a coil of $N$ turns, total flux linkage is $\lambda = N\Phi$. By Faraday's Second Law:

$$e = -\frac{d\lambda}{dt} = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Text Row 2:** This is the universal mathematical statement — EMF equals the *negative rate of change* of flux linkage.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\Phi(t)$ with respect to time.**

$$e = -N \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[B A \cos(\omega t)\right] = -N B A \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\cos(\omega t)\right]$$

Using the chain rule $\frac{d}{dt}\cos(\omega t) = -\omega \sin(\omega t)$:

$$e = -N B A \cdot \left[-\omega \sin(\omega t)\right] = N B A \omega \sin(\omega t)$$

**Text Row 3:** The two negatives cancel — the minus from Lenz's law and the minus from differentiating cosine both disappear, leaving a clean positive sine.

**Step 4 — Define the maximum EMF and write the final sinusoidal expression.**

Let $E_m = N B A \omega$ (the peak value of EMF). Therefore:

$$\boxed{e(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)}$$

**Text Row 4:** This is the defining equation of an ideal sinusoidal AC source. Every AC generator in the world produces an EMF that follows this law (or its cosine-shifted form, depending on initial position).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency of EMF in a Multi-Pole Generator

**Setup:** A generator has $P$ magnetic poles ($P/2$ North + $P/2$ South), and the rotor runs at $N_s$ revolutions per minute (rpm).

**Step 1 — Time for one mechanical revolution.**

$$T_{mech} = \frac{60}{N_s} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Number of electrical cycles per mechanical revolution.**

In a 2-pole machine, 1 revolution = 1 electrical cycle.
In a $P$-pole machine, 1 revolution = $P/2$ electrical cycles (each pole-pair produces one cycle).

**Step 3 — Time period of one electrical cycle.**

$$T = \frac{T_{mech}}{P/2} = \frac{60/N_s}{P/2} = \frac{120}{P \cdot N_s} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{P \cdot N_s}{120} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{P N_s}{120} \text{ Hz}}$$

**Text Row:** This formula is the **synchronization equation** of every power station alternator — the grid frequency must be held at 50 Hz in India by adjusting the rotor speed $N_s$ according to the number of poles $P$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Value of a Sinusoid over a Half-Cycle

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of arithmetic mean.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{\text{Area under one half-cycle}}{\text{Base length of half-cycle}} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Pull $V_m$ out of the integral.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Integrate.**

$$\int_0^{\pi} \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_0^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) = -(-1) - (-1) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = 0.6366\, V_m \approx 63.66\% \text{ of } V_m}$$

**Text Row:** The $2/\pi$ factor is a constant of nature for pure sinusoids. The full-cycle average is zero — which is why we always use the **half-cycle** average for AC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: RMS Value of a Sinusoid (Heating Equivalent)

**Step 1 — Start from the RMS definition.**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and write $dt = d\theta/\omega$ with $\theta = \omega t$.**

When $t: 0 \to T$, then $\theta: 0 \to 2\pi$. Also, $T = 2\pi/\omega$, so $1/T = \omega/(2\pi)$:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\theta)\cdot \frac{d\theta}{\omega}} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} \sin^2(\theta)\, d\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$.**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}\, d\theta} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} (1-\cos 2\theta)\, d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term by term.**

$$\int_0^{2\pi} 1\, d\theta = 2\pi$$

$$\int_0^{2\pi} \cos(2\theta)\, d\theta = \left[\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}\right]_0^{2\pi} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Combine the integrals.**

$$\int_0^{2\pi} (1-\cos 2\theta)\, d\theta = 2\pi - 0 = 2\pi$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and simplify.**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4\pi}\cdot 2\pi} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Rationalize and evaluate numerically.**

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_m\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071\, V_m}$$

**Text Row:** The RMS value gives the DC equivalent — a DC voltage of $V_m/\sqrt{2}$ produces the **same heating** ($I^2R$ loss) in a resistor as the AC sinusoid of peak $V_m$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Form Factor and Peak Factor for a Sinusoid

**Step 1 — Form Factor definition.**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2V_m} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**

$$K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3.1416}{2 \times 1.4142} = \frac{3.1416}{2.8284} \approx 1.11$$

$$\boxed{K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11}$$

**Step 3 — Peak Factor definition.**

$$K_p = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

$$\boxed{K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414}$$

**Text Row:** These two dimensionless ratios are **waveform fingerprints**. A pure sinusoid has $K_f = 1.11$ and $K_p = 1.414$. A square wave has $K_f = 1.0$, $K_p = 1.0$. A triangular wave has $K_f = 1.15$, $K_p = 1.732$. A sawtooth has $K_f = 1.155$, $K_p = 1.732$.

---

## 3.6 📊 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Examples (Model-Answer Style)

### Example 1 — Frequency and Angular Frequency
Given: $f = 50$ Hz (Indian mains). Find $\omega$ and $T$.

**Solution:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

### Example 2 — RMS, Average, Form Factor
Given: $V_m = 325$ V (peak of 230 V mains).

**Solution:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{325}{1.4142} \approx 229.81 \approx 230 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 325}{3.1416} \approx 206.9 \text{ V}$$

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{230}{206.9} \approx 1.11 \checkmark$$

### Example 3 — Generated EMF in a Generator
Given: $N = 200$ turns, $B = 1.0$ T, $A = 0.05$ m², $f = 50$ Hz.

**Solution:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$E_m = N B A \omega = 200 \times 1.0 \times 0.05 \times 314.16 = 3141.6 \text{ V}$$

$$e(t) = 3141.6 \sin(314.16\, t) \text{ V}$$

### Example 4 — Synchronous Speed
Given: 4-pole generator, $f = 50$ Hz. Find rotor speed $N_s$.

**Solution:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

### Example 5 — Instantaneous Value
Given: $v(t) = 100 \sin(314 t + 30°)$ V. Find value at $t = 5$ ms.

**Solution:**

At $t = 0.005$ s, $\omega t = 314 \times 0.005 = 1.57$ rad $\approx 90°$.

$$v(5\text{ms}) = 100 \sin(90° + 30°) = 100 \sin(120°) = 100 \times 0.866 = 86.6 \text{ V}$$

# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Elementary AC Generator — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of an elementary AC generator from mechanical input to electrical output, illustrating the role of each component and Faraday's law in action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Mover e.g. Turbine, Engine] --> B[Mechanical Shaft]
    B --> C[Rotating Coil ABCD with N turns]
    D[Uniform Magnetic Field N S poles, B Tesla] --> C
    C --> E[Induced EMF e equals NBA omega sin omega t]
    E --> F[Slip Ring 1 and Slip Ring 2]
    F --> G[Carbon Brushes]
    G --> H[External Load Resistor Lamp Motor]
    H --> I[Sinusoidal AC Output v t equals Vm sin omega t]
    C --> J[Fleming Right Hand Rule determines direction]
    J --> E
```

**How to read the diagram:**
- **Prime Mover** rotates the shaft → coil rotates in field → flux changes → EMF induced (Faraday's Law) → current flows out via slip rings and brushes → sinusoidal AC delivered to load.
- Fleming's right-hand rule governs the *direction* of the induced current, completing the Lenz's Law loop.

---

## 4.2 AC Waveform, Phasor, and Symbolic Representations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Sinusoidal AC Voltage v of t equals Vm sin omega t plus phi] --> A[Time Domain Waveform Plot v vs t]
    R --> B[Phasor Diagram Rotating Vector of length Vm, angle phi]
    R --> C[Symbolic Form V equals Vm angle phi or V equals Vrms angle phi]
    A --> D[Peak Vm, Period T, Frequency f]
    B --> E[Phase angle phi in degrees or radians]
    C --> F[Polar Complex Rectangular Forms]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Any single sinusoidal AC quantity has **three equivalent representations** — examiners often ask students to **convert between them**. Always label $V_m$, $T$, $\omega$, $\phi$ on every diagram.

---

## 4.3 RMS Derivation — Visual Computation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Start with v of t equals Vm sin omega t] --> S2[Step 2 Square it v squared equals Vm squared sin squared omega t]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Average over one full cycle 1 over T integral from 0 to T]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Apply sin squared x equals 1 minus cos 2x over 2]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Integrate, cos term averages to zero over full cycle]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Result Vrms squared equals Vm squared over 2]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Final Vrms equals Vm over square root of 2]
    S7 --> S8[Numerical Vrms equals 0.7071 times Vm]
```

---

## 4.4 Sinusoid Parameter Map (Conceptual Web)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sinusoid v of t equals Vm sin omega t] --> B[Peak Vm]
    A --> C[Angular freq omega rad per s]
    A --> D[Phase phi]
    C --> E[Frequency f equals omega over 2pi in Hz]
    C --> F[Period T equals 2pi over omega in seconds]
    B --> G[RMS Vrms equals Vm over root 2]
    B --> H[Average Vavg equals 2 Vm over pi]
    G --> I[Form Factor Kf equals pi over 2 root 2 equals 1.11]
    G --> J[Peak Factor Kp equals root 2 equals 1.414]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Note for KTU Exam:** A clean, **labelled** sinusoidal waveform sketch with $V_m$, $-V_m$, $T/2$, $T$ axes marked is worth **2 marks** on its own in Part B. Always label axes ($v$ on y, $t$ on x) and mark the peak, zero crossings, and period.

---

## 4.5 Faraday's Law — Physical Picture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Changing Magnetic Flux Phi over time] --> P2[Induced EMF e in conductor or coil]
    P2 --> P3[Direction by Lenzs Law opposes cause]
    P2 --> P4[Magnitude by Faradays Second Law e equals minus N dPhi over dt]
    P3 --> P5[Conservation of Energy]
    P4 --> P6[Mathematical computation of EMF]
    P6 --> P7[Engineering application Generator Transformer Inductor Charger]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎓 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]

**State Faraday's Laws of Electromagnetic Induction. Explain the significance of Lenz's Law in the equation $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** Whenever the magnetic flux linking with a conductor or a coil changes, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in the conductor. The induced EMF exists only as long as the change in flux continues.

**Faraday's Second Law:** The magnitude of the induced EMF in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of flux linkages. Mathematically:

$$e = -N\frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Significance of Lenz's Law (the negative sign):** Lenz's Law states that the induced EMF always opposes the cause that produces it. The negative sign in Faraday's equation mathematically enforces this opposition. Its significance lies in the **conservation of energy** — if the induced EMF were to aid the cause, we could create a perpetual source of energy, violating the first law of thermodynamics. The negative sign ensures that mechanical work must be done against the induced EMF to sustain the motion, and this work appears as electrical energy.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]

**Define RMS value and average value of an AC waveform. Why is the RMS value preferred over the average value in AC measurements?**

**Model Answer:**

**Average Value:** The arithmetic mean of all instantaneous values of a waveform over a specified interval. For a pure sinusoid, the average over a full cycle is **zero** (due to symmetry), so the **half-cycle average** is used:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\, V_m$$

**RMS Value (Root Mean Square):** The square root of the mean of the squares of instantaneous values over one complete cycle. For a sinusoid:

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\, V_m$$

**Why RMS is preferred:**

1. **Heating equivalence:** RMS gives the DC equivalent — a DC value of $V_{rms}$ produces the same $I^2R$ heating in a resistor as the AC waveform.
2. **Non-zero for full cycle:** Unlike the average, RMS is **non-zero** for a complete cycle, making it a true measure of waveform magnitude.
3. **Instrumentation:** All AC voltmeters, ammeters, and wattmeters are calibrated to read **RMS** values.
4. **Power calculations:** $P = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$ is the standard power formula, requiring RMS quantities.

---

## 5.2 🎓 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of an elementary single-loop AC generator. Derive the expression for the instantaneous EMF generated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 3-phase alternator runs at synchronous speed. Each phase winding has 200 turns and flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. Calculate: (i) the synchronous speed, (ii) the maximum EMF per phase, and (iii) the RMS EMF per phase. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Construction, Working, and EMF Derivation

**Construction:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. The elementary generator consists of:

- A **rectangular coil ABCD** rotating about its own axis.
- A **uniform magnetic field** of flux density $B$ (between N–S poles).
- **Two slip rings** (one connected to end A, the other to end D of the coil).
- **Two carbon brushes** sliding on the slip rings to deliver power to the external circuit.

**Working Principle:**

When the coil rotates in the uniform magnetic field, the flux linking the coil changes continuously. By **Faraday's Law**, an EMF is induced in the coil. The direction of the induced current at any instant is given by **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule**. As the coil rotates one complete revolution, the flux variation is sinusoidal, producing a sinusoidal AC EMF at the output terminals.

**Derivation of Instantaneous EMF:**

Let $A$ = area of the coil, $N$ = number of turns, $B$ = flux density, $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), and $\theta = \omega t$ = angle between normal to coil and field direction at time $t$.

**Step 1:** Flux through the coil at time $t$:

$$\Phi(t) = B \cdot A \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Faraday's Second Law:

$$e = -N\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \cdot \frac{d}{dt}[BA\cos(\omega t)]$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate:

$$e = -NBA \cdot \left[-\omega \sin(\omega t)\right] = NBA\omega \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4:** Define $E_m = NBA\omega$:

$$\boxed{e(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t) \text{ volts}}$$

This is the instantaneous EMF of a single-phase AC generator, sinusoidal in nature with peak value $E_m$ and angular frequency $\omega$.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Number of poles: $P = 4$
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Number of turns per phase: $N = 200$
- Flux per pole: $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb

**(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \boxed{1500 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum EMF per phase:**

Angular velocity: $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 50 = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ rad/s.

$$E_m = N \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega = 200 \times 0.05 \times 314.16 = 200 \times 15.708 = \boxed{3141.6 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) RMS EMF per phase:**

$$E_{rms} = \frac{E_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3141.6}{1.4142} = \boxed{2221.4 \text{ V}}$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the average value and RMS value of a sinusoidal AC voltage. Hence obtain the value of form factor and peak factor for a sinusoidal waveform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sinusoidal AC voltage has a peak value of 311 V and a frequency of 50 Hz. Determine: (i) the RMS voltage, (ii) the average voltage (half-cycle), (iii) the form factor, (iv) the peak factor, and (v) the instantaneous voltage at $t = 2.5$ ms. Write the complete time-domain expression. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — Derivation of Average, RMS, Form Factor, Peak Factor

**Average Value (Half-Cycle):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos\theta\right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cdot 2 = \boxed{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}}$$

**RMS Value:**

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2\theta\, d\theta = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}(1-\cos 2\theta)\, d\theta = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi}\cdot 2\pi = \frac{V_m^2}{2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \boxed{\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

**Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11}$$

**Peak (Crest) Factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} = \boxed{\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $V_m = 311$ V, $f = 50$ Hz.

**(i) RMS Voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{311}{1.4142} = \boxed{219.91 \approx 220 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Average Voltage (half-cycle):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 311}{3.1416} = \frac{622}{3.1416} = \boxed{197.99 \approx 198 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{219.91}{197.99} = \boxed{1.11}$$

**(iv) Peak Factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{311}{219.91} = \boxed{1.414}$$

**(v) Instantaneous Voltage at $t = 2.5$ ms:**

$\omega = 2\pi f = 100\pi$ rad/s.
$\omega t = 100\pi \times 0.0025 = 0.25\pi = \pi/4 = 45°$.

$$v(2.5\text{ ms}) = 311 \sin(45°) = 311 \times 0.7071 = \boxed{219.9 \text{ V}}$$

**Complete Time-Domain Expression:**

$$\boxed{v(t) = 311 \sin(100\pi t) \text{ volts} = 311 \sin(314.16\, t) \text{ V}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Read This Before Writing the Exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in Faraday's Law:** Always write $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$. Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the Lenz's Law negative sign is missing.
>
> 2. **Confusing half-cycle average with full-cycle average:** For a sinusoid, the **full-cycle average is ZERO**. The meaningful average is the **half-cycle average**, $V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi$. Examiners will give **0 marks** if you write $V_{avg} = 0$ for a question asking for "average value" of AC.
>
> 3. **Mixing up RMS and average in instruments:** AC voltmeters read **RMS**, not average. Stating the wrong one costs at least **1 mark** in Part A.
>
> 4. **Units in generator EMF calculation:** $E_m = NBA\omega$ — if you forget $\omega = 2\pi f$ and use $f$ directly, the units will be inconsistent and you lose **1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Synchronous speed formula error:** The correct formula is $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, NOT $N_s = \dfrac{60 f}{P}$ (that is for 2-pole, sometimes mistakenly generalized). **Always derive or recall the $120$ factor** for $P$-pole machines.
>
> 6. **Not labeling the waveform:** A sinusoid drawn without $V_m$, $-V_m$, $T/2$, and $T$ marked on the axes will lose **at least 2 marks** in the diagram step of any 14-mark question.
>
> 7. **Phase confusion:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ — remember $\phi$ is the **leading** phase if positive. A 30° phase error in interpretation can cascade through a numerical problem.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Bullet Points Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ — negative sign = Lenz's Law = energy conservation.
- ✅ **Elementary Generator EMF:** $e = E_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $E_m = NBA\omega$.
- ✅ **Frequency–Pole–Speed Relation:** $f = \dfrac{P N_s}{120}$ Hz (or $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm).
- ✅ **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s. **Period:** $T = 1/f$ s.
- ✅ **RMS of sinusoid:** $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071\, V_m$. Always what instruments display.
- ✅ **Average of sinusoid (half-cycle):** $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\, V_m$.
- ✅ **Full-cycle average of sinusoid = 0** (due to positive–negative symmetry).
- ✅ **Form Factor:** $K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$ (sinusoid).
- ✅ **Peak Factor:** $K_p = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (sinusoid).
- ✅ **230 V mains = RMS**; actual peak = $230\sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V.
- ✅ **Slip rings** (alternator) vs **Commutator** (DC generator) — slip rings give AC; commutator rectifies to DC.
- ✅ **Fleming's Right-Hand Rule** → direction of induced current in generators.
- ✅ **Phasor representation:** $V = V_{rms} \angle \phi$ — magnitude is RMS, not peak, in power-system conventions.
- ✅ **Sinusoid identity used in RMS derivation:** $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 board favourite numericals:** Find $V_{rms}$, $V_{avg}$, $K_f$, $K_p$ from a given $V_m$ and $f$; find $N_s$ given $P$ and $f$.

---

topics.title: DC Circuits: Resistance in Series and Parallel, Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Laws, Voltage and Current Divider Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DC Circuits: Resistance in Series and Parallel, Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Laws, Voltage and Current Divider Rule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ohm's Law — The Foundation of DC Circuit Analysis

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided the physical conditions (such as temperature) remain constant. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of voltage to current being a constant, defined as the **Resistance (R)**, measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> "The current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided the physical conditions like temperature, pressure, etc., are kept constant."

**The Fundamental Equation:**

$$V = I \times R$$

Where:
- $V$ = Potential difference in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = Current in **Amperes (A)**
- $R$ = Resistance in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipe Model":**
Imagine a water tank connected to a pipe. The water pressure pushing through the pipe is like **Voltage (V)** — it is the "push" or "driving force." The rate of water flow (litres per second) is like **Current (I)**. Now, if you make the pipe narrower or fill it with sand, less water flows for the same pressure. This "narrowness" or "friction" is the **Resistance (R)**. So, more pressure $\rightarrow$ more flow, but more resistance $\rightarrow$ less flow for the same pressure. Ohm's Law simply states this relationship mathematically: $V = I \times R$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conductance (G)** is the reciprocal of resistance: $G = 1/R$, measured in **Siemens (S)** or **Mho ($\mho$)**. A high conductance means the material allows current to flow easily (like copper), while low conductance means it resists current (like rubber).

## 1.2 Resistance in Series — "The Single-File Highway"

**Formal Definition:**
Two or more resistors are said to be connected in **series** when they are connected end-to-end such that the **same current flows through each resistor**, and the total voltage across the combination is the **sum of the individual voltage drops**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Cars on a Single-Lane Road":**
Think of resistors in series as cars driving one behind another on a single-lane road. The **same number of cars (current)** must pass through every narrow section. The total "delay" (voltage drop) is the sum of delays at every narrow section. Hence, resistances add up directly.

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$$

## 1.3 Resistance in Parallel — "The Multi-Lane Highway"

**Formal Definition:**
Two or more resistors are said to be connected in **parallel** when they are connected across the **same two nodes**, so that the **same voltage appears across each resistor**, and the **total current entering the node splits** among the branches.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Water Flowing into Branching Pipes":**
Imagine a water main pipe that splits into three smaller pipes. The **pressure (voltage)** at the junction is the same for all three branches, but the **total flow (current)** is the sum of the individual flows in each branch. Adding more parallel pipes makes it *easier* for water to flow, hence the **equivalent resistance decreases**.

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In a parallel combination, the equivalent resistance is always **less than the smallest** individual resistance in the group. This is a high-yield concept tested frequently.

## 1.4 Kirchhoff's Laws — The Two Pillars of Network Analysis

### 1.4.1 Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) — The Law of Conservation of Charge
**Formal Definition:** The algebraic sum of all currents entering and leaving a **node (junction)** in an electrical network is **zero**. Equivalently, the sum of currents entering a node equals the sum of currents leaving the node.

$$\sum I_{entering} = \sum I_{leaving} \quad \text{or} \quad \sum I = 0 \text{ at a node}$$

**Analogy:** Think of a junction as a roundabout. The total number of cars entering the roundabout must equal the total number of cars leaving — cars cannot magically appear or vanish.

### 1.4.2 Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) — The Law of Conservation of Energy
**Formal Definition:** The algebraic sum of all **EMFs (voltage sources)** and **voltage drops** around any **closed loop** in an electrical network is **zero**.

$$\sum V = 0 \text{ around a closed loop}$$

**Analogy:** Imagine hiking around a mountain and returning to your starting point. The total elevation gained must equal the total elevation lost — your net change in altitude is zero.

## 1.5 Voltage Divider Rule (VDR) — Splitting Voltage Intelligently

**Formal Definition:** In a series circuit, the voltage across any resistor is **proportional to its resistance** relative to the total series resistance.

$$V_x = V_{total} \times \frac{R_x}{R_{total}}$$

**Analogy:** A long queue of resistors divides the total voltage like a tug-of-war — the "stronger" (higher resistance) member takes a larger share of the voltage.

## 1.6 Current Divider Rule (CDR) — Splitting Current Intelligently

**Formal Definition:** In a parallel circuit, the current through any branch is **inversely proportional to its resistance** (or directly proportional to its conductance).

$$I_x = I_{total} \times \frac{R_{eq}}{R_x} \quad \text{(for two resistors: } I_1 = I_{total} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Voltage Divider Rule (VDR) and Current Divider Rule (CDR) are heavily weighted in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorising these two formulas and their boundary conditions is critical for scoring above 80%.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Series and Parallel Networks Behave

### 2.1.1 Series Network Characteristics
When $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ are connected in series:
- **Same Current** flows through every resistor: $I_1 = I_2 = I_3 = \cdots = I_n = I$
- **Voltage Divides** across each resistor (largest resistor gets largest share)
- **Equivalent Resistance** is the algebraic sum: $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n$
- **Total Power** dissipated is the sum of individual powers: $P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + \cdots + P_n$
- If one resistor fails (open circuit), the **entire circuit stops working** — this is a major drawback of series circuits

### 2.1.2 Parallel Network Characteristics
When $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ are connected in parallel:
- **Same Voltage** appears across every branch: $V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = \cdots = V_n = V$
- **Current Divides** among branches (smallest resistor carries the largest current)
- **Reciprocal of Equivalent Resistance** is the sum of reciprocals
- **Conductances Add Directly:** $G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2 + \cdots + G_n$
- If one branch fails (open circuit), the **other branches continue to work** — this is why household wiring is in parallel

## 2.2 Why Kirchhoff's Laws Work — The Physical "Why"

- **KCL** is rooted in the **Law of Conservation of Electric Charge**: charge cannot accumulate at a node (in steady-state DC analysis). What flows in must flow out.
- **KVL** is rooted in the **Law of Conservation of Energy**: a unit charge traversing a closed loop returns to the same potential, so the net work done on it must be zero.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Must-Memorise" Table for KTU ESE.** All entries here have appeared in KTU past papers with a frequency of 2–3 times per semester.

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Boundary Case | Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | Linear, temperature-constant conductors | V, A, $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Conductance | $G = 1/R$ | Always positive | Siemens (S) |
| 3 | Power Dissipated | $P = V \cdot I = I^2 R = V^2 / R$ | Any resistive element | Watts (W) |
| 4 | Energy | $E = P \cdot t$ | DC circuits, $t$ in seconds | Joules (J) |
| 5 | Series Resistance | $R_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k$ | Same current through all | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | Parallel Resistance | $1/R_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} 1/R_k$ | Same voltage across all | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Two-Resistor Parallel | $R_{eq} = (R_1 R_2) / (R_1 + R_2)$ | Product-over-sum shortcut | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | KCL at a Node | $\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$ | Sum of currents $\sum I = 0$ with sign | A |
| 9 | KVL around a Loop | $\sum V_{rises} = \sum V_{drops}$ | Closed loop, $\sum V = 0$ with sign | V |
| 10 | Voltage Divider Rule | $V_x = V_{total} \cdot R_x / R_{total}$ | Series circuit only | V |
| 11 | Current Divider Rule (n branches) | $I_x = I_{total} \cdot G_x / \sum G_k$ | Parallel circuit only | A |
| 12 | CDR (Two Resistors) | $I_1 = I \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | $I_1$ is through $R_1$ | A |
| 13 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = V_{source}$ when $I = 0$ | $I = 0$ condition | V |
| 14 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} = V_{source} / R_{min}$ | $V = 0$ across element | A |
| 15 | Resistivity | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = length, $A$ = area | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Reminder (Critical for KVL):** While traversing a loop, if you encounter a **voltage source from $-$ to $+$** (a rise), take it as **positive**; if from $+$ to $-$ (a drop), take it as **negative**. For a **resistor**, if you traverse in the **direction of current flow**, the drop is **$-IR$**; against the current, the rise is **$+IR$**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Concept Used | Why It Matters |
|-------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Household Wiring** | Parallel circuits | Each appliance gets full 230 V; failure of one does not affect others |
| **Christmas Tree Lights (Old)** | Series circuits | Single bulb failure breaks the chain — an old design flaw |
| **Battery Packs** | Series/Parallel combination | Series increases voltage; parallel increases current capacity (mAh) |
| **Potentiometers (Volume Knobs)** | Voltage Divider | Taps a fraction of the input voltage to deliver a controlled output |
| **Ammeter Shunts** | Current Divider | Diverts most current through a low-resistance shunt, protecting the meter |
| **Wheatstone Bridge** | KCL + KVL | Used in strain gauges and precision sensors |
| **PCB Traces** | Ohm's Law + Resistivity | Designers calculate trace width to avoid excessive voltage drop and heating |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **KTU Board Examination Standard** — the kind of step-by-step working that examiners specifically look for to award full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Resistance in Series

**Statement to Prove:** The equivalent resistance of $n$ resistors connected in series is the algebraic sum of the individual resistances.

**Given:** $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ connected in series across a source of EMF $V$.

**Step 1 — State the defining property of a series circuit.**
In a series circuit, the **same current $I$** flows through every resistor.

$$I_1 = I_2 = I_3 = \cdots = I_n = I$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law across each resistor.**
The voltage drop across each resistor (by Ohm's Law) is:

$$V_1 = I \cdot R_1, \quad V_2 = I \cdot R_2, \quad V_3 = I \cdot R_3, \quad \ldots, \quad V_n = I \cdot R_n$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the closed loop.**
KVL states that the sum of all voltages around a closed loop is zero. The total source voltage equals the sum of the individual voltage drops:

$$V = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_n$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Ohm's Law expressions.**

$$V = I \cdot R_1 + I \cdot R_2 + I \cdot R_3 + \cdots + I \cdot R_n$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common current $I$.**

$$V = I \cdot (R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n)$$

**Step 6 — Define the equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$.**
For the entire series combination to behave as a single resistor $R_{eq}$ drawing current $I$ from source $V$:

$$V = I \cdot R_{eq}$$

**Step 7 — Equate and conclude.**

$$\boxed{R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Resistance in Parallel

**Statement to Prove:** The reciprocal of the equivalent resistance of $n$ resistors in parallel is the algebraic sum of the reciprocals of the individual resistances.

**Given:** $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ connected in parallel across a source of EMF $V$.

**Step 1 — State the defining property of a parallel circuit.**
In a parallel circuit, the **same voltage $V$** appears across every branch.

$$V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = \cdots = V_n = V$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to find current in each branch.**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1}, \quad I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2}, \quad I_3 = \frac{V}{R_3}, \quad \ldots, \quad I_n = \frac{V}{R_n}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node.**
The total current $I$ supplied by the source equals the sum of branch currents:

$$I = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \cdots + I_n$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the branch currents.**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_1} + \frac{V}{R_2} + \frac{V}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{V}{R_n}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common voltage $V$.**

$$I = V \cdot \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Define the equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$ and equate.**

Since $I = V / R_{eq}$:

$$\frac{V}{R_{eq}} = V \cdot \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $V$ from both sides and conclude.**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_k}}$$

**Special Case — Two Resistors in Parallel:**

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 R_2}$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)

**Statement to Prove:** In a series circuit with resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ across a source $V$, the voltage across $R_1$ is $V \cdot R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)$.

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law to the series combination.**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across $R_1$ alone (by Ohm's Law).**

$$V_1 = I \cdot R_1 = \frac{V}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot R_1$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{V_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

By symmetry:

$$\boxed{V_2 = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalised VDR:** For $n$ resistors in series, $V_x = V_{total} \cdot R_x / R_{total}$. The voltage across any resistor is **directly proportional** to its resistance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Current Divider Rule (CDR)

**Statement to Prove:** In a parallel circuit with two branches $R_1$ and $R_2$ carrying total current $I$, the current through $R_1$ is $I \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.

**Step 1 — Voltage across the parallel combination (by Ohm's Law).**

$$V = I \cdot R_{eq} = I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Current through $R_1$ branch.**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = \frac{1}{R_1} \cdot I \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $R_1$.**

$$\boxed{I_1 = I \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

By symmetry:

$$\boxed{I_2 = I \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDR Inverse Relationship:** Notice the "crossover" — the current through $R_1$ depends on $R_2$ in the numerator. This is the key source of student errors in KTU exams. **The smaller the resistance, the larger the current that flows through it.**

**Generalised CDR for $n$ Parallel Branches:**

$$I_x = I_{total} \cdot \frac{G_x}{G_1 + G_2 + \cdots + G_n} = I_{total} \cdot \frac{1/R_x}{1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + \cdots + 1/R_n}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Worked Example: Mixed Series-Parallel Network

**Problem:** Find the equivalent resistance between terminals A and B for the network: $R_1 = 6 \, \Omega$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$ and $R_3 = 12 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute the parallel combination $R_2 \parallel R_3$.**

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{4 \times 12}{4 + 12} = \frac{48}{16} = 3 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Add $R_1$ in series with $R_{23}$.**

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_{23} = 6 + 3 = 9 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{AB} = 9 \, \Omega}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Kirchhoff's Laws Analytical Justification

**KCL Proof from Charge Conservation:**

Consider a node where currents $I_1, I_2$ enter and $I_3, I_4$ leave. In a time $dt$, the charge entering is $(I_1 + I_2) \, dt$ and leaving is $(I_3 + I_4) \, dt$. By charge conservation, no charge can accumulate:

$$I_1 + I_2 = I_3 + I_4 \implies \boxed{\sum I = 0 \text{ at a node}}$$

**KVL Proof from Energy Conservation:**

Consider a unit positive charge traversing a closed loop. The work done by the electric field must be zero (charge returns to the same potential). Work done equals the sum of potential differences encountered:

$$\boxed{\sum V = 0 \text{ around any closed loop}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid syntax** for clean rendering across the KTU digital learning platform. They depict the topological structure of circuits, as physical resistor-rendering is best done on paper during lab work.

## 4.1 Series Circuit Topology (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[V source positive] --> rA[Resistor R1]
    rA --> rB[Resistor R2]
    rB --> rC[Resistor R3]
    rC --> srcB[V source negative]
    srcA -. same current I flows through all .-> rA
    rA -. same current I flows through all .-> rB
    rB -. same current I flows through all .-> rC
    style srcA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style srcB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rB fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rC fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The dashed annotations explicitly mark the defining property — the **same current I** flows through $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_3$. The equivalent resistance is simply the sum: $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$.

## 4.2 Parallel Circuit Topology (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeTop((Node A))
    nodeBot((Node B))
    src[V source]
    src --> nodeTop
    nodeBot --> src
    nodeTop --- p1[R1]
    nodeTop --- p2[R2]
    nodeTop --- p3[R3]
    p1 --- nodeBot
    p2 --- nodeBot
    p3 --- nodeBot
    nodeTop -. same voltage V across all branches .-> p1
    nodeTop -. same voltage V across all branches .-> p2
    nodeTop -. same voltage V across all branches .-> p3
    style nodeTop fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style nodeBot fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p1 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p2 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three branches connect the **same two nodes (A and B)**, so the **same voltage V** appears across each. The total current $I$ from the source splits into $I_1 + I_2 + I_3$ at Node A by KCL.

## 4.3 KCL Illustration — Node Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    n((Junction Node))
    i1[I1 entering] --> n
    i2[I2 entering] --> n
    n --> o1[I3 leaving]
    n --> o2[I4 leaving]
    n -. KCL: I1 plus I2 equals I3 plus I4 .-> o1
    style n fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px
    style i1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style i2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style o1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style o2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** At the junction, two currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ enter, and two currents $I_3$ and $I_4$ leave. KCL guarantees $I_1 + I_2 = I_3 + I_4$. This is the foundation of **nodal analysis**.

## 4.4 KVL Illustration — Loop Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    start((Start Point A)) --> e1[Battery EMF E]
    e1 --> rdrop1[Drop I times R1]
    rdrop1 --> rdrop2[Drop I times R2]
    rdrop2 --> rdrop3[Drop I times R3]
    rdrop3 --> back((Back to Point A))
    start -. KVL: E minus IR1 minus IR2 minus IR3 equals 0 .-> back
    style start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style back fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style e1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rdrop1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rdrop2 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rdrop3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single closed loop starts and ends at Point A. The battery provides a rise $E$, and each resistor causes a drop $IR$. KVL enforces $E - IR_1 - IR_2 - IR_3 = 0$, giving $I = E / (R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$.

## 4.5 Voltage Divider Network (Sensing Application)

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[V in] --> rtap[Resistor R1]
    rtap --> rb[Resistor R2]
    rb --> gnd[Ground]
    rtap -. Vout tapped here .-> voutNode((Vout Node))
    style vin fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style gnd fill:#808080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rtap fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rb fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style voutNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **potentiometer / sensor divider** architecture. $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. Used in **light sensors, temperature sensors, and volume knobs**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3 marks each, short answer) and Part B (14 marks each, internal choice with two sub-parts of 7 marks). All questions are tagged with a simulated KTU past-year reference to give you exam-realistic practice.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL). Apply KCL to find the current $I_3$ in a node where $I_1 = 5\,\text{A}$ enters, $I_2 = 3\,\text{A}$ enters, and $I_4 = 4\,\text{A}$ leaves.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) states that the algebraic sum of all currents meeting at a node (junction) in an electrical network is zero. It is based on the **Law of Conservation of Charge**.

$$\sum I = 0 \text{ at a node} \quad \text{or} \quad \sum I_{entering} = \sum I_{leaving}$$

**Application:**
Applying KCL at the given node:

$$I_1 + I_2 = I_3 + I_4$$

$$5 + 3 = I_3 + 4$$

$$8 = I_3 + 4 \implies I_3 = 4\,\text{A (leaving the node)}$$

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Three resistors of $10\,\Omega$, $20\,\Omega$, and $30\,\Omega$ are connected in parallel. Find the equivalent resistance using the product-over-sum method for the first two, then combining with the third.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Combine $R_1 = 10\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 20\,\Omega$ in parallel:

$$R_{12} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10 \times 20}{10 + 20} = \frac{200}{30} = 6.667\,\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Combine $R_{12}$ with $R_3 = 30\,\Omega$ in parallel:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_{12} \cdot R_3}{R_{12} + R_3} = \frac{6.667 \times 30}{6.667 + 30} = \frac{200}{36.667} = 5.455\,\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq} \approx 5.45\,\Omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Direct calculation $1/R_{eq} = 1/10 + 1/20 + 1/30 = 6/60 + 3/60 + 2/60 = 11/60$ gives $R_{eq} = 60/11 \approx 5.45\,\Omega$. ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Option A). **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain Ohm's Law. State the conditions for its validity. Two resistors of $4\,\Omega$ and $6\,\Omega$ are connected in series across a $20\,\text{V}$ battery. Find (i) the current through the circuit, (ii) the voltage across each resistor using the Voltage Divider Rule.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the network shown below (resistors $R_1 = 12\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$ in parallel; this combination in series with $R_3 = 4\,\Omega$ across a $24\,\text{V}$ source), find: (i) the total equivalent resistance, (ii) the total current from the source, (iii) the current through each parallel branch using the Current Divider Rule, (iv) the voltage across $R_3$.

#### Model Solution for B1(a):

**Ohm's Law Statement:**
Ohm's Law states that the potential difference across a conductor is **directly proportional** to the current flowing through it, provided physical conditions (like temperature) remain constant.

$$V = I \cdot R$$

**Conditions for Validity:**
1. The conductor must be at **constant temperature**.
2. The material must be **ohmic** (linear $V$-$I$ relationship), e.g., constantan, manganin.
3. There should be no **back-EMF** or non-linear effects.

**Calculation:**

(i) **Series equivalent resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 = 4 + 6 = 10\,\Omega$$

**Current through the circuit:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{20}{10} = 2\,\text{A}$$

(ii) **Voltage across $R_1$ using VDR:**

$$V_1 = V \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \frac{4}{10} = 8\,\text{V}$$

**Voltage across $R_2$ using VDR:**

$$V_2 = V \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 20 \cdot \frac{6}{10} = 12\,\text{V}$$

**Verification:** $V_1 + V_2 = 8 + 12 = 20\,\text{V} = V_{source}$ ✓ (KVL satisfied)

#### Model Solution for B1(b):

**Network:** ($R_1 = 12\,\Omega \parallel R_2 = 6\,\Omega$) in series with $R_3 = 4\,\Omega$, $V = 24\,\text{V}$.

(i) **Parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$:**

$$R_{12} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{12 \times 6}{12 + 6} = \frac{72}{18} = 4\,\Omega$$

**Total equivalent resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = R_{12} + R_3 = 4 + 4 = 8\,\Omega$$

(ii) **Total current from source:**

$$I_{total} = \frac{V}{R_{eq}} = \frac{24}{8} = 3\,\text{A}$$

(iii) **Current through parallel branches using CDR:**
The total current $I_{total} = 3\,\text{A}$ enters the parallel section.

**Current through $R_1$ (note the crossover — $R_2$ in numerator):**

$$I_1 = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 3 \cdot \frac{6}{12 + 6} = 3 \cdot \frac{6}{18} = 1\,\text{A}$$

**Current through $R_2$:**

$$I_2 = I_{total} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 3 \cdot \frac{12}{12 + 6} = 3 \cdot \frac{12}{18} = 2\,\text{A}$$

**Verification by KCL:** $I_1 + I_2 = 1 + 2 = 3\,\text{A} = I_{total}$ ✓

(iv) **Voltage across $R_3$:**

$$V_{R_3} = I_{total} \cdot R_3 = 3 \times 4 = 12\,\text{V}$$

---

### Question B2 (Option B). **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL). Apply it to a single-loop circuit with a $12\,\text{V}$ battery and three resistors $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, $R_3 = 7\,\Omega$ connected in series. Find the loop current and the voltage drop across each resistor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the given two-loop circuit, verify the answers using both mesh and nodal analysis. [Circuit: A $30\,\text{V}$ source in the left branch with $R_1 = 10\,\Omega$, and a $20\,\text{V}$ source in the right branch with $R_2 = 10\,\Omega$, sharing a middle branch $R_3 = 5\,\Omega$.]

#### Model Solution for B2(a):

**KVL Statement:**
Kirchhoff's Voltage Law states that the algebraic sum of all EMFs and voltage drops around any closed loop in an electrical network is zero. It is based on the **Law of Conservation of Energy**.

$$\sum V = 0 \text{ around a closed loop}$$

**Single-Loop Circuit:** $V = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, $R_3 = 7\,\Omega$ in series.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL (taking current $I$ in the clockwise direction):**

$$V - I \cdot R_1 - I \cdot R_2 - I \cdot R_3 = 0$$

$$12 - 2I - 3I - 7I = 0$$

$$12 = 12I$$

$$I = 1\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage drops:**

$$V_{R_1} = I \cdot R_1 = 1 \times 2 = 2\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{R_2} = I \cdot R_2 = 1 \times 3 = 3\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{R_3} = I \cdot R_3 = 1 \times 7 = 7\,\text{V}$$

**Verification by KVL:** $V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} + V_{R_3} = 2 + 3 + 7 = 12\,\text{V} = V_{source}$ ✓

#### Model Solution for B2(b):

**Two-Loop Network:** Two mesh currents $I_1$ (left loop) and $I_2$ (right loop), both assumed clockwise.

**Mesh 1 (Left Loop) KVL:**

$$30 - 10 I_1 - 5 (I_1 - I_2) = 0$$
$$30 - 10 I_1 - 5 I_1 + 5 I_2 = 0$$
$$30 - 15 I_1 + 5 I_2 = 0$$
$$-15 I_1 + 5 I_2 = -30 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Mesh 2 (Right Loop) KVL:**

$$-20 - 10 I_2 - 5 (I_2 - I_1) = 0$$
$$-20 - 10 I_2 - 5 I_2 + 5 I_1 = 0$$
$$5 I_1 - 15 I_2 = 20 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Solving the simultaneous equations:**
From (i): $I_2 = (-30 + 15 I_1) / 5 = -6 + 3 I_1$

Substituting into (ii):

$$5 I_1 - 15 (-6 + 3 I_1) = 20$$
$$5 I_1 + 90 - 45 I_1 = 20$$
$$-40 I_1 = -70$$
$$I_1 = 1.75\,\text{A}$$

Then: $I_2 = -6 + 3(1.75) = -6 + 5.25 = -0.75\,\text{A}$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign on $I_2$ indicates that the actual current direction in the right mesh is **counter-clockwise** (opposite to the assumed direction). The magnitude is $0.75\,\text{A}$.

**Current through middle branch $R_3$:**

$$I_{R_3} = I_1 - I_2 = 1.75 - (-0.75) = 2.5\,\text{A (from left to right)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **CDR Crossover Confusion (Most Common — 2 Marks Lost):** When using the Current Divider Rule for two resistors, students often write $I_1 = I \cdot R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)$ instead of the correct $I_1 = I \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. **Remember: the resistor that is NOT in the branch goes in the numerator.**
> 2. **VDR Applied to Parallel Circuits:** VDR is **only valid for series circuits**. Using it on a parallel network is a guaranteed zero for that sub-part.
> 3. **Sign Convention in KVL:** Failing to assign a consistent sign convention (clockwise traversal assumed positive) results in sign errors throughout. Examiners explicitly look for a stated sign convention in the first line.
> 4. **Not Verifying with KCL/KVL:** KTU's model answer scheme gives bonus credit for verification steps. Always check $I_{in} = I_{out}$ at nodes and $\sum V = 0$ around loops.
> 5. **Units Missing:** Numerical answers without units lose half a mark in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Skipping the "Statement" Portion:** In 7-mark sub-parts, the first 2 marks are awarded for the **statement of the law**, not the calculation. Students who jump straight to the math lose easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- [x] **Ohm's Law:** $V = I \cdot R$. Valid for linear, isothermal conductors. Non-ohmic devices (diodes, LEDs) do **not** obey it.
- [x] **Series Rule:** $R_{eq} = \sum R_k$. Same current. $V$ divides. **$R_{eq} > R_{max}$**.
- [x] **Parallel Rule:** $1/R_{eq} = \sum 1/R_k$. Same voltage. $I$ divides. **$R_{eq} < R_{min}$**.
- [x] **Two-Resistor Parallel Shortcut:** $R_{eq} = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. Memorise this — it appears in 60% of KTU numericals.
- [x] **KCL:** $\sum I_{in} = \sum I_{out}$ at any node. Conservation of charge.
- [x] **KVL:** $\sum V = 0$ around any closed loop. Conservation of energy.
- [x] **VDR:** $V_x = V_{total} \cdot R_x / R_{total}$. Series only. Voltage is **proportional** to resistance.
- [x] **CDR:** $I_x = I_{total} \cdot R_{other} / (R_1 + R_2)$ (two-resistor case). Parallel only. Current is **inversely proportional** to resistance.
- [x] **Power:** $P = VI = I^2 R = V^2/R$. Series total power = sum of individual. Parallel total power = sum of individual.
- [x] **Conductance:** $G = 1/R$. Measured in Siemens. In parallel, conductances add.
- [x] **Open Circuit:** $I = 0$, $V$ = full source voltage. **Short Circuit:** $V = 0$, $I$ = maximum (theoretically infinite if no internal resistance).
- [x] **Sign Convention for KVL:** Assume a direction (usually clockwise). EMFs traversed from $-$ to $+$ are **positive**. Resistor drops in the direction of assumed current are **negative**.
- [x] **Resistivity:** $R = \rho L / A$. Material property. Copper $\rho \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-8}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$. Nichrome $\rho \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-6}\,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$.
- [x] **Temperature Effect:** $R_t = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$, where $\alpha$ is the temperature coefficient. **KTU favourite 3-mark question.**
- [x] **Verification Trick:** Always end your KTU numerical by verifying KCL at one node and KVL around one loop. Examiners reward this with 1–2 bonus marks.

---

> **End of Module 1 — DC Circuits Notes**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Alternating Current (AC) Circuit?

An **Alternating Current (AC) circuit** is an electrical circuit in which the magnitude and direction of current (and consequently the voltage) vary periodically with time, most commonly following a **sinusoidal waveform**. Unlike Direct Current (DC), where voltage and current remain constant, AC continuously reverses polarity, making it the global standard for power generation, transmission, and distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GZEST204):**
> An AC circuit operates on a sinusoidal supply of the form $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $V_m$ is the **peak voltage** (in Volts, **V**), $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** (in rad/s), and $f$ is the **frequency** (in Hertz, **Hz**). The standard supply frequency in India is **50 Hz**.

### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Ideal Circuit Elements

In AC circuit analysis, the response of a circuit is governed by three passive linear elements, each exhibiting a unique voltage–current relationship:

1. **Resistor (R)** — Governed by Ohm's Law: $v = iR$. Dissipates energy as heat.
2. **Inductor (L)** — Stores energy in a magnetic field. Opposes changes in current.
3. **Capacitor (C)** — Stores energy in an electric field. Opposes changes in voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study Ideal AC Circuits First?**
> Understanding the behavior of *purely* resistive, inductive, and capacitive circuits forms the bedrock of analyzing **any** complex R-L, R-C, or R-L-C network. This is the foundational step before applying phasor analysis, impedance concepts, and resonance (KTU Module 1 & 2).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ocean Wave vs. The Garden Hose"

Imagine you are watering a garden:

- **Pure Resistor (R)** — Like water flowing through a **narrow pipe with friction**. Whatever pressure (voltage) you apply, water (current) flows instantly and in proportion. There is **no delay**. Energy is lost as heat (friction).
- **Pure Inductor (L)** — Like a **heavy flywheel connected to the water pipe**. When you suddenly apply pressure, the flywheel's inertia **delays** the flow. When you remove pressure, the flywheel keeps pushing water. The current *lags* behind the voltage.
- **Pure Capacitor (C)** — Like a **balloon attached at the end of the pipe**. Before water can flow, the balloon must first **fill up** (charge). Current flows *before* voltage builds up — current *leads* voltage.

This intuitive picture directly maps to the phasor relationships you will encounter throughout this module.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (Highly Used in KTU Exams):**
> **"ELI the ICE man"**
> - **ELI** → In an **L** (inductor): **E**MF (**E**) **L**eads **I** (current) → Voltage leads current.
> - **ICE** → In a **C** (capacitor): **I** (current) leads **E** (EMF/Voltage) → Current leads voltage.

---

## 1.4 Key AC Quantities — The Language of Sinusoids

For a sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_v)$ and current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_i)$:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|------|
| Instantaneous Voltage | $v(t)$ | Value at time $t$ | Volt (**V**) |
| Peak (Maximum) Voltage | $V_m$ | Maximum amplitude | Volt (**V**) |
| RMS Voltage | $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | DC equivalent heating value | Volt (**V**) |
| Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | Mean over half-cycle | Volt (**V**) |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Rate of rotation of phasor | rad/s |
| Frequency | $f$ | Cycles per second | Hertz (**Hz**) |
| Time Period | $T = \frac{1}{f}$ | Time for one full cycle | second (**s**) |
| Phase Angle | $\phi$ | Angular shift between V and I | radian / degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever an AC voltage or current is mentioned in a problem without specifying "peak" or "RMS," the default convention in Indian engineering practice (and KTU exams) is the **RMS value**. However, the *mathematical* sinusoidal equation uses the **peak value** $V_m$.
