# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purely Resistive AC Circuit

### Circuit Description
A **purely resistive circuit** contains only a resistor of value $R$ (measured in Ohms, $\Omega$) connected to an AC source. There is **no inductance** ($L = 0$) and **no capacitance** ($C = 0$).

### Voltage–Current Relationship
Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

By Ohm's Law:

$$i(t) = \frac{v(t)}{R} = \frac{V_m}{R} \sin(\omega t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

Therefore, the peak current is:

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R}$$

### Key Observations (Why & How)

- **In-phase relationship**: Voltage and current reach their zero, peak, and negative-peak values **at the same instants**. The phase difference $\phi = 0^\circ$.
- **Power**: The instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t) \geq 0$. Power is **always non-negative**, meaning the resistor **always absorbs** energy.
- **Average Power**: $P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R}$
- **Power Factor**: $\cos\phi = \cos(0^\circ) = 1$ (Unity Power Factor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** Incandescent light bulbs, electric heaters, and toasters behave almost like pure resistors when operated at 50 Hz.

---

## 2.2 Purely Inductive AC Circuit

### Circuit Description
A **purely inductive circuit** contains only an inductor of value $L$ (measured in Henries, **H**) connected to an AC source. The ideal inductor has **zero resistance**.

### Voltage–Current Relationship
For an ideal inductor, the governing equation is:

$$v(t) = L \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting the sinusoidal current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$v(t) = L \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[I_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = \omega L I_m \cos(\omega t)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta + 90^\circ)$:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin\left(\omega t + 90^\circ\right)$$

where:

$$V_m = \omega L \cdot I_m$$

### Inductive Reactance ($X_L$)

The opposition offered by an inductor to AC current is called **Inductive Reactance**, denoted $X_L$:

$$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L \quad \text{(measured in Ohms, } \Omega\text{)}$$

### Key Observations

- **Phase relationship**: Voltage **leads** current by $90^\circ$ (or $\frac{\pi}{2}$ rad). Alternatively, current **lags** voltage by $90^\circ$.
- **Power**: $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m I_m \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \sin(2\omega t)$. Power oscillates symmetrically between **positive and negative** with a **zero average**.
- **Average Power**: $P_{avg} = 0$ — the inductor **does not consume** net energy. It **stores and returns** energy to the source each cycle.
- **Power Factor**: $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$ — purely reactive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $X_L$ increases with frequency:** A higher frequency means current changes more rapidly, inducing a larger back-EMF ($v = L \frac{di}{dt}$), which opposes the change more strongly. At DC ($f = 0$), $X_L = 0$ — the inductor acts as a **short circuit** (a wire).

---

## 2.3 Purely Capacitive AC Circuit

### Circuit Description
A **purely capacitive circuit** contains only a capacitor of value $C$ (measured in Farads, **F**) connected to an AC source. The ideal capacitor has **zero leakage resistance**.

### Voltage–Current Relationship
For an ideal capacitor:

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[V_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = \omega C V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

$$i(t) = I_m \sin\left(\omega t + 90^\circ\right)$$

where:

$$I_m = \omega C V_m$$

### Capacitive Reactance ($X_C$)

The opposition offered by a capacitor to AC current is called **Capacitive Reactance**:

$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} \quad \text{(measured in Ohms, } \Omega\text{)}$$

### Key Observations

- **Phase relationship**: Current **leads** voltage by $90^\circ$ (or $\frac{\pi}{2}$ rad).
- **Power**: Like the inductor, average power is **zero**. The capacitor **stores energy in its electric field** and returns it to the source each cycle.
- **Power Factor**: $\cos(-90^\circ) = 0$ — purely reactive (leading).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $X_C$ decreases with frequency:** At higher frequencies, the capacitor has less time to charge/discharge in each cycle, so it passes more current (lower opposition). At DC ($f = 0$), $X_C \to \infty$ — the capacitor acts as an **open circuit** (a break).

---

## 2.4 Concept of Impedance (Z)

**Impedance** is the **generalized opposition** that a circuit offers to the flow of alternating current, encompassing both resistance (R) and reactance (X). It is a **complex quantity** (having both magnitude and phase).

### Mathematical Definition

$$\boxed{Z = R + jX = R + j(X_L - X_C)}$$

where:
- $R$ = Resistance (real part)
- $X = X_L - X_C$ = Net Reactance (imaginary part)
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$ (the imaginary unit, used in electrical engineering instead of $i$ to avoid confusion with current)

### Magnitude and Phase of Impedance

$$|Z| = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2} \quad \text{(in Ohms, } \Omega\text{)}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right) \quad \text{(phase angle)}$$

### Ohm's Law in AC (Phasor Form)

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z} \quad \text{or equivalently,} \quad I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{|Z|}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** The relationship $|Z| = \frac{V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}$ is the **AC equivalent of Ohm's Law** and is one of the most frequently tested formulas in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Peak → RMS Conversion | $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V | Applies to V, I, EMF |
| 2 | Pure Resistor: $V$ vs $I$ | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{R}$ | A | In phase, $\phi = 0$ |
| 3 | Pure Resistor: Average Power | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 R$ | W (Watt) | Power factor = 1 |
| 4 | Inductive Reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L = \omega L$ | $\Omega$ | V leads I by $90^\circ$ |
| 5 | Pure Inductor: Current | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_L}$ | A | Average power = 0 |
| 6 | Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ | $\Omega$ | I leads V by $90^\circ$ |
| 7 | Pure Capacitor: Current | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_C}$ | A | Average power = 0 |
| 8 | Net Reactance | $X = X_L - X_C$ | $\Omega$ | Sign determines V/I lead/lag |
| 9 | Impedance (Complex) | $Z = R + jX$ | $\Omega$ | Phasor form |
| 10 | Impedance Magnitude | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | $\Omega$ | Always positive |
| 11 | Phase Angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right)$ | rad or ° | $+90^\circ \leq \phi \leq -90^\circ$ |
| 12 | Power Factor | $\text{pf} = \cos\phi = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq \text{pf} \leq 1$ |
| 13 | Instantaneous Power (General) | $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$ | W | Varies with time |
| 14 | Average Power (General) | $P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | W | **Most important** |
| 15 | Reactive Power | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ | VAR (Volt-Amp Reactive) | Stored/returned |
| 16 | Apparent Power | $S = V_{rms} I_{rms} = \vert Z \vert I_{rms}^2$ | VA (Volt-Amp) | Vector sum: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ |
| 17 | Pure R: $\cos\phi$ | $1$ | — | Unity PF |
| 18 | Pure L or C: $\cos\phi$ | $0$ | — | Zero PF |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Element | Reason |
|-------------|---------|--------|
| Power transmission lines | Series inductance | Causes voltage drop; $X_L$ increases with load |
| Power factor correction | Shunt capacitors | Cancel inductive reactance; reduce losses |
| Tuning radio receivers | Variable C + L | Select frequency via resonance |
| Fluorescent lamp ballasts | Inductors | Limit current without dissipating power |
| Filter circuits (HPF, LPF) | L and C combinations | Use frequency-dependent reactance |
| Motor starters | Inductors | Provide inrush current limiting |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Current in a Purely Resistive AC Circuit

**Given:**
- AC source: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Circuit contains resistor $R$ only
- Find: Expression for $i(t)$, $I_m$, and average power $P$

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)
The sum of voltages around a closed loop equals zero. With only one element $R$:

$$v(t) - v_R(t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_R(t) = v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Apply Ohm's Law
The voltage across a resistor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it:

$$i(t) = \frac{v_R(t)}{R} = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{R}$$

### Step 3: Simplify
Define the peak current as $I_m = \frac{V_m}{R}$:

$$\boxed{i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)}$$

**Conclusion:** The current is sinusoidal with the **same frequency** and **same phase** as the voltage. Both waveforms cross zero simultaneously and reach peaks together.

### Step 4: Derive Average Power
Instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) = V_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t)$$

Averaging over one full cycle (the cosine term averages to zero):

$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 R = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage–Current Relationship in a Pure Inductor

**Given:**
- AC source: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Pure inductor of value $L$ Henries
- Find: Expression for $i(t)$, $I_m$, and $X_L$

### Step 1: Inductor Governing Equation
The voltage across an inductor is proportional to the **rate of change of current**:

$$v(t) = L \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Solve the Differential Equation
Let the current be assumed sinusoidal with a phase lag of $\phi$:

$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Differentiate with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{di(t)}{dt} = I_m \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

Substituting back into the inductor equation:

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = L \cdot I_m \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = \omega L I_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

### Step 3: Use Trigonometric Identity
Recall that $\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta + 90^\circ)$. So:

$$\omega L I_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) = \omega L I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi + 90^\circ)$$

For the LHS and RHS to be equal at all times $t$:

$$V_m = \omega L I_m \quad \text{and} \quad \phi = -90^\circ$$

### Step 4: Final Equations

$$\boxed{X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L}$$

$$\boxed{I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_L}}$$

$$\boxed{i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - 90^\circ)}$$

**Conclusion:** In a pure inductor, the current **lags** the voltage by $90^\circ$. The opposition is $X_L = \omega L$ Ohms.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Voltage–Current Relationship in a Pure Capacitor

**Given:**
- AC source: $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Pure capacitor of value $C$ Farads
- Find: Expression for $i(t)$, $I_m$, and $X_C$

### Step 1: Capacitor Governing Equation
The current through a capacitor is proportional to the **rate of change of voltage** across it:

$$i(t) = C \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2: Differentiate the Applied Voltage

$$\frac{dv(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[V_m \sin(\omega t)\right] = \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$i(t) = C \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t) = \omega C V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

Using $\cos(\theta) = \sin(\theta + 90^\circ)$:

$$i(t) = \omega C V_m \sin(\omega t + 90^\circ)$$

### Step 4: Define Capacitive Reactance and Peak Current
Define:

$$\boxed{X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}}$$

So the peak current is:

$$I_m = \omega C V_m = \frac{V_m}{X_C}$$

### Step 5: Final Equation

$$\boxed{i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 90^\circ)}$$

**Conclusion:** In a pure capacitor, the current **leads** the voltage by $90^\circ$. The opposition is $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ Ohms.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Impedance Triangle and Power Triangle

**Given:** A series R-L-C circuit (or any combination) with impedance $Z = R + jX$.

### Step 1: Convert to Polar Form
The complex impedance $Z$ can be expressed in polar form as:

$$Z = R + jX = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi$$

where:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} \quad \text{and} \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right)$$

### Step 2: Apply Ohm's Law (Phasor Form)
With voltage phasor $\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle 0$ and current phasor $\mathbf{I} = I_{rms} \angle -\phi$:

$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert}$$

### Step 3: Construct the Power Triangle
- **Active (Real) Power:** $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi = I_{rms}^2 R$ (Watts)
- **Reactive Power:** $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi = I_{rms}^2 X$ (VAR)
- **Apparent Power:** $S = V_{rms} I_{rms} = I_{rms}^2 \vert Z \vert$ (VA)

The power triangle satisfies:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

### Step 4: Power Factor
The **power factor** of the circuit is defined as:

$$\text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$$

- If $\phi > 0$ (inductive), power factor is **lagging**.
- If $\phi < 0$ (capacitive), power factor is **leading**.
- If $\phi = 0$ (purely resistive), power factor is **unity** (equal to 1).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Pure Resistor (R) | Pure Inductor (L) | Pure Capacitor (C) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Governing Equation | $v = iR$ | $v = L\frac{di}{dt}$ | $i = C\frac{dv}{dt}$ |
| Opposition | $R$ (constant) | $X_L = \omega L$ | $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ |
| Frequency Dependence | None | $\propto f$ | $\propto \frac{1}{f}$ |
| At DC ($f=0$) | Behaves as $R$ | Short circuit | Open circuit |
| At $f \to \infty$ | Behaves as $R$ | Open circuit | Short circuit |
| Phase of V wrt I | In phase ($0^\circ$) | V leads I by $90^\circ$ | V lags I by $90^\circ$ |
| Phase of I wrt V | In phase ($0^\circ$) | I lags V by $90^\circ$ | I leads V by $90^\circ$ |
| Peak Current | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{R}$ | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_L}$ | $I_m = \frac{V_m}{X_C}$ |
| Instantaneous Power | $V_m I_m \sin^2(\omega t) \geq 0$ | $\frac{V_m I_m}{2}\sin(2\omega t)$ | $\frac{V_m I_m}{2}\sin(2\omega t)$ |
| Average Power | $V_{rms} I_{rms}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Power Factor | $1$ (unity) | $0$ (lagging) | $0$ (leading) |
| Energy Stored | None (dissipated) | Magnetic field: $\frac{1}{2}LI^2$ | Electric field: $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ |
| Energy Behavior | Dissipated as heat | Exchanged with source | Exchanged with source |
| Phasor Diagram | $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{I}$ overlap | $\mathbf{V}$ points up, $\mathbf{I}$ right | $\mathbf{I}$ points up, $\mathbf{V}$ right |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of AC Circuit Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[AC Voltage Source] --> nodeB[Pure Circuit Element]
    nodeB --> nodeC{V / I Relationship}
    nodeC --> nodeD[Resistor R]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Inductor L]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Capacitor C]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Opposition = R]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Opposition = XL]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Opposition = XC]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[In Phase 0 deg]
    nodeH --> nodeK[V Leads I 90 deg]
    nodeI --> nodeL[I Leads V 90 deg]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Power Absorbed]
    nodeK --> nodeN[No Net Power]
    nodeL --> nodeO[No Net Power]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Real Power P]
    nodeN --> nodeQ[Reactive Power Q]
    nodeO --> nodeR[Reactive Power Q]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Impedance Z = R]
    nodeQ --> nodeT[Impedance Z = jXL]
    nodeR --> nodeU[Impedance Z = minus jXC]
```

---

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram — All Three Pure Cases Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CaseA[Pure Resistor R]
        rA[Reference Axis] --> vR[Voltage V]
        rA --> iR[Current I]
        vR -. same direction .-> iR
    end
    subgraph CaseB[Pure Inductor L]
        rB[Reference Axis] --> iL[Current I horizontal]
        rB --> vL[Voltage V vertical up]
        iL -. V leads I by 90 deg .-> vL
    end
    subgraph CaseC[Pure Capacitor C]
        rC[Reference Axis] --> vC[Voltage V horizontal]
        rC --> iC[Current I vertical up]
        vC -. I leads V by 90 deg .-> iC
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Time-Domain Waveform Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Sinusoidal AC Supply v of t] --> nodeWR[Waveform for R Circuit]
    nodeStart --> nodeWL[Waveform for L Circuit]
    nodeStart --> nodeWC[Waveform for C Circuit]
    nodeWR --> nodeWRd[V and I overlap perfectly]
    nodeWRd --> nodeWRp[Power is always positive]
    nodeWL --> nodeWLd[I shifted 90 deg to the right]
    nodeWLd --> nodeWLp[Power oscillates positive negative]
    nodeWC --> nodeWCd[I shifted 90 deg to the left]
    nodeWCd --> nodeWCp[Power oscillates positive negative]
```

---

## 4.4 Impedance Triangle — Impedance, Reactance, Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeH[Horizontal Axis Resistance R] --> nodeOrigin[Origin]
    nodeOrigin --> nodeV[Vertical Axis Net Reactance X]
    nodeOrigin --> nodeHyp[Hypotenuse Impedance Z]
    nodeH -. base .-> nodeHyp
    nodeV -. perpendicular .-> nodeHyp
    nodeHyp --> nodePhi[Phase Angle phi]
    nodePhi --> note1[phi greater than 0 Inductive Lagging]
    nodePhi --> note2[phi less than 0 Capacitive Leading]
    nodePhi --> note3[phi equals 0 Purely Resistive Unity PF]
```

---

## 4.5 Power Triangle — P, Q, S

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Active Power P Watts horizontal] --> nodeOrigin[Origin]
    nodeOrigin --> nodeQ[Reactive Power Q VAR vertical]
    nodeOrigin --> nodeS[Apparent Power S VA hypotenuse]
    nodeP -. base .-> nodeS
    nodeQ -. perpendicular .-> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeAngle[Angle phi between P and S]
    nodeAngle --> nodePF[Power Factor cos phi]
    nodePF --> noteA[0 less than PF less than or equal to 1]
```

---

## 4.6 Frequency Response of Reactances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF[Frequency f Hz] --> nodeXL[Inductive Reactance XL]
    nodeF --> nodeXC[Capacitive Reactance XC]
    nodeXL --> noteXL[XL increases linearly with f]
    nodeXC --> noteXC[XC decreases inversely with f]
    noteXL --> nodeCross[At Resonance XL equals XC]
    noteXC --> nodeCross
    nodeCross --> nodeRes[Resonance Frequency fr]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Inductive Reactance. State its S.I. unit.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Model Q1]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Inductive Reactance** is the opposition offered by a pure inductor to the flow of alternating current. It arises due to the back-EMF induced in the inductor when the current through it changes with time.
>
> **Mathematical Definition:**
>
> $$X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$$
>
> where $\omega$ is the angular frequency (rad/s), $f$ is the supply frequency (Hz), and $L$ is the inductance (Henry, H).
>
> **S.I. Unit:** **Ohm ($\Omega$)** (since reactance, like resistance, has the same dimensional units).
>
> **Key Property:** $X_L$ is **directly proportional** to frequency. At DC ($f = 0$), $X_L = 0$; at very high frequencies, $X_L \to \infty$.

### Question 2: Define Capacitive Reactance. State its S.I. unit.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Model Q2]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Capacitive Reactance** is the opposition offered by a pure capacitor to the flow of alternating current. It arises because the capacitor must be continuously charged and discharged, requiring current to flow even though no conduction occurs through the dielectric.
>
> **Mathematical Definition:**
>
> $$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$$
>
> where $C$ is the capacitance (Farad, F).
>
> **S.I. Unit:** **Ohm ($\Omega$)**.
>
> **Key Property:** $X_C$ is **inversely proportional** to frequency. At DC ($f = 0$), $X_C \to \infty$ (capacitor blocks DC); at very high frequencies, $X_C \to 0$ (capacitor acts as a short circuit).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### 🅰️ CHOICE A: Pure R, L, C Analysis with Waveforms

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Q-A]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the instantaneous current through a pure resistor, pure inductor, and pure capacitor connected individually across an AC source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. In each case, state the phase relationship between voltage and current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pure inductor of $L = 0.2$ H is connected across a $230$ V, $50$ Hz AC supply. Calculate:
  (i) Inductive reactance $X_L$
  (ii) RMS current $I_{rms}$
  (iii) Peak current $I_m$
  (iv) Average power absorbed
  (v) Write the instantaneous equation of current. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**Step 1: Pure Resistor**
Applying Ohm's Law: $i(t) = \frac{v(t)}{R} = \frac{V_m}{R}\sin(\omega t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$

**Phase relationship:** Voltage and current are **in phase** ($\phi = 0^\circ$).

**Step 2: Pure Inductor**
From the inductor equation: $v(t) = L\frac{di}{dt}$

Assume $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. Then $\frac{di}{dt} = I_m \omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.

Substituting: $V_m \sin(\omega t) = \omega L I_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) = \omega L I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi + 90^\circ)$

For equality: $\phi = -90^\circ$ and $V_m = \omega L I_m$

Therefore: $i(t) = \frac{V_m}{\omega L}\sin(\omega t - 90^\circ) = I_m \sin(\omega t - 90^\circ)$

**Phase relationship:** Current **lags** voltage by $90^\circ$ (or voltage leads current by $90^\circ$).

**Step 3: Pure Capacitor**
From the capacitor equation: $i(t) = C\frac{dv}{dt} = C \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t) = \omega C V_m \sin(\omega t + 90^\circ)$

Therefore: $i(t) = \frac{V_m}{1/(\omega C)}\sin(\omega t + 90^\circ) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 90^\circ)$

**Phase relationship:** Current **leads** voltage by $90^\circ$.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $L = 0.2$ H
- $V_{rms} = 230$ V
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi(50) = 314.159$ rad/s

**(i) Inductive Reactance:**
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi(50)(0.2) = 62.83 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) RMS Current:**
$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{X_L} = \frac{230}{62.83} = 3.661 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Peak Current:**
$$I_m = I_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 3.661 \times 1.4142 = 5.177 \, \text{A}$$

**(iv) Average Power Absorbed:**
For a pure inductor, the average power over a complete cycle is **zero**, since it stores and returns energy symmetrically:
$$P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos(90^\circ) = V_{rms} I_{rms} \times 0 = 0 \, \text{W}$$

**(v) Instantaneous Equation of Current:**
Current lags voltage by $90^\circ$, so taking the supply as reference:
$$v(t) = \sqrt{2}(230)\sin(314.159\, t) = 325.27 \sin(314.16\, t) \, \text{V}$$
$$i(t) = 5.177 \sin(314.16\, t - 90^\circ) \, \text{A}$$

---

### 🅱️ CHOICE B: Impedance, Reactance, and Power Factor

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Q-B]**

**(a)** Define the terms (i) Impedance, (ii) Reactance, and (iii) Power Factor. Derive the relationship between impedance, resistance, and reactance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A series R-L circuit has $R = 30 \, \Omega$ and $X_L = 40 \, \Omega$. It is connected to a $200$ V, $50$ Hz AC supply. Calculate:
  (i) Impedance $Z$ and its phase angle $\phi$
  (ii) RMS current $I_{rms}$
  (iii) Active power $P$, Reactive power $Q$, and Apparent power $S$
  (iv) Power factor of the circuit **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)

**(i) Impedance (Z):** The total opposition offered by an AC circuit to the flow of alternating current, comprising both resistance and reactance. It is a complex quantity with magnitude and phase.

**(ii) Reactance (X):** The opposition offered to AC by either a pure inductor (Inductive Reactance, $X_L$) or a pure capacitor (Capacitive Reactance, $X_C$). Net reactance $X = X_L - X_C$.

**(iii) Power Factor (pf):** The cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current, given by $\cos\phi = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$. It indicates the fraction of apparent power that is converted to useful (active) power.

**Derivation of the Relationship:**

For a series R-L-C circuit with supply $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, applying KVL:

$$v(t) = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t) = R i(t) + L\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int i\, dt$$

In phasor form, using $V_L = I \cdot j\omega L = jX_L I$ and $V_C = I \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -jX_C I$:

$$V = I[R + j\omega L - j/(\omega C)] = I[R + j(X_L - X_C)] = I \cdot Z$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z = R + jX = R + j(X_L - X_C)}$$

In polar form:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right)$$

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)

**Given:** $R = 30 \, \Omega$, $X_L = 40 \, \Omega$, $V_{rms} = 200$ V, $f = 50$ Hz

**(i) Impedance and Phase Angle:**

Net reactance: $X = X_L - X_C = 40 - 0 = 40 \, \Omega$ (purely inductive)

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50 \, \Omega$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) = 53.13^\circ \quad \text{(lagging, since inductive)}$$

**(ii) RMS Current:**

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{200}{50} = 4 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Power Calculations:**

Active Power:
$$P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi = 200 \times 4 \times \cos(53.13^\circ) = 200 \times 4 \times 0.6 = 480 \, \text{W}$$

Reactive Power:
$$Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi = 200 \times 4 \times \sin(53.13^\circ) = 200 \times 4 \times 0.8 = 640 \, \text{VAR}$$

Apparent Power:
$$S = V_{rms} I_{rms} = 200 \times 4 = 800 \, \text{VA}$$

**Verification:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2 \Rightarrow 800^2 = 480^2 + 640^2 \Rightarrow 640000 = 230400 + 409600 = 640000$ ✓

**(iv) Power Factor:**

$$\text{pf} = \cos\phi = \cos(53.13^\circ) = 0.6 \quad \text{(lagging)}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing Peak and RMS:** Using $V_m$ directly in $I = V/R$ without converting to RMS or vice-versa. Always check what the problem gives.
> 2. **Wrong Phase Sign:** Writing "voltage lags current in an inductor" — INCORRECT. The correct statement is **"current lags voltage"** in an inductor.
> 3. **Forgetting the √2:** Converting $V_{rms}$ to $V_m$ requires multiplying by $\sqrt{2}$, not by 2. Similarly, $I_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{rms}$.
> 4. **Unit Confusion:** Using Henry (H) for capacitance or Farad (F) for inductance. **Inductance → H, Capacitance → F.**
> 5. **Power Factor Sign:** Always state whether the power factor is **lagging** (inductive) or **leading** (capacitive) — a numerical value of $0.6$ without a sign convention loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing Phasors Wrong:** The reference (along the positive x-axis) should be labeled. Conventionally, $\mathbf{I}$ is the reference for series circuits.
> 7. **Averaging Error:** For half-wave, average value is $V_m/\pi$; for full-wave, it is $2V_m/\pi$. Mixing these up is a frequent error.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three Pure Elements, Three Behaviors:** Resistor (in-phase, dissipates), Inductor (I lags V by 90°, stores magnetic energy), Capacitor (I leads V by 90°, stores electric energy).
- ✅ **Reactance vs. Resistance:** Both measured in Ohms, but reactance depends on frequency and does not dissipate energy.
- ✅ **Frequency Behavior of Reactances:** $X_L \uparrow$ as $f \uparrow$; $X_C \downarrow$ as $f \uparrow$.
- ✅ **DC Limits:** At DC, $X_L = 0$ (short) and $X_C = \infty$ (open).
- ✅ **Impedance Formula:** $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$, magnitude $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$, phase $\phi = \tan^{-1}(X/R)$.
- ✅ **Power Factor:** $\cos\phi = R/\vert Z \vert$. Ranges from 0 to 1.
- ✅ **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$. Units: $P$ in W, $Q$ in VAR, $S$ in VA.
- ✅ **Memory Aid — "ELI the ICE man":** E-L-I (E leads I in L) and I-C-E (I leads E in C).
- ✅ **RMS vs. Peak:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$. Always state which form your equation uses.
- ✅ **KTU Viva Favorites:** Expect questions on deriving $X_L$ and $X_C$, drawing phasor diagrams, and explaining why the average power in a pure L or pure C is zero.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy (60-Second Revision):**
> Memorize the three pairs: **(R, in-phase)**, **(L, I-lags-V, $X_L = \omega L$)**, **(C, I-leads-V, $X_C = 1/(\omega C)$)**. From these, the entire impedance concept follows. Phasor diagrams are scoring — always include them in long answers for 2–3 extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase AC Systems: Representation of Three Phase Voltages, Star and Delta Connections (Balanced Only), Relation Between Line and Phase Voltages, Line and Phase Currents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Three Phase AC Systems: Representation of Three Phase Voltages, Star and Delta Connections (Balanced Only), Relation Between Line and Phase Voltages, Line and Phase Currents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Three-Phase AC System?

A **Three-Phase AC System** is an electrical power generation, transmission, and distribution scheme in which **three sinusoidal voltages** of equal magnitude, equal frequency, and displaced from each other by a phase angle of **120° (2π/3 radians)** are produced simultaneously by a synchronous generator (alternator). It is the global standard for bulk power generation, high-voltage transmission, industrial motor drives, and commercial power distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A polyphase system is said to be a *balanced three-phase system* if the three voltages (or currents) have (i) the same amplitude (RMS value), (ii) the same frequency, and (iii) a mutual phase displacement of exactly **120°** between any two successive phases.

### 1.2 The Three Phases — Naming Convention

The three windings (or conductors) of a three-phase system are designated as:

- **R phase (Red)** — reference phase
- **Y phase (Yellow)** — lags R by 120°
- **B phase (Blue)** — lags Y by 120° (or leads R by 240°)

The **phase sequence (R-Y-B)** is called the *positive sequence*. The reverse sequence (R-B-Y) is the *negative sequence*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Person Pedal Cart"

Imagine three cyclists riding a three-seated pedal cart. Each cyclist pushes the pedal **down** at a different moment in time — exactly **1/3rd of a cycle apart**. While one cyclist is at the peak of their push, the other two are either ramping up or easing down. The result? The cart receives **smooth, continuous torque** with virtually no dead spot.

This is precisely why three-phase systems dominate real engineering:

- A **single-phase** system pushes power in pulses (falling to zero twice per cycle).
- A **two-phase** system (now obsolete) is better, but still not smooth.
- A **three-phase** system delivers **constant instantaneous power** in a balanced load — no pulsation, smoother motors, smaller conductors, and higher efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The total instantaneous power in a balanced three-phase system is *constant* (non-pulsating). This is the single most important engineering advantage that makes three-phase indispensable for running heavy industrial motors and feeding the national grid.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Frequencies:** **50 Hz** (India, Europe, most of Asia/Africa) and **60 Hz** (USA, Japan, parts of Americas).
- **Phase Displacement:** Exactly **120° electrical** between consecutive phases.
- **Standard Indian LV Supply:** **415 V (Line-to-Line)** at **50 Hz** for three-phase; **230 V** for single-phase (derived from line-to-neutral).
- **Phase Sequence Indicator:** A device that determines R-Y-B vs R-B-Y order before connecting heavy motors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representation of Three-Phase Voltages

Assuming the **R-phase** as the reference (zero phase angle), the instantaneous voltage equations for a balanced three-phase system of peak value **V_m** and angular frequency **ω = 2πf** are:

$$
v_R(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
v_Y(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) = V_m \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right)
$$

$$
v_B(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) = V_m \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{4\pi}{3}\right)
$$

### Phasor Representation (RMS Form)

Using RMS phasors with **V** as the RMS phase voltage magnitude:

$$
\bar{V}_R = V \angle 0°
$$

$$
\bar{V}_Y = V \angle -120°
$$

$$
\bar{V}_B = V \angle -240° = V \angle +120°
$$

### Fundamental Property — Phasor Sum is Zero

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any **balanced** three-phase system, the vector sum of the three phase voltages (or currents) is **always zero**. This is a direct consequence of the 120° symmetry.

$$
\bar{V}_R + \bar{V}_Y + \bar{V}_B = 0
$$

## 2.2 Two Standard Connection Schemes

### 2.2.1 Star (Y) Connection

- The **similar (start) terminals** of all three phases are joined at a common point called the **Neutral (N)**.
- The other three terminals (R, Y, B) are brought out as **line conductors**.
- A **neutral wire** may or may not be brought out (4-wire vs 3-wire star).

### 2.2.2 Delta (Δ) Connection

- The **end of one phase** is connected to the **start of the next phase** (R-Y, Y-B, B-R), forming a closed loop resembling the Greek letter Δ.
- **No neutral point** exists.
- The line conductors are taken from the three junction nodes (R, Y, B).

## 2.3 Line vs Phase — Defining the Quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase quantity** = voltage/current associated with a *single phase winding* (between the line and neutral in star, or across one winding in delta).
>
> **Line quantity** = voltage/current in the *external line conductor* connecting the source to the load.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Star (Y) Connection | Delta (Δ) Connection |
|---|----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Relation between **Line Voltage (V_L)** and **Phase Voltage (V_Ph)** | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{Ph}$ | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ |
| 2 | Relation between **Line Current (I_L)** and **Phase Current (I_Ph)** | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}$ |
| 3 | Total Active Power **P** (Balanced) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| 4 | Total Reactive Power **Q** | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ |
| 5 | Apparent Power **S** | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ |
| 6 | Neutral Current (Balanced) | $I_N = 0$ (theoretically) | **Not Applicable** (no neutral) |
| 7 | Number of Conductors | 3 or 4 (with neutral) | 3 only |

**Where:**
- $V_L$ = RMS Line Voltage (volts)
- $V_{Ph}$ = RMS Phase Voltage (volts)
- $I_L$ = RMS Line Current (amperes)
- $I_{Ph}$ = RMS Phase Current (amperes)
- $\phi$ = Phase angle between phase voltage and phase current (load-dependent)
- $\cos\phi$ = Power factor of the load

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Generation:** All alternators in power stations (NTPC, KSEB, etc.) generate three-phase power.
- **Transmission:** Three-phase overhead lines (with 6 conductors for double circuit) transmit power over hundreds of kilometers with minimal losses.
- **Industrial Loads:** Three-phase induction motors (3-phase IM) are self-starting, robust, and form the workhorse of industry.
- **Commercial Distribution:** Office buildings receive 415 V three-phase; the neutral gives 230 V single-phase for lighting and sockets.
- **Domestic Single-Phase:** A single household receives *one phase + neutral* from the three-phase distribution transformer — this is why power cuts are sometimes localized to particular phases in your locality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{Ph}$ in Star Connection

Consider a balanced star-connected system with the neutral at the origin. The phase voltage phasors (each equal in magnitude $V_{Ph}$) are:

$$
\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph} \angle 0°
$$

$$
\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph} \angle -120°
$$

$$
\bar{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph} \angle +120°
$$

The **line voltage** between line R and line Y is the potential difference between those two line terminals:

$$
\bar{V}_{RY} = \bar{V}_{RN} - \bar{V}_{YN}
$$

Substituting the phasor values:

$$
\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph} \angle 0° - V_{Ph} \angle -120°
$$

Express each in rectangular form:

$$
\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph}(1 + j0) = V_{Ph}
$$

$$
\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph}\left[\cos(-120°) + j\sin(-120°)\right] = V_{Ph}\left[-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph} - V_{Ph}\left[-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]
$$

$$
\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left[1 + \frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] = V_{Ph}\left[\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]
$$

The magnitude is:

$$
\vert \bar{V}_{RY} \vert = V_{Ph} \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}
$$

Expanding the squares:

$$
= V_{Ph} \sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = V_{Ph} \sqrt{\frac{12}{4}} = V_{Ph}\sqrt{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{Ph}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The line voltage is **√3 times the phase voltage** and **leads the phase voltage (V_RN) by 30°** in a star connection. This 30° phase shift is critical when performing phasor analysis for power calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph}$ in Delta Connection

In a delta connection, the **line conductors are connected directly to the load phase terminals**. So the *phase voltage equals the line voltage*:

$$
\boxed{V_L = V_{Ph}}
$$

Now consider the currents at node R of the delta. The **phase current** flowing from R to Y (let's call it $\bar{I}_{RY}$) and the phase current flowing from B to R ($\bar{I}_{BR}$) meet at node R. The **line current** $\bar{I}_R$ leaving the node must equal the algebraic sum of these (KCL):

$$
\bar{I}_R = \bar{I}_{RY} - \bar{I}_{BR}
$$

Assume a balanced inductive load with phase current $I_{Ph}$ lagging the phase voltage by angle $\phi$:

$$
\bar{I}_{RY} = I_{Ph} \angle -\phi \quad \text{(reference: voltage } V_{RY} \angle 0°)
$$

$$
\bar{I}_{BR} = I_{Ph} \angle (-\phi - 120°)
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph} \angle -\phi - I_{Ph} \angle (-\phi - 120°)
$$

Factor out $I_{Ph} \angle (-\phi - 60°)$ using phasor arithmetic:

$$
\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph} \angle (-\phi - 60°) \cdot \left[\angle 60° - \angle(-60°)\right]
$$

Using Euler's identity $\angle 60° - \angle(-60°) = j2\sin 60° = j\sqrt{3}$:

$$
\bar{I}_R = I_{Ph} \angle (-\phi - 60°) \cdot \sqrt{3} \angle 90°
$$

The magnitude:

$$
\vert \bar{I}_R \vert = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{Ph}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In a delta connection, the **line current is √3 times the phase current** and **lags the corresponding phase current by 30°**. Note that this 30° shift is in the *opposite direction* to the star voltage shift.

## 3.3 Verification: Star Current Relationship ($I_L = I_{Ph}$)

In a star connection, the **line conductor IS the phase conductor** — the current flowing in the line wire R is the same current flowing through phase winding R-N. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{I_L = I_{Ph}}
$$

## 3.4 Verification: Delta Voltage Relationship ($V_L = V_{Ph}$)

In a delta connection, the line terminals R, Y, B are the same physical nodes as the phase winding terminals. Hence the voltage measured between any two lines is identical to the voltage across the phase winding connected between those two lines. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_L = V_{Ph}}
$$

## 3.5 Total Power in a Balanced Three-Phase System

The total active power is the sum of the power in each phase. For a balanced load, each phase delivers the same power:

$$
P_{total} = 3 \cdot V_{Ph} \cdot I_{Ph} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**For Star connection** (substitute $V_{Ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ and $I_{Ph} = I_L$):

$$
P_{total} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi
$$

**For Delta connection** (substitute $V_{Ph} = V_L$ and $I_{Ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$):

$$
P_{total} = 3 \cdot V_L \cdot \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful Result:** The total power expression is **identical** for both star and delta — only the *internal* phase currents and voltages differ. This is why the formula $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is universal for all balanced three-phase systems.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A balanced three-phase star-connected load draws a line current of 10 A from a 415 V, 50 Hz supply. The load phase angle is 30° lagging. Find:
(i) Phase voltage, (ii) Phase current, (iii) Total active power, (iv) Total reactive power, (v) Apparent power.

**Solution:**

(i) **Phase voltage** in star:

$$
V_{Ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 239.6 \text{ V}
$$

(ii) **Phase current** in star = Line current:

$$
I_{Ph} = I_L = 10 \text{ A}
$$

(iii) **Total active power** (with $\cos\phi = \cos 30° = 0.866$):

$$
P = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.866 \approx 6230.5 \text{ W} \approx 6.23 \text{ kW}
$$

(iv) **Total reactive power** (with $\sin\phi = \sin 30° = 0.5$):

$$
Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.5 \approx 3594.0 \text{ VAR} \approx 3.59 \text{ kVAR}
$$

(v) **Apparent power**:

$$
S = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 10 \approx 7188.0 \text{ VA} \approx 7.19 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Verification:** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{6230.5^2 + 3594.0^2} \approx 7188$ VA ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a *balanced three-phase system*. State the conditions for a three-phase supply to be balanced. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A three-phase system is said to be **balanced** when the three phase voltages (and consequently the three phase currents for a balanced load) satisfy the following three conditions simultaneously:

1. **Equal Magnitude:** All three phase voltages have the same RMS value, i.e., $V_R = V_Y = V_B = V_{Ph}$.
2. **Equal Frequency:** All three voltages have the same frequency $f$ (and hence same angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$).
3. **Mutual Phase Displacement of 120°:** Each phase is displaced from the other by exactly **120° electrical** (or $2\pi/3$ radians). For a positive (R-Y-B) sequence: $V_Y$ lags $V_R$ by 120°, and $V_B$ lags $V_Y$ by 120°.

A direct mathematical consequence is that the phasor sum is zero: $\bar{V}_R + \bar{V}_Y + \bar{V}_B = 0$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Compare star and delta connections for a balanced three-phase load in terms of line and phase quantities. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Star (Y) | Delta (Δ) |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| Line Voltage vs Phase Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \, V_{Ph}$ | $V_L = V_{Ph}$ |
| Line Current vs Phase Current | $I_L = I_{Ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \, I_{Ph}$ |
| Neutral Point | Available (4-wire possible) | Not available |
| Total Power | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ |
| Typical Use | Distribution, long-distance transmission | Motor windings, high-current loads |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> Each question carries **14 marks** and has an internal choice between **Option A** and **Option B**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Star Connection Deep Dive

> **A(a)** For a balanced star-connected load of impedance $Z = (8 + j6)\,\Omega$ per phase, supplied from a three-phase 415 V, 50 Hz source, calculate:
> (i) Phase voltage and phase current,
> (ii) Line current,
> (iii) Power factor, total active power, total reactive power, and apparent power.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1 — Phase voltage in star:**

$$
V_{Ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of load impedance:**

$$
\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Phase angle (load angle):**

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6}{8}\right) = 36.87°
$$

**Step 4 — Phase current (= Line current in star):**

$$
I_{Ph} = I_L = \frac{V_{Ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{239.6}{10} = 23.96 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Power factor:**

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 \text{ (lagging, inductive load)}
$$

**Step 6 — Total active power:**

$$
P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 23.96 \times 0.8
$$

$$
P \approx 1.732 \times 415 \times 23.96 \times 0.8 \approx 13776 \text{ W} \approx 13.78 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Total reactive power ($\sin\phi = 6/10 = 0.6$):**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi = 1.732 \times 415 \times 23.96 \times 0.6 \approx 10332 \text{ VAR} \approx 10.33 \text{ kVAR}
$$

**Step 8 — Apparent power:**

$$
S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = 1.732 \times 415 \times 23.96 \approx 17220 \text{ VA} \approx 17.22 \text{ kVA}
$$

**Verification:** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{13776^2 + 10332^2} \approx 17220$ VA ✓

---

> **A(b)** Derive the relationship between line voltage and phase voltage in a balanced star-connected system. Show that the line voltage leads the phase voltage by 30°. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for A(b):**

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. Express the three phase voltage phasors (taking $\bar{V}_{RN}$ as reference):
   $\bar{V}_{RN} = V_{Ph}\angle 0°$, $\bar{V}_{YN} = V_{Ph}\angle -120°$, $\bar{V}_{BN} = V_{Ph}\angle +120°$.

2. The line voltage $\bar{V}_{RY} = \bar{V}_{RN} - \bar{V}_{YN}$.

3. Substitute rectangular forms and simplify:
   $\bar{V}_{RY} = V_{Ph}\left[\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right]$.

4. Compute magnitude: $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{Ph}$.

5. Compute phase angle: $\angle V_{RY} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{3/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) = +30°$.

**Final Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{Ph} \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{V}_{RY} \text{ leads } \bar{V}_{RN} \text{ by } 30°}
$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Delta Connection & Power Deep Dive

> **B(a)** A balanced delta-connected load has an impedance of $Z = (30 + j40)\,\Omega$ per phase. The line voltage is 400 V, 50 Hz. Find:
> (i) Phase voltage and phase current,
> (ii) Line current,
> (iii) Total power consumed.
> **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1 — Phase voltage (= Line voltage in delta):**

$$
V_{Ph} = V_L = 400 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of load impedance:**

$$
\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Phase current:**

$$
I_{Ph} = \frac{V_{Ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Line current in delta:**

$$
I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 8 = 13.86 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Phase angle:**

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) = 53.13°
$$

**Step 6 — Power factor:**

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{30}{50} = 0.6 \text{ (lagging)}
$$

**Step 7 — Total active power:**

$$
P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = 1.732 \times 400 \times 13.86 \times 0.6
$$

$$
P \approx 5760 \text{ W} = 5.76 \text{ kW}
$$

---

> **B(b)** With reference to a balanced three-phase delta-connected load, derive the relationship $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph}$. State the phase angle relationship between line current and the corresponding phase current. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for B(b):**

Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.2** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. At any node of the delta (say R), apply KCL: $\bar{I}_R = \bar{I}_{RY} - \bar{I}_{BR}$.

2. With $\bar{I}_{RY} = I_{Ph}\angle -\phi$ and $\bar{I}_{BR} = I_{Ph}\angle(-\phi - 120°)$:

3. Factor and simplify using $\angle 60° - \angle(-60°) = j\sqrt{3}$:

4. The magnitude gives $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph}$.

5. The argument gives the line current **lags** the phase current by 30°.

**Final Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph} \quad \text{and} \quad \bar{I}_R \text{ lags } \bar{I}_{RY} \text{ by } 30°}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up V_L and V_Ph:** The biggest trap. In a *star* connection, the **line voltage is √3 times the phase voltage**, NOT the other way around. In *delta*, **V_L = V_Ph**. Confusing these is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Mixing up I_L and I_Ph:** Equally dangerous. In *star*, **I_L = I_Ph**. In *delta*, **I_L = √3 × I_Ph**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 30° phase shift:** Many students write the magnitude relations but forget the 30° lead/lag. KTU examiners *love* asking for the phase angle relationship. Marks are awarded for the angle too.
>
> 4. **Power factor angle φ vs phase sequence angle 120°:** The 120° is the angle *between phases of the supply*. The φ is the angle *between phase voltage and phase current* of the load. They are completely different and must NEVER be confused.
>
> 5. **Writing √3 as 1.732 without units:** Always carry units (V, A, W, VAR, VA) in every step. Examiner's mark schemes explicitly check for unit consistency.
>
> 6. **Forgetting "Balanced" in the problem statement:** If the question says "balanced" load, you may use the symmetric formula $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ directly. If it says "unbalanced," you must compute phase-by-phase and sum — this is **out of KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus**, but examiners sometimes test if you notice the word "balanced."

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Print this section. Revise before every KTU exam.**

✅ **Three-phase supply:** Three sinusoidal voltages, equal magnitude, equal frequency, **120° apart**.

✅ **Phasor form:** $\bar{V}_R = V\angle 0°$, $\bar{V}_Y = V\angle -120°$, $\bar{V}_B = V\angle +120°$.

✅ **Balanced system condition:** $\bar{V}_R + \bar{V}_Y + \bar{V}_B = 0$.

✅ **Star (Y) — memorize cold:**
   - $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{Ph}$
   - $I_L = I_{Ph}$
   - **V_L leads V_Ph by 30°**
   - Neutral current = 0 (balanced)

✅ **Delta (Δ) — memorize cold:**
   - $V_L = V_{Ph}$
   - $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{Ph}$
   - **I_L lags I_Ph by 30°**
   - No neutral — always 3-wire

✅ **Total Power (Balanced, both connections):**
   - Active: $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ (Watts)
   - Reactive: $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ (VAR)
   - Apparent: $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ (VA)
   - Power Triangle: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$

✅ **Standard Indian values:** 415 V (line), 239.6 V (phase in star), 50 Hz.

✅ **Standard conversion trick:** $\frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6 \approx 240$ V (used constantly in problems).

✅ **Phase angle φ:** Always measured from **phase voltage to phase current** of the *same phase*, lagging for inductive (R-L) loads.

✅ **Unit vigilance:** V_L in volts, I_L in amperes, P in watts, Q in VAR, S in VA — never mix up.

✅ **Common KTU trick:** "A 415 V, 3-phase motor draws 10 A at 0.8 pf lagging" — you should *immediately* know they mean **line** voltage and **line** current. The phase values are derived from there.

✅ **Real-world links:** NTPC generating stations (generation), 400 kV/220 kV transmission lines (transmission), 11 kV/415 V distribution transformers (distribution to homes/industries), three-phase induction motors in factories (utilization).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power in AC Circuits: Power Factor, Active, Reactive and Apparent Power in Single Phase and Three Phase Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power in AC Circuits: Power Factor, Active, Reactive and Apparent Power in Single Phase and Three Phase Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Power in AC Circuits — The Formal Definition

In an **Alternating Current (AC) circuit**, the voltage and current vary sinusoidally with time. Because of this time-varying nature, the concept of electrical power is far richer than in DC circuits. A single quantity called "power" splits into **three distinct components**, each with its own physical meaning, unit, and engineering consequence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> In a sinusoidal AC circuit supplied by $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and drawing a current $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, the **instantaneous power** is $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$, which yields three averaged engineering quantities: **Active Power (P)**, **Reactive Power (Q)**, and **Apparent Power (S)**.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Nature |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Active (Real) Power | $P$ | **Watt (W)** | Useful power that does real work (heat, light, motion) |
| Reactive Power | $Q$ | **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)** | Power that oscillates between source and load (no net work) |
| Apparent Power | $S$ | **Volt-Ampere (VA)** | Total power supplied by the source (vector sum of P and Q) |

## 1.2 The "Beer Mug" Intuitive Analogy 🍺

Imagine you order a mug of beer in a glass:

- **The Beer itself** = **Active Power (P)** — this is what you actually wanted. It does the "real" job of quenching your thirst. In an electrical circuit, P is the power dissipated in resistors, or converted into mechanical work in motors.
- **The Foam on top** = **Reactive Power (Q)** — it looks like beer, fills the mug, but you cannot drink it. It just sits there and has to be carried around. In circuits, Q oscillates back and forth between the inductor/capacitor and the source — it does *no* net work but is *essential* for the magnetic/electric fields to exist.
- **The Full Mug (Beer + Foam)** = **Apparent Power (S)** — the *total volume* the bartender had to pour and the glass had to carry. The utility company must size its wires and transformers to handle the *entire* mug, not just the beer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?**
> A poor **Power Factor** (more foam, less beer) means you are paying the bartender to transport useless foam. Electricity boards in India (KSEB, for instance) levy a *penalty* on industrial consumers whose power factor falls below **0.9 lagging**.

## 1.3 Power Factor — The "Usefulness" Ratio

The **Power Factor (pf)** is the cosine of the angle by which the current *leads or lags* the voltage in an AC circuit. It is the single most important industrial AC quantity after voltage and current.

$$pf = \cos(\phi) = \frac{P}{S}$$

- **Unity Power Factor (pf = 1)**: Purely resistive load (no foam at all — only beer).
- **Lagging Power Factor**: Inductive load (motor, transformer) — current *lags* voltage. Most industrial loads.
- **Leading Power Factor**: Capacitive load (capacitor banks) — current *leads* voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If a question simply says "power factor", you **must** also state whether it is **lagging** or **leading**. A bare numerical value will lose you at least 1 mark in the ESE.

## 1.4 Single-Phase vs Three-Phase — Why It Matters

- **Single-Phase**: A typical household supply (230 V, 50 Hz in India). Power is delivered through two wires (Phase + Neutral).
- **Three-Phase**: The industrial standard (415 V, 50 Hz). Power is delivered through three phases (R, Y, B) plus a neutral. Three-phase systems deliver **constant (pulsation-free) instantaneous power** and use conductors far more economically than single-phase for the same total power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Instantaneous Power Breakdown

Let the AC voltage and current be:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

The instantaneous power is:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m I_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\phi) - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$$

This reveals two distinct parts:

- A **constant (DC) component** = $V_m I_m \cos(\phi) / 2$ → This is **Active Power (P)**.
- A **double-frequency oscillating component** → This is the **Reactive Power oscillation** associated with $Q$.

## 2.2 Why Three Distinct Quantities Exist

The vector (phasor) addition of $P$ and $Q$ gives $S$ because **$P$ and $Q$ are 90° out of phase in nature** (one does work, the other stores/releases energy). This is the **Power Triangle**.

## 2.3 The Power Triangle — The Visual Truth

```
              /|
             / |
        S  /  |  Q
          /   |    (VAR)
         /    |
        / φ   |
       /______|
          P
         (W)
```

- **Hypotenuse** = $S$ (Apparent Power, VA)
- **Adjacent (Horizontal)** = $P$ (Active Power, W)
- **Opposite (Vertical)** = $Q$ (Reactive Power, VAR)
- **Angle at base** = $\phi$ (Phase angle between V and I)

The fundamental relationship:

$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** $P$, $Q$, and $S$ form a **right-angled triangle**. You can always find the third side if you know any two, using the Pythagorean theorem. Examiners love this 2-mark quick question.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR THIS TABLE:** The vertical bar `\vert` has been used in place of the pipe symbol `$\vert$` to preserve markdown table integrity. In your written exam, write the absolute value using standard notation.

### Table 2.A — Single-Phase Power Formulas

| Quantity | Formula (RMS) | Formula (Peak) | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Active Power (P) | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | $P = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos\phi$ | **W (Watt)** |
| Reactive Power (Q) | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ | $Q = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \sin\phi$ | **VAR** |
| Apparent Power (S) | $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ | $S = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$ | **VA** |
| Power Factor | $pf = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S}$ | $pf = \frac{P}{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| Power Triangle Hyp. | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | VA |
| Phasor Angle | $\phi = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{P}{S}\right)$ | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Q}{P}\right)$ | rad or deg |

### Table 2.B — Three-Phase Power Formulas

| Quantity | Star (Y) Connection | Delta (Δ) Connection | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Line Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ | $V_L = V_{ph}$ | V |
| Line Current | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot I_{ph}$ | A |
| Total Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | **W** |
| Total Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | **VAR** |
| Total Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$ | $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$ | **VA** |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Three-Phase Rule:** The $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L$ form is **universal** — it works for BOTH Star and Delta connections. The line/phase conversion just helps you compute $V_L$ and $I_L$ from the load's rated values.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electricity Billing:** Industrial consumers are billed in **kVA** (or **kVAh** for apparent energy) but penalized for low $pf$. Improving $pf$ from 0.7 to 0.95 can reduce a factory's electricity bill by 10–15%.
- **Transformer & Cable Sizing:** A transformer rated 100 kVA can deliver 100 kW *only* at $pf=1$. At $pf=0.8$, it delivers only 80 kW — the rest is unusable reactive current that still heats the windings.
- **Generator Design:** Three-phase generators are the backbone of the global power grid because they deliver constant torque (no pulsations), use less copper per kW transmitted, and self-start the rotating magnetic field.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Single-Phase Active, Reactive, and Apparent Power

### Starting Point

Let the source voltage and the resulting current be:
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $\phi$ is the phase angle by which the current lags the voltage (positive $\phi$ for inductive loads).

### Step 1: Compute Instantaneous Power

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

Substituting:
$$p(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Product-to-Sum Identity

The trigonometric identity used is:
$$\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A - B) - \cos(A + B)\right]$$

Letting $A = \omega t$ and $B = \omega t - \phi$:

$$A - B = \omega t - (\omega t - \phi) = \phi$$
$$A + B = \omega t + \omega t - \phi = 2\omega t - \phi$$

Therefore:
$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\phi) - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 3: Identify the Average (Active) Power

The first term $\frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\phi)$ is **constant** (does not depend on time $t$). The second term $\frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ is a cosine wave at frequency $2\omega$, which has an average value of **zero over a full cycle**.

Hence the average power over one complete cycle is:
$$P = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\phi)$$

### Step 4: Convert to RMS Values

Using $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} I_{rms}}{2} = V_{rms} I_{rms}$$

Thus the **Active Power** is:
$$P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos(\phi) \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

### Step 5: Identify the Reactive Power

The peak amplitude of the oscillating component in $p(t)$ is $\frac{V_m I_m}{2}$. This is defined as the **Reactive Power** (with the sine term to differentiate it from the cosine-based P):

$$Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin(\phi) \quad \text{[VAR]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of Q:** This represents the peak rate of energy exchange between the source and the reactive elements (L and C). It never contributes to net energy delivered — it just "shuttles" back and forth at twice the supply frequency.

### Step 6: Identify the Apparent Power

The **Apparent Power** is defined as the product of the RMS values of voltage and current, with no phase factor. It represents the magnitude of the complex power $S = P + jQ$:

$$S = V_{rms} I_{rms} \quad \text{[VA]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Triangle Relationship

Starting from the definitions:
$$P = S \cos\phi$$
$$Q = S \sin\phi$$

Squaring and adding:
$$P^2 + Q^2 = S^2 \cos^2\phi + S^2 \sin^2\phi$$

$$P^2 + Q^2 = S^2 (\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi)$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$:

$$P^2 + Q^2 = S^2$$

$$\boxed{S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Three-Phase Active Power

### Balanced Three-Phase System

A balanced three-phase system has three sinusoidal voltages, each displaced by $120°$ in time. The three phase voltages are:

$$v_R(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$v_Y(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$v_B(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

The corresponding line currents (assuming a balanced Y-connected load with impedance $Z$ per phase and phase angle $\phi$):

$$i_R(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$
$$i_Y(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120° - \phi)$$
$$i_B(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 120° - \phi)$$

### Total Instantaneous Power

The total instantaneous three-phase power is:
$$p_{3\phi}(t) = v_R i_R + v_Y i_Y + v_B i_B$$

### Step 1: Expand Each Phase Power

Each phase power has the same form derived in Section 3.1:
$$p_R(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$$
$$p_Y(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t - 240° - \phi)$$
$$p_B(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t + 240° - \phi)$$

### Step 2: Sum the Three Phases

Summing the constant terms:
$$3 \times \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi = \frac{3 V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi$$

### Step 3: Sum the Oscillating Terms

The three cosine terms are:
$$\cos(2\omega t - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t - 240° - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t + 240° - \phi)$$

Let $\theta = 2\omega t - \phi$. Then the sum is:
$$\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 240°) + \cos(\theta + 240°)$$

Using the identity $\cos A + \cos B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$ on the first and third terms:

$$\cos(\theta + 240°) + \cos(\theta - 240°) = 2\cos\theta \cos(240°) = 2\cos\theta \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\cos\theta$$

Adding the middle term:
$$-\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 240°)$$

Using the identity again:
$$-\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 240°) = -2\sin\left(\frac{\theta + \theta - 240°}{2}\right)\sin\left(\frac{\theta - \theta + 240°}{2}\right)$$
$$= -2\sin(\theta - 120°)\sin(120°) = -2\sin(\theta - 120°) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$
$$= -\sqrt{3}\sin(\theta - 120°)$$

This is a sinusoidal function of $\theta = 2\omega t - \phi$, which has an **average value of zero** over a complete cycle. Therefore, the entire oscillating part of $p_{3\phi}(t)$ averages to zero.

### Step 4: Total Average Three-Phase Power

$$\boxed{P_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos\phi = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi}$$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Line Quantities (Star Connection)

For a **Star (Y) connection**:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}, \quad I_{ph} = I_L$$

Substituting:
$$P_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

### Step 6: Verify for Delta Connection

For a **Delta (Δ) connection**:
$$V_{ph} = V_L, \quad I_{ph} = \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting:
$$P_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot V_L \cdot \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

**Both give the same universal result.** The reactive and apparent power follow identical logic:

$$\boxed{Q_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi \quad \text{[VAR]}}$$

$$\boxed{S_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \quad \text{[VA]}}$$

## 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Loads and Their Signatures

| Load Type | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Power Factor | P (W) | Q (VAR) | S (VA) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pure Resistor (R) | $0°$ | $1.0$ (unity) | $V I$ | $0$ | $V I$ |
| Pure Inductor (L) | $+90°$ | $0$ (lagging) | $0$ | $V I$ | $V I$ |
| Pure Capacitor (C) | $-90°$ | $0$ (leading) | $0$ | $-V I$ | $V I$ |
| R-L Series | $0° < \phi < 90°$ | $0 < pf < 1$ (lag) | $V I \cos\phi$ | $+V I \sin\phi$ | $V I$ |
| R-C Series | $-90° < \phi < 0°$ | $0 < pf < 1$ (lead) | $V I \cos\phi$ | $-V I \sin\phi$ | $V I$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign of Q** tells you the nature of the load: **+Q → Inductive (lagging)**, **−Q → Capacitive (leading)**. This is a standard KTU examination question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The most common mistakes students make in this topic (losing 2–4 marks per error):
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in three-phase power formulas. The 230 V household is single-phase; the 415 V industrial is three-phase — both use the *same* $V_L$ value, but the formula changes.
> 2. **Mixing up Star and Delta relationships** between line and phase values. KTU specifically tests $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ (Star) vs $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ (Delta).
> 3. **Omitting the lagging/leading designation** on power factor values. A bare $pf = 0.8$ is incomplete.
> 4. **Using peak values ($V_m$, $I_m$) in place of RMS values** without the factor of $1/2$ or $1/\sqrt{2}$ correction. Always read the question carefully.
> 5. **Confusing units**: $P$ is in W (or kW), $Q$ in VAR (or kVAR), $S$ in VA (or kVA). They are NOT interchangeable.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define Active Power, Reactive Power, and Apparent Power in a single-phase AC circuit. State their SI units and the relationship among them.

**Model Answer:**

In a single-phase AC circuit supplied by $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ drawing $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$:

- **Active Power (P):** The average value of instantaneous power over one complete cycle. It represents the actual power consumed by the load to do useful work (heating, lighting, mechanical motion). $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$. Unit: **Watt (W)**.

- **Reactive Power (Q):** The peak value of the oscillating component of instantaneous power. It represents the rate of energy exchange between the source and the reactive elements (L and C) of the load. $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$. Unit: **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)**.

- **Apparent Power (S):** The product of RMS voltage and RMS current, irrespective of phase angle. It represents the total power that the source must supply. $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$. Unit: **Volt-Ampere (VA)**.

**Relationship:** They form a right-angled triangle known as the **Power Triangle**:

$$S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$$

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for each definition + unit, 0 marks for relationship, bonus implied for the formula]**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
What is Power Factor? Why is a low power factor considered undesirable in industrial installations?

**Model Answer:**

**Power Factor (pf):** It is the cosine of the phase angle between the voltage and current in an AC circuit.

$$pf = \cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}$$

**Why low power factor is undesirable:**

1. **Increased current draw:** For the same active power $P$, a lower $pf$ means a larger current $I = P / (V \cos\phi)$. This causes higher $I^2R$ losses in transmission lines and cables.
2. **Larger conductor size required:** To handle the higher current, thicker and more expensive copper conductors are needed.
3. **Reduced capacity of transformers and generators:** A 100 kVA transformer can deliver only 100 kW at $pf=1$, but only 80 kW at $pf=0.8$, effectively wasting 20% of its capacity.
4. **Penalty from electricity board:** Utilities like KSEB levy a power factor penalty on industrial consumers with $pf < 0.9$.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 mark for definition + formula, 2 marks for any two valid reasons]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Regular Exam]**

**A (a).** A single-phase motor operating at 230 V, 50 Hz draws a current of 10 A at a power factor of 0.7 lagging. Calculate:
  (i) Active Power consumed
  (ii) Reactive Power drawn
  (iii) Apparent Power
  (iv) Phase angle between V and I
  (v) The capacitance required to improve the power factor to 0.95 lagging.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $I = 10$ A, $pf_1 = \cos\phi_1 = 0.7$ lagging.

**(i) Active Power P:**

$$P = V I \cos\phi_1 = 230 \times 10 \times 0.7$$
$$P = 1610 \text{ W} = 1.61 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Reactive Power Q₁:**

First compute $\sin\phi_1 = \sqrt{1 - 0.7^2} = \sqrt{0.51} = 0.7141$

$$Q_1 = V I \sin\phi_1 = 230 \times 10 \times 0.7141$$
$$Q_1 = 1642.5 \text{ VAR} = 1.6425 \text{ kVAR (lagging)}$$

**(iii) Apparent Power S:**

$$S = V I = 230 \times 10 = 2300 \text{ VA} = 2.3 \text{ kVA}$$

**Verification:** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q_1^2} = \sqrt{1610^2 + 1642.5^2} = \sqrt{2592100 + 2697806.25} = \sqrt{5289906.25} \approx 2300$ VA ✓

**(iv) Phase angle φ₁:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.7) = 45.57°$$

**(v) Capacitance for Power Factor Correction:**

Target power factor: $pf_2 = 0.95$ lagging, so $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$.

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$$
$$\sin\phi_2 = \sqrt{1 - 0.95^2} = \sqrt{0.0975} = 0.3122$$

Active power $P$ remains constant = 1610 W.

Reactive power after correction:
$$Q_2 = P \tan\phi_2 = 1610 \times \tan(18.19°) = 1610 \times 0.3287 = 529.2 \text{ VAR}$$

Reactive power to be supplied by the capacitor:
$$Q_C = Q_1 - Q_2 = 1642.5 - 529.2 = 1113.3 \text{ VAR}$$

The capacitor must supply this much reactive power:
$$Q_C = \frac{V^2}{X_C} = V^2 \cdot \omega C = V^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

$$C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V^2} = \frac{1113.3}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 230^2}$$

$$C = \frac{1113.3}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 52900} = \frac{1113.3}{16,620,160}$$

$$\boxed{C = 67.0 \text{ μF}}$$

> **[7 Marks Breakdown: (i) 1 mark, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 1 mark, (iv) 1 mark, (v) 2.5 marks]**

---

**A (b).** Derive the expression for total active power in a balanced three-phase AC system. Show that $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ for both Star and Delta connections.

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. Write the three phase voltages displaced by 120° (1 mark)
2. Write the three phase currents displaced by 120° with the phase angle $\phi$ (1 mark)
3. Write the expression for total instantaneous power (1 mark)
4. Expand using the product-to-sum identity (1 mark)
5. Sum all three phases and show the oscillating components sum to zero (1.5 marks)
6. Express $P = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$ (0.5 mark)
7. Substitute Star and Delta relationships separately to show $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ in both cases (1 mark)

> *Note: The full derivation is provided in Section 3.3 of these notes. Students should reproduce the derivation showing the cancellation of oscillating terms — this is the step most commonly skipped, costing 2–3 marks.*

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**B (a).** A balanced three-phase, star-connected load draws a line current of 20 A from a 400 V, 50 Hz three-phase supply. The load phase angle is 30° lagging.
  (i) Find the phase voltage and phase current.
  (ii) Calculate the total Active, Reactive, and Apparent Power.
  (iii) Find the value of the delta-connected capacitor bank (per phase) required to improve the power factor to unity.
  (iv) Draw the power triangle before and after correction.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Star-connected balanced load, $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 20$ A, $\phi = 30°$ lagging, $f = 50$ Hz.

**(i) Phase Voltage and Phase Current:**

For a Star connection:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$
$$I_{ph} = I_L = 20 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Total Active, Reactive, and Apparent Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 \times \cos(30°)$$
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 \times 0.866 = 12,000 \text{ W} = 12 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 \times \sin(30°)$$
$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 \times 0.5 = 6928.2 \text{ VAR} \approx 6.93 \text{ kVAR}$$

$$S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 20 = 13,856.4 \text{ VA} \approx 13.86 \text{ kVA}$$

**Verification:** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{12000^2 + 6928.2^2} = \sqrt{144,000,000 + 48,000,000} = \sqrt{192,000,000} \approx 13,856$ VA ✓

**(iii) Delta-Connected Capacitor Bank for Unity Power Factor:**

To improve $pf$ to unity ($\phi = 0°$), the entire reactive power $Q$ must be supplied by the capacitor bank.

Total reactive power to be supplied: $Q_C = 6.93$ kVAR = 6928.2 VAR.

For a delta-connected bank, each capacitor experiences the line voltage $V_L = 400$ V.

Reactive power per phase:
$$Q_{C,ph} = \frac{Q_C}{3} = \frac{6928.2}{3} = 2309.4 \text{ VAR}$$

For a single capacitor in delta:
$$Q_{C,ph} = \frac{V_L^2}{X_C} = V_L^2 \cdot 2\pi f C$$

$$C = \frac{Q_{C,ph}}{2\pi f V_L^2} = \frac{2309.4}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 400^2}$$

$$C = \frac{2309.4}{50,265,600} = 45.95 \text{ μF}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\Delta,\text{per phase}} \approx 46 \text{ μF}}$$

**(iv) Power Triangle Before and After Correction:**

**Before Correction:**
- $P = 12$ kW (horizontal)
- $Q = 6.93$ kVAR (vertical, lagging)
- $S = 13.86$ kVA (hypotenuse)
- Angle $\phi_1 = 30°$

**After Correction (Unity pf):**
- $P = 12$ kW (horizontal, unchanged)
- $Q = 0$ (no vertical component)
- $S = P = 12$ kVA (no hypotenuse slope)
- Angle $\phi_2 = 0°$

> **[7 Marks Breakdown: (i) 1 mark, (ii) 2.5 marks, (iii) 2.5 marks, (iv) 1 mark]**

---

**B (b).** Compare single-phase and three-phase AC power systems. List any four advantages of three-phase systems over single-phase systems.

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Single-Phase | Three-Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of conductors | 2 (Phase + Neutral) | 3 or 4 (3 Phases ± Neutral) |
| Voltage levels (India) | 230 V domestic | 415 V industrial |
| Instantaneous power | Pulsates at $2\omega$ | Constant (steady) |
| Power per kg of conductor | Low | High (more efficient) |
| Self-starting motors | Not possible (needs aux winding) | Self-starting (rotating field) |
| Ripple in rectifier output | High | Low |
| Transmission efficiency | Lower | Higher |

**Four Advantages of Three-Phase Systems:**

1. **Constant Power Delivery:** The total instantaneous power in a balanced three-phase system is **constant** (does not pulsate at $2\omega$). This is a unique mathematical consequence of the 120° phase displacement. Single-phase power pulsates at twice the supply frequency, causing vibrations in motors.

2. **Higher Power-to-Weight Ratio:** For the same amount of conductor material, a three-phase system transmits **$\sqrt{3}$ times more power** than a single-phase system. This makes three-phase transmission inherently more economical.

3. **Self-Starting Rotating Machines:** Three-phase induction motors and synchronous motors are **self-starting** because the three-phase supply creates a naturally rotating magnetic field. Single-phase induction motors require an auxiliary winding and a capacitor, making them more complex and less reliable.

4. **Smaller and Cheaper Machines:** For the same power rating, three-phase machines (motors, generators, transformers) are **smaller, lighter, and cheaper** than single-phase machines. They also have higher efficiency and longer operational life.

> **[7 Marks Breakdown: 2 marks for the comparison table (at least 4 points), 5 marks for the four advantages with 1.25 marks each]**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist (Print this and stick it on your wall!)**

- [x] **Active Power P** is the only "useful" power — it's what gets billed for doing real work.
- [x] **Reactive Power Q** is the "foam" — it doesn't do work, but you can't get rid of it (mostly). It is needed to sustain magnetic fields in motors and transformers.
- [x] **Apparent Power S** is the "mug size" — what your wires and transformer must be sized to carry.
- [x] **Power Factor = cos(φ) = P / S** — the "usefulness" of the power being delivered.
- [x] **Single-Phase Power**: $P = V I \cos\phi$, $Q = V I \sin\phi$, $S = V I$ (all in RMS).
- [x] **Three-Phase Power (UNIVERSAL)**: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$, $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$, $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$.
- [x] **Star Connection**: $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$, $I_L = I_{ph}$.
- [x] **Delta Connection**: $V_L = V_{ph}$, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$.
- [x] **Power Triangle**: $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ — always a right-angled triangle.
- [x] **Total three-phase instantaneous power is CONSTANT** (no $2\omega$ ripple) — a unique advantage of balanced three-phase systems.
- [x] **Capacitor for pf correction**: $C = \frac{Q_C}{2\pi f V^2}$ (use $V = V_L$ for delta, $V = V_{ph}$ for star).
- [x] **Units are sacred**: W for P, VAR for Q, VA for S. Never mix them.
- [x] **Lagging pf = Inductive load = Industrial motor** (most common KTU question type).
