5. Houses, shops, and small industries tap this distributor through **service mains** (small cables like 4 mm², 6 mm², or 10 mm²) to their energy meters.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Cheat Sheet — Save This for the Exam!**

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Unit / Notes** |
|------------------------|-------------|------------------|
| RMS Voltage | $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts (V) |
| RMS Current | $I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Amperes (A) |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s |
| Single-Phase Power | $P = VI\cos\phi$ | Watts (W) |
| Three-Phase Power | $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Watts (W) |
| Reactive Power (3φ) | $Q = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR |
| Apparent Power (3φ) | $S = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L$ | VA |
| Power Factor | $\text{PF} = \cos\phi$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Star — Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3}\, V_{ph}$ | $I_L = I_{ph}$ |
| Star — Current | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | — |
| Delta — Voltage | $V_L = V_{ph}$ | — |
| Delta — Current | $I_L = \sqrt{3}\, I_{ph}$ | — |
| Line Loss | $P_{loss} = I^2 R$ | W |
| Standard Indian Freq. | $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ | $\omega = 314\,\text{rad/s}$ |
| Domestic Voltage | 230 V (1φ), 415 V (3φ) | RMS |
| Phase Displacement | $120°$ electrical | $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ rad |
| Conductors in 3-Wire | 3 (R, Y, B only) | No neutral |
| Conductors in 4-Wire | 4 (R, Y, B, N) | Neutral grounded |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial / Practical Relevance:**
> - **Three-phase systems** power every major industrial motor, elevator, HVAC chiller, and large compressor in the world.
> - **4-wire LT distribution** is the backbone of urban Indian electrification (e.g., BESCOM, KSEB, MSEDCL).
> - **EHV transmission** (400 kV, 765 kV) integrates the Indian National Grid, enabling power transfer from generating stations in one state to load centers hundreds of kilometers away.
> - **HVDC links** (e.g., Raigad–Pugalur $\pm$320 kV) efficiently transmit bulk power over 1,500+ km with minimal losses.
> - The correct identification of **Feeder vs. Distributor vs. Service Main** is foundational to **distribution network planning, voltage-drop analysis, and load-flow studies** for any power engineer.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Line-to-Phase Voltage Relationship in Star Connection

### Given
Three phase voltages of a balanced star-connected system:
$$\vec{V}_R = V_{ph} \angle 0°, \quad \vec{V}_Y = V_{ph} \angle -120°, \quad \vec{V}_B = V_{ph} \angle -240°$$

### To Prove
$$V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The line voltage between R and Y is the phasor difference:
$$\vec{V}_{RY} = \vec{V}_R - \vec{V}_Y$$

**Step 2:** Expressing in rectangular form:
$$\vec{V}_R = V_{ph} (1 + j0)$$
$$\vec{V}_Y = V_{ph} \left(\cos(-120°) + j\sin(-120°)\right) = V_{ph}\left(-\frac{1}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Subtracting:
$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{ph}\left(1 - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right) + j V_{ph}\left(0 - \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying the real and imaginary parts:
$$\vec{V}_{RY} = V_{ph}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + j V_{ph}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = V_{ph}\left(\frac{3}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Computing the magnitude:
$$|\vec{V}_{RY}| = V_{ph}\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluating the squares:
$$|\vec{V}_{RY}| = V_{ph}\sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = V_{ph}\sqrt{\frac{12}{4}} = V_{ph}\sqrt{3}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, by definition of line voltage in a star system:
$$V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line-to-Phase Current Relationship in Delta Connection

### Given
A balanced delta-connected load where the phase currents are:
$$\vec{I}_{RY} = I_{ph} \angle 0°, \quad \vec{I}_{YB} = I_{ph} \angle -120°, \quad \vec{I}_{BR} = I_{ph} \angle -240°$$

### To Prove
$$I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** By Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the R terminal of the delta:
$$\vec{I}_R = \vec{I}_{RY} - \vec{I}_{BR}$$

**Step 2:** Expressing in phasor form:
$$\vec{I}_{RY} = I_{ph}(1 + j0)$$
$$\vec{I}_{BR} = I_{ph}\left(\cos(-240°) + j\sin(-240°)\right) = I_{ph}\left(-\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Subtracting:
$$\vec{I}_R = I_{ph}\left(1 - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right) + j I_{ph}\left(0 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying:
$$\vec{I}_R = I_{ph}\left(\frac{3}{2} - j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Computing the magnitude:
$$|\vec{I}_R| = I_{ph}\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluating:
$$|\vec{I}_R| = I_{ph}\sqrt{\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = I_{ph}\sqrt{\frac{12}{4}} = I_{ph}\sqrt{3}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the line current equals:
$$I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Three-Phase Power

### Given
A balanced three-phase load (star or delta) with line voltage $V_L$, line current $I_L$, and load impedance angle $\phi$.

### To Prove
$$P_{total} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

### Case A: Star-Connected Load

**Step 1:** Phase voltage:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 2:** Phase current (line current in star):
$$I_{ph} = I_L$$

**Step 3:** Power per phase:
$$P_{phase} = V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 4:** Total power (3 phases):
$$P_{total} = 3 \cdot P_{phase} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying:
$$P_{total} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{3}} V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi \quad \blacksquare$$

### Case B: Delta-Connected Load

**Step 1:** Phase voltage:
$$V_{ph} = V_L$$

**Step 2:** Phase current:
$$I_{ph} = \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3:** Power per phase:
$$P_{phase} = V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi = V_L \cdot \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 4:** Total power:
$$P_{total} = 3 \cdot P_{phase} = 3 \cdot V_L \cdot \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\phi$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying:
$$P_{total} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The total three-phase power formula is **universal** — it applies to both star and delta connections equally, as long as the load is **balanced**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Voltage Drop Along a Radial Distributor with Concentrated Loads

### Given (KTU-Style Problem Setup)
A **2-wire DC distributor** (analogous to single-phase AC distributor for voltage-drop analysis) of length $L$ with resistance $r$ per unit length. Loads of $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are tapped at distances $x_1, x_2, x_3$ from the feeding end.

### To Derive
The voltage drop from the feeding point (A) to the farthest load point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define currents in each section. Let the current in section $A$–$1$ (between feeding point and first load) be:
$$I_{A1} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3$$

**Step 2:** Current in section 1–2:
$$I_{12} = I_2 + I_3$$

**Step 3:** Current in section 2–3:
$$I_{23} = I_3$$

**Step 4:** Voltage drop in section A–1 (over length $x_1$):
$$\Delta V_{A1} = r x_1 (I_1 + I_2 + I_3)$$

**Step 5:** Voltage drop in section 1–2 (over length $x_2 - x_1$):
$$\Delta V_{12} = r (x_2 - x_1)(I_2 + I_3)$$

**Step 6:** Voltage drop in section 2–3 (over length $x_3 - x_2$):
$$\Delta V_{23} = r (x_3 - x_2)(I_3)$$

**Step 7:** Total voltage drop from A to the farthest load (point 3):
$$\Delta V_{A3} = r x_1 (I_1 + I_2 + I_3) + r (x_2 - x_1)(I_2 + I_3) + r (x_3 - x_2)(I_3)$$

**Step 8:** Expanding and grouping:
$$\Delta V_{A3} = r \left[ x_1 I_1 + x_2 I_2 + x_3 I_3 \right]$$

**Step 9:** **Generalized result** for $n$ loads:
$$\Delta V = r \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k I_k$$

This is the **KTU standard result** for the voltage drop along a distributor, where $x_k$ is the distance from the feeding end to the $k^{th}$ load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Use:** The voltage drop must be kept within statutory limits (typically $\leq 6\%$ of rated voltage at the consumer end, as per CEA regulations). If it exceeds the limit, the conductor cross-section must be increased.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Power Loss Reduction by Stepping Up Voltage

### Given
Power to be transmitted: $P$ (Watts)
Line resistance: $R$ (ohms)
Original transmission voltage: $V_1$
Stepped-up voltage: $V_2 = k V_1$ (where $k > 1$)
Power factor: $\cos\phi$ (assumed constant)

### To Show
Line losses reduce by a factor of $k^2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Current at original voltage:
$$I_1 = \frac{P}{V_1 \cos\phi}$$

**Step 2:** Power loss at original voltage:
$$P_{loss,1} = I_1^2 R = \left(\frac{P}{V_1 \cos\phi}\right)^2 R = \frac{P^2 R}{V_1^2 \cos^2\phi}$$

**Step 3:** Current at stepped-up voltage:
$$I_2 = \frac{P}{V_2 \cos\phi} = \frac{P}{k V_1 \cos\phi}$$

**Step 4:** Power loss at stepped-up voltage:
$$P_{loss,2} = I_2^2 R = \left(\frac{P}{k V_1 \cos\phi}\right)^2 R = \frac{P^2 R}{k^2 V_1^2 \cos^2\phi}$$

**Step 5:** Ratio of losses:
$$\frac{P_{loss,2}}{P_{loss,1}} = \frac{\frac{P^2 R}{k^2 V_1^2 \cos^2\phi}}{\frac{P^2 R}{V_1^2 \cos^2\phi}} = \frac{1}{k^2}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:
$$P_{loss,2} = \frac{P_{loss,1}}{k^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Illustration:** If the transmission voltage is increased by a factor of $k = 10$, the line losses reduce to $\frac{1}{100}$ (1%) of their original value. This is why EHV transmission is essential.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Star, Delta, 3-Wire, and 4-Wire

| Parameter | Star (3-Wire) | Star (4-Wire) | Delta (3-Wire) | Delta (4-Wire — rare) |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|------------------------|
| $V_L / V_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3}$ | $\sqrt{3}$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| $I_L / I_{ph}$ | $1$ | $1$ | $\sqrt{3}$ | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| Neutral Provided | No | Yes (grounded) | No | Yes (artificial) |
| Phase Voltage Available | No | Yes ($V_L/\sqrt{3}$) | No | No |
| Total Power | $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Same | Same | Same |
| Suitable for | Balanced 3φ loads | Mixed 1φ + 3φ loads | 3φ motors only | Special instrumentation |
| Common Application | 3φ motor starters | Domestic distribution | Industrial 415V motors | Lab reference circuits |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part A Model (As per KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each question carries 3 marks, requires crisp conceptual answers, and tests CO mapping (typically CO2 for Module 2).

---

### **Question 1: Define a Single-Phase AC Supply System. State any two of its applications.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase AC supply system is one in which a single sinusoidal alternating voltage is supplied through a pair of conductors (one phase and one neutral). The instantaneous voltage varies sinusoidally with time as $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, with a standard Indian frequency of **50 Hz** and nominal RMS voltage of **230 V**.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Domestic lighting and household appliances** (fans, tube lights, televisions, refrigerators).
2. **Small commercial establishments** and **low-power single-phase motors** (up to ~1 kW, e.g., fan motors, small pumps).

*Examiner's Note:* 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two distinct applications.

---

### **Question 2: Compare Star and Delta Connections in a Three-Phase System (any four points).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Star (Y) | Delta (Δ) |
|---------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Line-to-Phase Voltage | $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ | $V_L = V_{ph}$ |
| 2 | Line-to-Phase Current | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ |
| 3 | Neutral Point | Available (can be brought out) | Not available |
| 4 | Number of Conductors | 3 or 4 (with neutral) | 3 only |
| 5 | Typical Use | Mixed 1φ + 3φ loads (domestic) | Pure 3φ loads (motors) |
| 6 | Starting Torque for Motors | Low (requires special winding) | High (preferred for 3φ motors) |

*Examiner's Note:* Any four contrasting points — 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part B Model (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, has internal choice (either/or), and is split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A three-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz supply feeds a balanced star-connected load of impedance $(15 + j20)\,\Omega$ per phase.**

**(a)** Calculate the phase voltage, line current, and total power consumed by the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same load is reconnected in delta across the same 415 V supply, calculate the new line current, total power, and compare it with the star case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a): Star-Connected Load**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
- Line voltage: $V_L = 415$ V
- Frequency: $f = 50$ Hz
- Impedance per phase: $Z = 15 + j20\;\Omega$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude of the impedance.
$$|Z| = \sqrt{15^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{225 + 400} = \sqrt{625} = 25\;\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Compute the phase angle.
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{20}{15}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.333) = 53.13°\;\text{(lagging, inductive)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the phase voltage.
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.6\;\text{V}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the phase current (which equals the line current in star).
$$I_{ph} = I_L = \frac{V_{ph}}{|Z|} = \frac{239.6}{25} = 9.58\;\text{A}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the total three-phase real power.
$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L\,I_L\,\cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 9.58 \times \cos(53.13°)$$
$$P = 1.732 \times 415 \times 9.58 \times 0.6 = 4130.4\;\text{W} \approx 4.13\;\text{kW}$$

**Alternative (via 3 × phase power):**
$$P = 3 \times V_{ph} \times I_{ph} \times \cos\phi = 3 \times 239.6 \times 9.58 \times 0.6 = 4130.4\;\text{W} \checkmark$$

**Result for (a):**
- $V_{ph} = 239.6$ V
- $I_L = 9.58$ A
- $P_{total} = 4.13$ kW

---

#### **Solution to (b): Delta-Connected Load**

**Step 1:** In delta, the phase voltage equals the line voltage.
$$V_{ph,\Delta} = V_L = 415\;\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** The phase current in delta:
$$I_{ph,\Delta} = \frac{V_{ph,\Delta}}{|Z|} = \frac{415}{25} = 16.6\;\text{A}$$

**Step 3:** The line current in delta:
$$I_{L,\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \times I_{ph,\Delta} = \sqrt{3} \times 16.6 = 28.75\;\text{A}$$

**Step 4:** Total power in delta:
$$P_\Delta = \sqrt{3} V_L I_{L,\Delta} \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 28.75 \times 0.6$$
$$P_\Delta = 1.732 \times 415 \times 28.75 \times 0.6 = 12,391.4\;\text{W} \approx 12.39\;\text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Comparison Table:**

| Quantity | Star | Delta | Ratio (Δ/Y) |
|----------|------|-------|-------------|
| Phase Voltage | 239.6 V | 415 V | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| Phase Current | 9.58 A | 16.6 A | $\sqrt{3}$ |
| Line Current | 9.58 A | 28.75 A | $3$ |
| Total Power | 4.13 kW | 12.39 kW | $3$ |

**Step 6: Conclusion.**
The delta connection draws **three times** the line current and delivers **three times** the power of the star connection (for the same line voltage). This is because each phase in delta sees the full line voltage ($\sqrt{3}$ times the star phase voltage), and the phase current is also $\sqrt{3}$ times higher; together this multiplies the power by a factor of $3$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **single-phase AC power supply system** and **three-phase AC power supply system**. State three advantages of three-phase over single-phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three-phase three-wire** and **three-phase four-wire** distribution systems. When is each preferred, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Single-Phase AC System:**
A single-phase system supplies a single sinusoidal voltage through **two conductors** (one phase and one neutral). The voltage varies as $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, with a standard Indian RMS value of **230 V** and frequency **50 Hz**.

**Three-Phase AC System:**
A three-phase system supplies three sinusoidal voltages displaced by **120 electrical degrees** through **three (or four) conductors**. The three sources $V_R, V_Y, V_B$ are mathematically:

$$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t),\; V_Y = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°),\; V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

**Three Advantages of Three-Phase over Single-Phase:**

1. **Constant Instantaneous Power:** The total instantaneous three-phase power is **constant** (does not pulsate with time), whereas single-phase instantaneous power is pulsating. This results in smoother operation of motors and reduced mechanical vibrations.

2. **Higher Power per kg of Conductor Material:** For the same power transmitted at the same voltage with the same line losses, a three-phase system requires only **75% of the copper** needed by a single-phase system.

3. **Self-Starting Three-Phase Motors:** Three-phase induction motors are **self-starting** (with a strong, uniform rotating magnetic field), whereas single-phase induction motors require auxiliary starting windings and capacitors.

*(Bonus advantages: Higher transmission efficiency, smaller and cheaper motors for the same power rating, capability to drive larger industrial loads.)*

---

#### **Solution to (b): Three-Phase 3-Wire and 4-Wire Systems**

**Three-Phase 3-Wire System:**
- Contains only **three line conductors**: R, Y, B.
- **No neutral conductor** is provided.
- Used in **delta-connected** systems or in **balanced star-connected** loads where the neutral is not required.
- Loads must be **balanced**; otherwise, unequal phase voltages develop (neutral displacement).
- Common in **industrial applications** with three-phase motors fed at 415 V.

**Three-Phase 4-Wire System:**
- Contains **three line conductors (R, Y, B) + one neutral conductor (N)**.
- The **neutral is solidly grounded** at the distribution transformer and at multiple consumer premises.
- Used when loads are **unbalanced** (as in domestic distribution where different houses draw different loads).
- Allows connection of **single-phase loads** (e.g., a 230 V bulb between any line and neutral) **and three-phase loads** (e.g., 415 V motor) from the same system.

**Preference:**
- **3-Wire preferred** when load is **balanced and purely three-phase** (e.g., large industrial motor, three-phase furnace).
- **4-Wire preferred** when load is **unbalanced and/or mixed** (single-phase + three-phase), such as in **residential, commercial, and small industrial** distribution.

**Reason:** In a 3-wire star system with unbalanced loads, the neutral point shifts (neutral displacement), causing the phase voltages to become unequal — this can damage single-phase loads. The 4-wire system provides a stable neutral reference, ensuring each phase voltage remains at its rated value ($V_L/\sqrt{3} = 230$ V).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$ in star/delta:** Students often blindly apply $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ in delta, which is WRONG. In delta, $V_L = V_{ph}$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_{ph}$ in star/delta:** Symmetric to the above. In star, $I_L = I_{ph}$. In delta, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to specify units** or to **state the formula before substituting** values — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unsystematic working.
> 
> 4. **Using 220 V instead of 230 V (or vice versa)** in numerical problems — both are accepted in India, but be consistent.
> 
> 5. **Not labelling the phasor diagram** with proper angles (0°, -120°, +120°) — at least 1 mark is reserved for a correctly drawn phasor diagram in 7-mark questions.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up Feeder and Distributor** in distribution system questions — remember: **Feeder has NO tappings**, **Distributor HAS tappings**.
> 
> 7. **Writing "phase sequence" wrongly** — the standard positive phase sequence is **R-Y-B**, not R-B-Y.
> 
> 8. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ factor** in the three-phase power formula — a single missing $\sqrt{3}$ can cause 3+ marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Additional Practice Problems (For Self-Assessment)

### Practice Problem 1 (7 Marks) `[Model KTU Question]`
A three-phase, 11 kV, 50 Hz transmission line delivers 5 MW to a balanced three-phase load at a power factor of 0.8 lagging. Calculate:
(a) The line current.
(b) The reactive power drawn by the load.
(c) The phase current if the load is connected in star.

**Solution Outline:**
- (a) $I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11000 \times 0.8} = 328$ A
- (b) $Q = P \tan\phi = 5 \times \tan(36.87°) = 3.75$ MVAR
- (c) $I_{ph,Y} = I_L = 328$ A

### Practice Problem 2 (7 Marks) `[Model KTU Question]`
State and explain the function of **(i) Feeder**, **(ii) Distributor**, and **(iii) Service Mains** in a power distribution system. Also explain the difference between a 3-wire and a 4-wire LT distribution system.

**Solution Outline:**
- (i) Feeder: Carries bulk power from substation; no tappings allowed.
- (ii) Distributor: Carries power from feeder/distribution transformer to consumers; tappings allowed.
- (iii) Service Mains: Final connection from distributor to consumer's meter; sized as per contract demand.
- 3-wire: No neutral; balanced loads only.
- 4-wire: With grounded neutral; allows unbalanced single-phase loads.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Exam Prep:**

### 🔑 Key Concepts to Memorize
- **Indian Standard Frequency:** 50 Hz (USA: 60 Hz)
- **Domestic Voltages:** 230 V (1φ), 415 V (3φ line)
- **Phase Displacement in 3φ:** 120° electrical (= 2π/3 rad)
- **Standard Sequence:** R → Y → B (positive sequence)

### 🔑 Star vs. Delta Relationships
- **Star:** $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$, $\;\;I_L = I_{ph}$
- **Delta:** $V_L = V_{ph}$, $\;\;I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$
- **Universal 3φ Power:** $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$

### 🔑 3-Wire vs. 4-Wire
- **3-Wire:** No neutral; balanced loads only; 3-phase industrial use
- **4-Wire:** With grounded neutral; supports unbalanced + single-phase loads; domestic use

### 🔑 Transmission Voltage Hierarchy
- **EHV:** 400 kV, 765 kV (and ±320/500/800 kV DC)
- **Primary:** 132 kV, 220 kV
- **Sub-Transmission:** 66 kV, 33 kV
- **Primary Distribution:** 11 kV, 22 kV
- **Secondary Distribution:** 415 V (3φ) / 230 V (1φ)

### 🔑 Distribution Components — "FDS" Mnemonic
- **F**eeder — **N**o **T**appings (FNT)
- **D**istributor — **T**appings allowed (DT)
- **S**ervice Main — **F**inal **L**ink to consumer (SFL)

### 🔑 Key Formulae
- $f = 50\;\text{Hz} \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 314\;\text{rad/s}$
- $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$
- $P_{1\phi} = V I \cos\phi$
- $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
- $P_{loss} = I^2 R \propto 1/V^2$ (at constant $P$)

### 🔑 Common Confusions to Avoid
- ⚠️ $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ is **ONLY for star** (not delta)
- ⚠️ $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$ is **ONLY for delta** (not star)
- ⚠️ Phase angle $\phi$ is **always between phase V and phase I**, not line V and line I (though they are equal in balanced star)
- ⚠️ Neutral wire carries current **only when loads are unbalanced** (in 4-wire system)
- ⚠️ **Feeder ≠ Distributor** — never interchange these terms in exams

### 🔑 One-Line Definitions (For 2-Mark Sub-Part Questions)
- **Single-Phase System:** A system with one AC source supplying one sinusoidal voltage through two conductors.
- **Three-Phase System:** A system with three AC sources displaced by 120° in time, supplying three sinusoidal voltages through three (or four) conductors.
- **Feeder:** A distribution line carrying bulk power from a substation to distribution centers, with **no intermediate tappings**.
- **Distributor:** A distribution line from which **tappings are taken** to feed individual consumers.
- **Service Main:** A small cable connecting the **distributor to a single consumer's meter** (the final link).
- **Transmission System:** The high-voltage (>33 kV) network carrying bulk power over long distances.
- **Distribution System:** The low/medium-voltage network delivering power to individual consumers at utilization voltages.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — GZEST204 Basic Electrical & Electronics Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> *All the best for your university examinations!* 🎓

topics.title: Electric Motors: Principle of Operation (Block Diagram Showing Power Stages, Losses and Efficiency), Classification and Types of DC and AC Motors, Common Applications, Principle of Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Motors: Principle of Operation (Block Diagram Showing Power Stages, Losses and Efficiency), Classification and Types of DC and AC Motors, Common Applications, Principle of Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Motor — Formal Definition

An **Electric Motor** is a rotating electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical input power** drawn from a supply source into **mechanical output power** delivered at the shaft, based on the fundamental interaction between a **current-carrying conductor** and a **magnetic field** governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Principle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GZEST204 - Module 2):**
> The motor is treated as a **two-port energy converter** — one electrical port and one mechanical port. Understanding the *path of power flow* and identifying *where losses occur* is the most frequently tested concept in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

The governing Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor inside a magnetic field is expressed as:

$$
\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}
$$

Where:
- $\vec{F}$ = Force experienced by the conductor (Newtons, **N**)
- $I$ = Current through the conductor (Amperes, **A**)
- $\vec{L}$ = Length vector of the conductor in the field (metres, **m**)
- $\vec{B}$ = Magnetic flux density (Tesla, **T**)

The **Torque** produced is the product of force and the effective radius of rotation:

$$
T = F \times r = BIL \times r
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Wheel of Electrons"

Imagine a **water wheel** in a flowing river. The flowing water strikes the paddles and rotates the wheel — converting the *kinetic energy of water* into *rotational mechanical energy*. 

An electric motor is conceptually identical:

| Water Wheel Concept | Electric Motor Equivalent |
|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Flowing water stream | Current ($I$) through conductor |
| Gravity / downhill path | Voltage ($V$) — potential difference |
| Paddle wheel blades | Armature conductors on rotor |
| River banks guiding flow | Magnetic field ($\vec{B}$) from stator/field winding |
| Rotating shaft (grinds flour) | Output shaft (drives a fan, pump, vehicle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** An electric motor works on the principle that **when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a mechanical force**. By arranging many such conductors on a rotating part (rotor), continuous rotation is achieved. The input is **Electrical Energy** and the output is **Mechanical Energy**.

## 1.3 Why Electric Motors Dominate Modern Engineering

- **High Efficiency:** Typically **85% to 95%** for industrial-grade machines.
- **Clean Operation:** No direct combustion, no exhaust emissions at point of use.
- **Compact Power Density:** Modern motors deliver **kW per kg** of mass.
- **Precise Controllability:** Speed, torque, and direction can be finely regulated.

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic primarily satisfies:
> - **CO2:** *Understand the constructional features and operating principles of electrical machines.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) and L3 (Apply)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Flow in an Electric Motor — Stage by Stage

The power flow in an electric motor follows a strict **cascading sequence** with losses extracted at each stage. The standard block diagram convention is mandatory for KTU 14-mark derivations.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Power Stage Logic

**Stage 1 — Electrical Input Power ($P_{in}$)**
- Power drawn from the supply mains (AC or DC source).
- $P_{in} = V \times I$ for DC motors; $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ for three-phase AC motors.
- This is the **starting point** of all power flow analysis.

**Stage 2 — Stator / Field Copper Loss ($P_{cu,field}$)**
- Occurs in the field winding (and for DC motors, also in the armature circuit before rotation).
- Formula: $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ — ohmic heating.
- Subtracting this gives: $P_{ag} = P_{in} - P_{cu,field}$ (air-gap power, for AC) or armature input for DC.

**Stage 3 — Core / Iron Loss ($P_{core}$)**
- Comprises **Hysteresis Loss** ($P_h = \eta B_{max}^{1.6} f V$) and **Eddy Current Loss** ($P_e = k_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$).
- Occurs in the iron laminations of stator/rotor due to alternating flux.

**Stage 4 — Rotor Copper Loss ($P_{cu,rotor}$)** *(AC motors only)*
- For induction motors: $P_{cu,rotor} = s \times P_{ag}$ where $s$ is the slip.

**Stage 5 — Mechanical Power Developed ($P_m$)**
- Power converted from electrical to mechanical form at the rotor surface.
- $P_m = T \times \omega$ where $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s.

**Stage 6 — Friction & Windage Loss ($P_{f+w}$)**
- Bearing friction, air drag on rotating parts.

**Stage 7 — Shaft Output Power ($P_{out}$)**
- Useful mechanical power delivered to the load.
- $P_{out} = P_m - P_{f+w}$

**Stage 8 — Efficiency ($\eta$)**
- Ratio of output to input power.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for the End Semester Exam. Every formula here is a potential 7-mark or 14-mark derivation question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | EMF Equation (DC) | $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Volts (**V**) | $\Phi$=flux, $Z$=conductors, $N$=rpm, $P$=poles, $A$=parallel paths |
| 2 | Torque (DC Motor) | $T = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = 0.159\, \Phi Z I_a \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ | N·m | $E_b$ = back EMF |
| 3 | Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | V | Fundamental DC motor equation |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = 9.55 \times \dfrac{P_{out}}{N}$ | N·m | Where $P_{out}$ in Watts, $N$ in rpm |
| 5 | Input Power (3-$\phi$) | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W | AC motor input |
| 6 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ = supply frequency |
| 7 | Slip (Induction Motor) | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le s \le 1$ for motoring |
| 8 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | Frequency induced in rotor |
| 9 | Rotor Cu Loss | $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | W | Air-gap power splits as $P_m : P_{cu} = (1-s) : s$ |
| 10 | Mechanical Power Developed | $P_m = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | W | Power at rotor surface |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Universal |
| 12 | Synchronous Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{3 V E}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ | N·m | $\delta$ = load/torque angle |
| 13 | Starting Torque (IM) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | For induction motor |
| 14 | Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = X_a + X_l$ | $\Omega$ | $X_a$=armature reaction, $X_l$=leakage |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Notice:** For absolute value or modulus expressions (such as $\vert Z \vert$), the LaTeX command `\vert` is used inside the markdown table to prevent breakage of the table delimiter pipe character.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The power-stage block diagram is not merely academic — it is the foundation of:

- **Energy Auditing:** Industrial plants calculate *where* power is wasted to target efficiency improvements.
- **Electric Vehicle Design:** EV engineers minimize iron losses at cruising speeds and optimize copper loss distribution.
- **Cooling System Sizing:** Loss components dictate the size of fans, oil coolers, and heat exchangers.
- **ISO 50001 Compliance:** Energy management standards require documented efficiency analysis based on these stages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: EMF Equation of a DC Generator / Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2. A 7-mark question is almost guaranteed in any KTU ESE paper for GZEST204.

**Given:** A DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, flux per pole $\Phi$ Webers, running at $N$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Flux cut per revolution**

In one complete revolution, each conductor cuts the flux from all $P$ poles. The total flux cut by one conductor per revolution is:

$$
\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = \Phi \times P
$$

**Step 2 — Number of revolutions per second**

Converting rotational speed from rpm to revolutions per second:

$$
n = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[revolutions/second]}
$$

**Step 3 — Flux cut per conductor per second (rate of flux cutting)**

Multiplying the flux per revolution by revolutions per second gives the rate of change of flux, which by **Faraday's Law** equals the EMF induced per conductor:

$$
\text{EMF per conductor} = \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 4 — Total conductors in series per parallel path**

The total $Z$ conductors are distributed across $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in series along one path is:

$$
Z_{series} = \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 5 — Total EMF (sum of all series conductors in one path)**

Since EMFs of series conductors add up:

$$
E = \frac{Z}{A} \times \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Standard Form**

Rearranging into the conventional textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,E = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}\,}
$$

**Logical Sanity Check:**
- If $P$ doubles → $E$ doubles ✓ (more flux cutting)
- If $N$ doubles → $E$ doubles ✓ (faster cutting)
- If $A$ increases (wave → lap) → $E$ decreases ✓ (lap winding has more parallel paths, lower EMF)

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Starting Point — Work Done in One Revolution:**

When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, work is done against the **magnetic drag force** (which is the manifestation of back EMF). For a motor operating at back EMF $E_b$ with armature current $I_a$:

$$
\text{Electrical power converted to mechanical} = E_b \cdot I_a
$$

This mechanical power is also expressed as $T \cdot \omega$:

$$
T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a
$$

**Solving for torque:**

$$
T = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{\omega}
$$

**Substituting $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$:**

$$
T = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}\right) \cdot I_a}{\left(\dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}\right)}
$$

**Simplification — the $N$ terms cancel and 60 cancels:**

$$
T = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, P \, I_a}{2 \pi A}
$$

**Re-expressing in the textbook form ($2\pi \approx 6.28$, or equivalently $1/2\pi \approx 0.159$):**

$$
\boxed{\,T = 0.159 \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a \left(\frac{P}{A}\right)\,} \quad \text{[N·m]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Torque is **independent of speed** $N$. It depends only on flux, armature current, and machine construction. This is why DC motors are *preferred* for traction and high-torque starting applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency from Power Flow

Consider a DC shunt motor. The power flow is:

**Stage 1:** $P_{in} = V I_L$ (mains input)

**Stage 2:** Field loss $P_f = V I_{sh} = \dfrac{V^2}{R_{sh}}$

**Stage 3:** Armature input $P_a = V I_a = P_{in} - P_f$

**Stage 4:** Armature copper loss $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$

**Stage 5:** Mechanical power developed $P_m = P_a - P_{cu,a} = E_b I_a$

**Stage 6:** Stray loss $P_{stray}$ and friction-windage loss $P_{f+w}$

**Stage 7:** Shaft output $P_{out} = P_m - P_{stray} - P_{f+w}$

**Efficiency Expression:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{\sum \text{losses}}{P_{in}}
$$

**Expanded form for DC shunt motor:**

$$
\eta = \frac{V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - P_{f+w} - P_{stray}}{V(I_a + I_{sh})}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Categories

| Loss Type | Where It Occurs | Formula | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|-----------------|---------|---------------------|
| Copper (Armature) | Armature winding | $I_a^2 R_a$ | Use low-resistance copper, larger conductors |
| Copper (Field) | Field winding | $I_f^2 R_f$ or $V^2 / R_f$ | Use high-resistance alloy, permanent magnets |
| Hysteresis | Iron core | $k_h f B_{max}^{1.6} V$ | Use silicon steel laminations |
| Eddy Current | Iron core | $k_e f^2 B_{max}^2 t^2$ | Thin laminations, high-resistivity steel |
| Friction | Bearings | $k_f \omega$ | Ball/roller bearings, lubrication |
| Windage | Rotating surfaces | $k_w \omega^2$ | Streamlined rotor, enclosed slots |
| Stray | Load-dependent | $k_{stray} \cdot \text{load}^2$ | Skewed slots, tighter manufacturing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Stage Block Diagram of an Electric Motor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram is the **visual representation of Section 2.1** and is mandatory for any 14-mark question on motor efficiency. Memorize the loss sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** 
**State and explain Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction as applied to a DC generator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement:** Whenever a conductor cuts magnetic flux, an EMF is induced in it; the magnitude of induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage. **(1 mark)**
- **Mathematical form:** $E = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ **(1 mark)**
- **Application to DC generator:** In a DC generator, the armature conductors mounted on a rotating drum cut the stationary magnetic flux produced by the field poles. As each conductor sweeps past a north and south pole in one revolution, it cuts a total flux of $\Phi P$ Webers, generating an alternating EMF that is rectified by the commutator to produce a unidirectional (DC) output at the brushes. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a separately excited and self-excited DC motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Separately Excited | Self-Excited |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| Field Supply | Independent external DC source | Motor's own supply via field winding |
| Construction | Two separate sources needed | Single supply suffices |
| Flux Control | Easier and wider range | Limited by series/shunt configuration |
| Application | Where wide speed range is needed (e.g., servo drives) | General purpose industrial drives |

*Any valid three differentiating points earn full marks.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**

**a) (7 Marks)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various power stages and losses in a DC shunt motor. Derive the EMF equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Power Stages Diagram (4 marks) and Explanation (3 marks):**

The power flow in a DC shunt motor proceeds as follows:

1. **Electrical Input Power** $P_{in} = V I_L$ — drawn from DC mains.
2. **Field Copper Loss** $P_{sh} = V I_{sh} = V^2 / R_{sh}$ — occurs in the shunt field winding.
3. **Armature Input** $P_a = V I_a = P_{in} - P_{sh}$.
4. **Armature Copper Loss** $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$.
5. **Mechanical Power Developed** $P_m = P_a - P_{cu,a} = E_b I_a$.
6. **Friction & Windage Loss** $P_{f+w}$ — bearing friction and air drag.
7. **Stray Loss** $P_{stray}$ — minor load-dependent loss.
8. **Shaft Output Power** $P_{out} = P_m - P_{f+w} - P_{stray}$.

**Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$

**Part (a) — EMF Equation Derivation (Full 7-mark version follows the same derivation as Section 3.1):**

- Each conductor cuts $\Phi P$ Webers per revolution.
- Revolutions per second = $N / 60$.
- EMF per conductor = $\Phi P \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$.
- Conductors in series per parallel path = $Z / A$.
- Total EMF $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ volts.

**b) (7 Marks)** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 720 conductors wave-wound. The armature and field resistances are $0.4 \, \Omega$ and $250 \, \Omega$ respectively. The motor draws a line current of 30 A at a speed of 1000 rpm. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Armature current, (iii) Total copper losses, (iv) Mechanical power developed, (v) Efficiency (neglect stray and friction losses).

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, $Z = 720$, wave-wound ($A = 2$)
- $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250 \, \Omega$
- $I_L = 30$ A, $N = 1000$ rpm

**Step 1 — Shunt field current:**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2 — Armature current:**

$$
I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 30 - 1 = 29 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Back EMF:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (29 \times 0.4) = 250 - 11.6 = 238.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Total copper losses:**

$$
P_{cu,total} = I_a^2 R_a + V I_{sh} = (29)^2 (0.4) + (250)(1) = 336.4 + 250 = 586.4 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical power developed:**

$$
P_m = E_b I_a = 238.4 \times 29 = 6913.6 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Input power and efficiency (neglecting stray and friction):**

$$
P_{in} = V I_L = 250 \times 30 = 7500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{out} \approx P_m = 6913.6 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{6913.6}{7500} \times 100 \approx 92.18 \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting** to subtract shunt field current to get armature current. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing** wave-wound and lap-wound. Wave → $A = 2$; Lap → $A = P$. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not** stating the unit of each answer explicitly. Marks deducted: **0.5–1 mark per part**.
> 4. **Stopping** at mechanical power and forgetting efficiency. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]**

**a) (7 Marks)** Explain the principle of operation of a three-phase induction motor. Derive the condition for maximum torque and the expression for slip at which it occurs.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**
A three-phase induction motor operates on the principle of a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)**:
- When a balanced 3-phase supply is given to stator windings spatially displaced by **120°**, a magnetic field of constant magnitude rotates at **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
- This rotating field cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF by Faraday's law.
- The induced current in the short-circuited rotor interacts with the RMF to produce a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$, generating **torque** in the direction of RMF rotation.
- The rotor chases the rotating field but **never catches up** — relative motion is essential for induction. The difference is quantified as **slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$.

**Maximum Torque Condition (3 marks):**

The torque of an induction motor is given by:

$$
T = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2 / s}{(R_2 / s)^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{s^2 E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}
$$

To find maximum torque, differentiate with respect to $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{s R_2}{R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2}\right] = 0
$$

Applying the quotient rule:

$$
\frac{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)(1) - s R_2 (2 s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2} = 0
$$

Numerator equals zero:

$$
R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2 s^2 X_2^2 = 0
$$

$$
R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\,s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}\,}
$$

**Maximum Torque Value:**

Substituting $s = R_2 / X_2$ back into torque expression:

$$
T_{max} = \frac{3}{2\pi N_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2}{2 X_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{4 \pi N_s X_2}\,}
$$

**Important Note:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2$, but the **slip at which it occurs** depends on $R_2$. This is the basis of rotor resistance starting methods.

**b) (7 Marks)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. Find: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Full-load rotor speed, (iii) Frequency of rotor EMF, (iv) Slip at which maximum torque occurs if rotor resistance = 0.1 $\Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance = 1.5 $\Omega$.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$, $s_{fl} = 0.04$, $R_2 = 0.1 \, \Omega$, $X_2 = 1.5 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Full-load rotor speed:**

$$
N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Rotor EMF frequency:**

$$
f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Slip for maximum torque:**

$$
s_{max T} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.1}{1.5} = 0.0667 \text{ or } 6.67\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors in Induction Motor Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Slip is a ratio (dimensionless) — *never* give a slip answer in rpm. **0.5 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the rotor frequency relationship** $f_r = s f$ — students often incorrectly write 50 Hz.
> 3. **Confusing rotor resistance per phase** — in slip-ring motors $R_2$ refers to per-phase rotor resistance.

---

## 5.3 Common Applications of Electric Motors

| Motor Type | Typical Applications |
|------------|----------------------|
| **DC Shunt Motor** | Lathes, centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, machine tools, printing presses (constant speed loads) |
| **DC Series Motor** | Electric locomotives, cranes, hoists, winches, trolley buses (high starting torque) |
| **DC Compound Motor** | Elevators, rolling mills, punches, shears, heavy planers (mixed load demands) |
| **Three-Phase Squirrel Cage IM** | Industrial fans, compressors, conveyors, pumps, mixers, domestic washing machines |
| **Three-Phase Slip-Ring IM** | Cranes, mills, hoists, large compressors requiring high starting torque |
| **Single-Phase IM** | Ceiling fans, refrigerators, washing machines, small workshop tools, hair dryers |
| **Synchronous Motor** | Power factor correction, constant-speed drives, large compressors, ball mills, clocks |

## 5.4 Principle of Traction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Mandate:** Students must understand the *why* of motor selection in traction.

**Electric Traction Definition:** The system of locomotion in which the **driving force** is obtained from **electric motors** is called *electric traction*.

**Key Requirements of Traction Motors:**
1. **High Starting Torque** — to accelerate heavy loads (trains, EVs).
2. **Wide Speed Range** — from standing start to cruising speed.
3. **Speed Control** — simple, efficient, often regenerative braking.
4. **Robustness** — withstands vibrations, dust, temperature variations.
5. **Series Characteristic** — naturally high torque at low speeds (like DC series motor).

**Traction Motor of Choice:**
- **Conventional Rail:** DC Series Motor (historically), now 3-phase Induction Motors with VFD control.
- **Modern Electric Vehicles:** Three-phase squirrel cage induction motor (Tesla) or PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor).

**Typical Power Flow in Traction:**
- Substation → Overhead Catenary → Pantograph → Power Converter (Rectifier/Chopper/Inverter) → Traction Motor → Gearbox → Wheels.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### **Core Definitions**
- ☐ Electric motor converts **electrical → mechanical** energy using Lorentz force $F = BIL$.
- ☐ Generator and motor are **dynamically identical**; only the direction of energy flow differs.
- ☐ **Back EMF** $E_b$ is the heart of DC motor operation — it self-regulates current.

### **Critical Formulas (Box Them!)**
- ☐ **EMF:** $E = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$
- ☐ **Torque:** $T = 0.159 \, \Phi Z I_a \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right)$ N·m (DC); $T = \dfrac{3 V E}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$ (Synchronous)
- ☐ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$
- ☐ **Slip:** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
- ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \text{losses}}{P_{in}}$

### **Classification Memory Trick**
- ☐ **DC Motors** = Separately Excited + (Shunt ∥ Series ∥ Compound) — mnemonic: **"SSC"**
- ☐ **AC Motors** = Synchronous + Induction (1-$\phi$ + 3-$\phi$); 3-$\phi$ IM = Squirrel Cage + Slip Ring
- ☐ **Compound** = Cumulative + Differential; further subdivided into Long Shunt + Short Shunt

### **Power Flow Sequence (Most Tested)**
- ☐ Input → Stator Cu Loss → Armature/Air-gap Power → Rotor Cu Loss (AC) → Mechanical Power → Friction + Windage + Stray → **Output**
- ☐ Efficiency = $1 - \dfrac{\text{All losses}}{P_{in}}$

### **Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ☐ Wave winding → $A = 2$ (always, regardless of poles); Lap winding → $A = P$.
- ☐ $N_s$ is in **rpm** when using $120 f / P$ formula.
- ☐ Slip $s$ is **dimensionless**, never in rpm.
- ☐ Synchronous motor runs at **constant speed** $N_s$ (no slip).
- ☐ Induction motor rotor frequency $f_r = s f$ — not $f$!

### **Traction Mnemonic**
- ☐ **"T-H-W-C-R"** = Torque (high), Heavy duty, Wide speed range, Control (regenerative), Robust.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Electric Motors Notes | GZEST204 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Earthing: Need for Earthing, Types of Earthing (Pipe Earthing, Plate Earthing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Earthing: Need for Earthing, Types of Earthing (Pipe Earthing, Plate Earthing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Earthing (Grounding):** Earthing is defined as the process of transferring the immediate discharge of electrical energy directly to the earth through a low-resistance wire. It is achieved by connecting the non-current carrying parts of an electrical equipment, or the neutral point of the supply system, to the general mass of the earth through a conductor of negligible resistance.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (GZEST204), **earthing** is broadly classified into two functional categories:

1. **Equipment Earthing (Protective Earthing):** The metal body of an electrical appliance (which is not supposed to carry current under normal conditions) is connected to the earth.
2. **System Earthing (Neutral Earthing):** The neutral point of a star-connected generator/transformer or the neutral of a three-phase, four-wire system is connected to the earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly differentiate between **earthing** and **grounding**. In Indian Standard (IS) practice, *earthing* means connecting to the general mass of earth, while *grounding* is often used for system neutral referencing. However, in common engineering parlance (and in KTU examinations), both terms are used synonymously.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a huge **water tank with an overflow pipe** connected to its base. If water (electric current) overflows or leaks, the overflow pipe (earth wire) safely drains it into the ground (earth pit) without damaging the tank (appliance) or the people around it.

Another simple analogy is a **pressure cooker safety valve**. The valve releases excess steam (fault current) directly to the atmosphere (earth) to prevent the cooker from bursting (appliance damage / electric shock).

In both cases, the earth acts as a **vast reservoir** that can absorb unlimited electrical charge at zero potential (0 V). Because the human body is also at roughly the same potential as earth (due to our feet touching the ground), no current flows through a person who touches a properly earthed appliance, even if an internal short circuit makes the metal body live.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Resistance of Earth (Earth Electrode Resistance):** Must be less than **1 Ω to 5 Ω** (ideal ≤ 1 Ω) for large installations; **≤ 8 Ω** permissible for small domestic systems.
- **Soil Resistivity (ρ):** Typically **10 Ω·m to 1000 Ω·m** depending on moisture, mineral content, and temperature.
- **Standard Reference:** The earth is considered to be at **0 V (Zero Potential)** by universal convention.
- **Touch Voltage Limit:** ≤ **50 V (AC)** / **120 V (DC)** as per IS 3043 for human safety.
- **Step Voltage Limit:** The potential difference between the feet of a person (assumed 1 m apart) standing near an earthed equipment.
- **Earth Pit Depth (Standard):** Typically **2.5 m to 4 m** below ground level for most practical installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Check (KTU Pattern):** The earth's potential is taken as the **reference (zero) potential** because the earth acts as an infinite sink of charge. Any charged body connected to the earth instantly acquires zero potential relative to it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept – Why Earthing is Needed

The need for earthing in any electrical installation arises from the following critical engineering reasons:

- **Safety of Human Life (Electric Shock Prevention):** If a live wire accidentally touches the metal body of an appliance, the body becomes energized. Without earthing, a person touching it forms the discharge path, leading to fatal electric shock. Earthing provides a low-resistance path that trips the fuse/MCB before the person is harmed.
- **Protection of Equipment:** Fault currents are safely diverted, preventing damage to insulation, windings, and connected loads.
- **Voltage Stabilization:** By connecting the neutral to earth, the system voltage is stabilized with respect to ground, limiting over-voltages due to lightning or switching surges.
- **Over-voltage Protection:** Lightning arresters and surge suppressors discharge transient over-voltages safely into the earth.
- **Fault Current Path:** Provides a return path for fault currents so that protective devices (MCB, MCCB, fuses, ELCB) operate reliably.
- **Prevention of Fire Hazards:** Reduces sparking and arcing by quickly clearing faults.
- **Noise and EMI Reduction:** In electronic circuits, proper grounding reduces electromagnetic interference (EMI).

## 2.2 Components of an Earthing System

A standard earthing installation consists of the following components:

- **Earth Electrode:** The metal rod, pipe, or plate buried in the earth (e.g., GI pipe, copper plate, cast iron plate).
- **Earth Conductor:** The wire connecting the appliance body or system neutral to the earth electrode. Made of **GI (Galvanized Iron) or copper** of adequate cross-section.
- **Earth Pit:** The masonry/concrete enclosure housing the earth electrode, often filled with a mixture of **charcoal, salt, and sand** to retain moisture and reduce soil resistivity.
- **Earth Resistance:** The total resistance offered by the earth electrode to the surrounding soil, given by:

$$
R_e = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = Soil resistivity in $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$
- $L$ = Length of the electrode in meters
- $d$ = Diameter of the electrode in meters
- $R_e$ = Earth electrode resistance in ohms (Ω)

- **Earth Wire (Protective Earth – PE):** Carries fault current. Color coded **Green** (or Green with Yellow stripe) as per IS standards.

## 2.3 Soil Resistivity and Its Importance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Soil resistivity ($\rho$) is the resistance of a unit cube of soil, measured in **Ω·m**. It is the single most important factor that determines the effectiveness of an earthing system.

### Factors Affecting Soil Resistivity

- **Moisture Content:** Wet soil has lower resistivity; dry soil has very high resistivity.
- **Soil Composition:** Clay < Loam < Sand < Gravel < Rocky (in order of increasing resistivity).
- **Temperature:** Resistivity increases sharply as temperature falls below 0 °C (frozen soil is a poor conductor).
- **Mineral Salt Content:** Dissolved salts (NaCl, MgSO₄) drastically reduce resistivity.
- **Depth of Electrode:** Deeper layers have more stable moisture and temperature.

### Improvement of Soil Resistivity (Treatment of Earth Pit)

To maintain low and stable earth resistance throughout the year, the earth pit is backfilled with alternate layers of:

- **Charcoal (Coke):** Retains moisture for long periods.
- **Salt (NaCl):** Dissolves in moisture and increases soil conductivity.
- **Sand/Gravel:** Provides good drainage and prevents the electrode from being in direct contact with solid earth.

The typical layer arrangement (bottom to top) is: **Sand → Charcoal → Salt → Earth Electrode → Salt → Charcoal → Sand**. A layer of **GI/Copper strip** is run from the electrode to the inspection chamber for periodic water pouring.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|--------|----------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | Earth Electrode Resistance (Pipe/Rod) | $R_e$ | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | $\Omega$ | Valid for $L \gg d$ |
| 2 | Earth Electrode Resistance (Plate) | $R_e$ | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{8r} + \dfrac{\rho}{4t}$ | $\Omega$ | For circular plate of radius $r$, depth $t$ |
| 3 | Current through Human Body | $I_{body}$ | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body} + R_e}$ | A | Used for shock severity analysis |
| 4 | Soil Resistivity (Wenner 4-Probe) | $\rho$ | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | $\Omega\cdot\text{m}$ | $a$ = probe spacing |
| 5 | Maximum Touch Voltage (AC) | $V_{touch}$ | $\leq 50\text{ V (AC rms)}$ | V | Per IS 3043 safety limit |
| 6 | Earth Resistance Target | $R_e$ | $\leq 1\text{ to }5\text{ }\Omega$ (industrial), $\leq 8\text{ }\Omega$ (domestic) | $\Omega$ | Per IEEE/IS standards |
| 7 | Step Voltage Limit | $V_{step}$ | $V_{step} = \dfrac{165 + \rho_s}{1000}$ (approx) | V | $\rho_s$ = surface soil resistivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipeline Note (Anti-Break):** All absolute-value bars inside markdown tables have been converted to `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Earthing

- **Domestic:** Protects washing machines, refrigerators, air-conditioners, and water heaters from leakage shocks.
- **Industrial:** Protects high-power motors, transformers, switchgear, and PLCs; essential for personnel safety in HV switchyards.
- **Telecom and IT:** Signal reference ground for data centers and server racks; EMI/EMC compliance.
- **Power Generation:** Generator neutral grounding to limit line-to-ground fault currents.
- **Lightning Protection:** Air terminals (Franklin rods) discharge lightning strokes safely into the earth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Earth Resistance of a Pipe / Rod Electrode

A **pipe electrode** is essentially a vertical cylindrical rod of length $L$ and diameter $d$ buried in soil of uniform resistivity $\rho$. We consider a hemispherical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ around the rod, where current flows radially outward.

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the conduction geometry:**
The current disperses radially into a hemispherical surface (because current only flows into the ground, not upward). The differential resistance of a hemispherical shell of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$ is:

$$
dR = \frac{\rho \, dx}{2\pi x^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from the surface of the pipe to infinity:**

$$
R_e = \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{\rho}{2\pi x^{2}} \, dx
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} x^{-2} \, dx = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[-x^{-1}\right]_{d/2}^{\infty}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the limits:**

$$
R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi} \left[ 0 - \left(-\frac{2}{d}\right) \right] = \frac{\rho}{\pi d}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the practical correction factor:**
In real installations, the pipe has finite length $L$, and the upper hemisphere is blocked by air. The empirical correction (with $L \gg d$) yields:

$$
\boxed{\,R_e = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]\,}
$$

This is the **standard KTU / IS 3043 formula** for a vertical pipe/rod electrode.

## 3.2 Derivation: Earth Resistance of a Plate Electrode

For a **circular plate electrode** of radius $r$ buried at depth $t$, the resistance has two components:

**Component 1 — Resistance of soil from plate to ground surface (hemicylindrical):**

$$
R_1 = \frac{\rho}{8r}
$$

**Component 2 — Resistance of soil from plate to remote earth (hemispherical):**

$$
R_2 = \frac{\rho}{4t}
$$

These two resistances are in **series**, so the total earth resistance of the plate is:

$$
\boxed{\,R_e = \frac{\rho}{8r} + \frac{\rho}{4t}\,}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pipe vs. Plate Earthing

| Parameter | Pipe Earthing | Plate Earthing |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Electrode Form** | Vertical GI pipe (length 2.5–4 m, dia 40–50 mm) | Vertical GI/Copper plate (60 cm × 60 cm × 6 mm) |
| **Earth Resistance** | Lower (better) for same mass of metal | Comparatively higher |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Excellent (rod-like, can be driven into soil) | Poor (plate may bend in rocky soil) |
| **Installation Cost** | Moderate | Higher (excavation large) |
| **Suitability of Soil** | Suitable for most soils including rocky | Best in dry/sandy soils |
| **Life Span** | 7–10 years (GI) | 8–10 years (GI); longer for copper |
| **Current Carrying Capacity** | Very high (long surface area) | High |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (water can be poured easily) | Difficult |
| **Standard Code** | IS 3043 (1987) | IS 3043 (1987) |
| **Preferred For** | Power stations, substations, large buildings | Domestic installations, small industries |

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step: Pipe Earthing Construction

> [!NOTE]
> The following 10-step procedure is the **standard construction sequence** as per IS 3043. KTU examiners frequently test this in Part B questions.

**Step 1 — Excavation:** Dig a pit of dimensions **1.5 m × 1.5 m** with a depth of approximately **3.5 m** to reach permanently moist subsoil.

**Step 2 — Electrode Selection:** Take a **GI pipe** of length **2.5 m to 3 m** and diameter **40 mm to 50 mm**. The lower end is tapered or fitted with a cast iron shoe for easy driving.

**Step 3 — Perforations:** Make holes of about **12 mm diameter** in the pipe at regular intervals (typically 75 mm apart) to allow seepage of moisture and salts into the surrounding soil.

**Step 4 — Driving the Pipe:** Place the pipe vertically at the center of the pit. Using a driving tool (sledgehammer or mechanical driver), drive the pipe until its top end is just below ground level.

**Step 5 — GI Strip / Wire Connection:** Connect a **GI strip (25 mm × 3 mm)** or **GI wire (8 SWG)** to the top of the pipe using a **nut, bolt, and washer** (galvanized).

**Step 6 — Backfilling — Layer 1 (Sand):** Place a **75 mm layer of fine sand** at the bottom of the pit around the pipe for drainage.

**Step 7 — Backfilling — Layer 2 (Charcoal + Salt):** Alternate layers of **charcoal/coke (75 mm) and common salt (75 mm)** around and above the pipe. This mixture retains moisture and reduces soil resistivity.

**Step 8 — Top Connection:** Run the GI strip through a **GI pipe sleeve** to the inspection chamber. Provide a **funnel and wire mesh** on top of the pit for periodic water pouring.

**Step 9 — Inspection Chamber:** Construct a **masonry chamber (30 cm × 30 cm)** with a cast iron cover at ground level. This is the inspection/maintenance access point.

**Step 10 — Earth Resistance Measurement:** Use an **earth tester (fall-of-potential method)** to verify that $R_e \leq 5 \, \Omega$. Record the value for future reference.

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step: Plate Earthing Construction

**Step 1 — Excavation:** Dig a pit of dimensions **1.2 m × 1.2 m** with depth approximately **3 m**.

**Step 2 — Electrode Selection:** Use a **GI plate (60 cm × 60 cm × 6 mm)** or **Copper plate (60 cm × 60 cm × 3 mm)**.

**Step 3 — Plate Placement:** Place the plate **vertically** at the bottom of the pit with its face parallel to the wall of the pit (this maximizes soil contact area on both sides).

**Step 4 — Conductor Connection:** Bolt a **GI strip (50 mm × 6 mm)** or **Copper strip (25 mm × 3 mm)** to the plate using **2 nos. of bolts and nuts** coated with tin to prevent corrosion.

**Step 5 — Backfilling with Coke/Salt:** Fill the pit with alternate layers of **charcoal (75 mm) and salt (75 mm)**, just like in pipe earthing. Compact each layer and pour water to settle the mixture.

**Step 6 — Lead-out Pipe:** Take the GI/Copper strip out through a **GI/Copper pipe sleeve** (40 mm dia) to a height of about 30 cm above ground level.

**Step 7 — Inspection Chamber & Funnel:** Build a masonry inspection chamber with a cast iron cover. Provide a **C.I. funnel with wire mesh** on top of the pit for water pouring.

**Step 8 — Earth Connection:** Connect the lead-out strip to the **main earth bus bar** of the building's electrical system.

**Step 9 — Soil Treatment:** Pour **2 buckets of water** through the funnel every 2–3 months to keep the soil moist and resistivity low.

**Step 10 — Testing:** Measure earth resistance using **fall-of-potential method** or **clamp-on earth tester**. The value should be ≤ 5 Ω.

## 3.6 Derivation: Why Touch Voltage is Limited to 50 V AC

The human body can be modeled as a resistance of approximately **1000 Ω (wet skin) to 100,000 Ω (dry skin)**. The safe let-go current is around **10 mA (AC)** at 50 Hz.

By Ohm's Law:

$$
V_{max} = I_{safe} \times R_{body} = 10\,\text{mA} \times 5000\,\Omega = 50\,\text{V}
$$

Therefore, the design of any earthing system must ensure that the **touch voltage** under worst-case fault conditions never exceeds **50 V AC** or **120 V DC** as per **IS 3043**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Complete Earthing System

> [!NOTE]
> Since the actual physical layout of an earth pit cannot be drawn accurately in a schematic, the following **Mermaid block diagram** represents the functional architecture of a complete pipe earthing system, showing the flow of fault current from the appliance body to the earth.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Live AC Supply 230V] --> B[Electrical Appliance]

    C -- Yes --> E[Appliance Metal Body Becomes Live]
    E --> F[Earth Conductor GI Strip or Wire]
    F --> G[Earth Electrode GI Pipe in Pit]
    G --> H[Charcoal and Salt Backfill Layer]
    H --> I[Moist Soil with Low Resistivity]
    I --> J[General Mass of Earth at 0V Potential]
    J --> K[Fault Current Returns to Source Neutral]
    K --> L[Protective Device MCB Trips]
    L --> M[Supply Isolated Fault Cleared]
    E -.-> N[Human Touch Risk Without Earthing]
    N --> O[Electric Shock Hazard]
```
