Electrolytic 1000uF 25V] B3[Smoothing Inductor L1
Iron Core 10H] end subgraph RegulationStage["Voltage Regulation"] C1[Zener Diode or Regulator IC 7805] C2[Output Capacitor C2
Ceramic 0.1uF for Decoupling] C3[Load Resistor RL] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C2 -. Decoupling .-> C1 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Transformer** (passive, magnetic) steps down 230 V AC to a lower AC voltage. - **Bridge Rectifier** (active, diodes) converts AC to pulsating DC. - **Capacitor C1** (passive, electric field) smooths the pulsations — stores energy during peaks, releases during troughs. - **Inductor L1** (passive, magnetic field) further filters ripple current. - **Zener/Regulator IC** (active) maintains a constant output voltage. - **Capacitor C2** (passive) decouples high-frequency noise from the load. - **RL** represents the load resistance of the circuit being powered. --- ## 4.4 Schematic Symbols Reference > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Practical Exam Tip:** Every lab exam asks you to **identify** the schematic symbol and the physical component. Memorize the following. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Schematic Symbols Reference] --> B[Resistor R] A --> C[Capacitor C] A --> D[Inductor L] A --> E[Variable Resistor] A --> F[Polarized Capacitor] A --> G[Variable Capacitor] A --> H[Iron Core Inductor] A --> I[Air Core Inductor] B --> B1[Symbol: Zigzag line or Rectangle] C --> C1[Symbol: Two parallel lines with gap] D --> D1[Symbol: Series of loops or coils] E --> E1[Symbol: Resistor with arrow across] F --> F1[Symbol: One straight line, one curved line with plus minus] G --> G1[Symbol: Two lines with diagonal arrow] H --> H1[Symbol: Inductor loops with parallel lines under] I --> I1[Symbol: Inductor loops with no lines under] ``` --- # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Guideline:** Part A questions are short, definition-based, and require crisp, precise answers. Aim for 2-3 sentences with a clear example. --- ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]** **"Differentiate between active and passive electronic components. Give two examples of each."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Active Components** are those that can **generate, amplify, or control** electrical signals and require an external power source for their operation. They have the ability to provide **power gain** (output power greater than input power). Examples: **Transistor (BJT/FET)**, **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)**, **Diode (when forward biased)**. **Passive Components** are those that **cannot generate or amplify** energy. They can only **receive, store, or dissipate** electrical energy and do not require an external power supply. They cannot provide power gain. Examples: **Resistor**, **Capacitor**, **Inductor**. | Parameter | Active | Passive | |---|---|---| | External power | Required | Not required | | Power gain | $> 1$ | $< 1$ | | Energy | Generates/amplifies | Stores/dissipates | **Examiner's Note:** Award full 3 marks if all three distinctions + two examples each are clearly stated. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]** **"State the colour code for a 4-band resistor with the following bands: Yellow, Violet, Red, Gold. Also write the resistor value in Ω, kΩ, and MΩ."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Given:** Yellow – Violet – Red – Gold | Band | Color | Significance | |---|---|---| | 1st Band | Yellow | 1st digit = **4** | | 2nd Band | Violet | 2nd digit = **7** | | 3rd Band | Red | Multiplier = $\times 10^2$ | | 4th Band | Gold | Tolerance = **±5%** | **Calculation:** $$R = 47 \times 10^2 = 4700\ \Omega$$ Converting the units: $$R = 4700\ \Omega = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega = 0.0047\ \text{M}\Omega$$ **Tolerance Application:** $$R = 4700\ \Omega \pm 5\% = [4465\ \Omega,\ 4935\ \Omega]$$ $$\boxed{R = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%}$$ **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark each for correct digit identification, multiplier application, and unit conversion. Mentioning tolerance gets the third mark. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions are 14 marks each with internal choice (Q-A or Q-B). Each part typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Provide a single comprehensive answer for the selected choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]** **Q.A. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **capacitor**. Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with a dielectric medium. *(7 marks)* **Q.A. (b)** Explain the **different types of capacitors** based on the dielectric material used, and state **two applications** of each type. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution for Q.A. (a):** **Construction:** A capacitor consists of **two conducting plates** separated by an **insulating medium** called the **dielectric**. The plates are typically made of aluminum, copper, or tin, and the dielectric can be air, ceramic, mica, paper, plastic film, or an oxide layer. The plates are connected to two external terminals through which charge can flow in and out of the device. **Working Principle:** When a voltage source is connected across the plates, electrons accumulate on the plate connected to the negative terminal of the source, and an equal number of electrons are repelled from the other plate, leaving it with a net positive charge. This creates a uniform **electric field** between the plates, perpendicular to the plate surfaces, with energy stored in this field. **Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.4 for standard schematic symbol. **Derivation (Exhaustive):** Consider a parallel-plate capacitor with: - Plate area = $A\ (\text{m}^2)$ - Plate separation = $d\ (\text{m})$ - Dielectric medium with relative permittivity = $\varepsilon_r$ - Permittivity of free space = $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ **Step 1 — Charge and Surface Charge Density:** If charge $+Q$ is on one plate and $-Q$ on the other: $$\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$$ **Step 2 — Electric Field Between the Plates (Gauss's Law):** For a uniform field between two infinite parallel plates: $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ **Step 3 — Voltage Between the Plates:** $$V = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \cdot d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ **Step 4 — Capacitance:** By definition: $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q \cdot \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{Q \cdot d}$$ $$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$ **Conclusion:** Capacitance is directly proportional to the dielectric constant and plate area, and inversely proportional to the distance between the plates. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A. (b):** **Types of Capacitors Based on Dielectric:** | Type | Dielectric Material | Capacitance Range | Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **Ceramic Capacitor** | Barium Titanate, other ceramics | 1 pF – 1 μF | Decoupling, bypass, high-frequency filtering in PCBs | | **Electrolytic Capacitor** | Aluminum oxide film | 1 μF – 1 F | Power supply filtering, audio coupling, energy storage | | **Tantalum Capacitor** | Tantalum pentoxide | 0.1 μF – 1000 μF | Compact designs, mobile phones, military equipment | | **Mica Capacitor** | Mica (natural mineral) | 1 pF – 1000 pF | RF circuits, high-stability oscillators, precision filters | | **Film Capacitor** | Polyester, Polypropylene, Polystyrene | 1 nF – 100 μF | Audio circuits, power electronics, motor run capacitors | | **Paper Capacitor** | Waxed paper | 1 nF – 10 μF | Legacy circuits, low-frequency applications | | **Air Capacitor** | Air | 10 pF – 500 pF | Variable tuning in radios, RF applications | | **Supercapacitor** | Activated carbon / electrolyte | 1 F – 5000 F | Memory backup, energy harvesting, EV regenerative braking | **Key Application Example:** A **0.1 μF ceramic capacitor** is placed as close as possible to the VCC pin of every digital IC on a PCB to decouple switching noise — this is a **mandatory design practice** in KTU-affiliated electronics labs. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Internal Choice Pattern]** **Q.B. (a)** What is a **resistor**? Explain the **types of fixed and variable resistors** with their applications. Also explain the **4-band and 5-band color coding systems** with suitable examples. *(8 marks)* **Q.B. (b)** What are the **standard E12 and E24 resistance values**? Why are these preferred number series used in electronics manufacturing? *(6 marks)* #### **Model Solution for Q.B. (a):** **Definition of Resistor:** A resistor is a two-terminal passive electronic component that **opposes the flow of electric current** and dissipates electrical energy in the form of heat, in accordance with Ohm's Law $V = I \cdot R$. **Types of Fixed Resistors:** 1. **Carbon Composition Resistor:** - Made by mixing carbon powder with a ceramic binder. - Tolerance: ±10% to ±20% (low precision). - Application: Legacy circuits, surge protection. 2. **Carbon Film Resistor:** - A thin layer of carbon film deposited on a ceramic substrate. - Tolerance: ±5% (general purpose). - Application: Most common in consumer electronics. 3. **Metal Film Resistor:** - A thin metal alloy (nichrome) film deposited on a ceramic rod. - Tolerance: ±1% or better (high precision). - Application: Precision analog circuits, instrumentation, audio equipment. 4. **Wire Wound Resistor:** - Resistance wire (nichrome) wound on a ceramic core. - Tolerance: ±1% to ±5%, high power ratings (5 W to 100+ W). - Application: Power supplies, current shunts, braking resistors. **Types of Variable Resistors:** 1. **Potentiometer (3-terminal):** - Acts as an adjustable voltage divider. - Application: Volume control, brightness control, calibration trims. 2. **Rheostat (2-terminal):** - Used in series to control current. - Application: Motor speed control, dimmer switches, laboratory variable loads. 3. **Trimmer / Trimpot:** - Small, screwdriver-adjustable, mounted on PCBs. - Application: Factory calibration, fine-tuning of circuit parameters. 4. **Digital Potentiometer:** - Electronically controlled by digital signals (I²C, SPI). - Application: Microcontroller-based systems, audio volume ICs. **4-Band Color Code System:** The first three bands give the resistance value, and the fourth gives the tolerance. **Example:** **Red – Red – Orange – Gold** | Band | Color | Value | |---|---|---| | 1st | Red | 2 | | 2nd | Red | 2 | | 3rd | Orange | $\times 10^3$ | | 4th | Gold | ±5% | **Solution:** $R = 22 \times 10^3 = 22{,}000\ \Omega = 22\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%$ **5-Band Color Code System:** Used for high-precision resistors. The first three bands give three significant digits. **Example:** **Brown – Green – Black – Red – Brown** | Band | Color | Value | |---|---|---| | 1st | Brown | 1 | | 2nd | Green | 5 | | 3rd | Black | 0 | | 4th | Red | $\times 10^2$ | | 5th | Brown | ±1% | **Solution:** $R = 150 \times 10^2 = 15{,}000\ \Omega = 15\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 1\%$ --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B. (b):** **Standard Resistance Values — E-Series:** The **E-series** are geometrically spaced preferred numbers standardized by the IEC (International Electrotechnical Commission). They are derived using the formula: $$V_n = 10^{n/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$ where $N$ is the series identifier (E6 → 6 values, E12 → 12 values, etc.). **E12 Series (±10% Tolerance):** $$1.0,\ 1.2,\ 1.5,\ 1.8,\ 2.2,\ 2.7,\ 3.3,\ 3.9,\ 4.7,\ 5.6,\ 6.8,\ 8.2$$ **E24 Series (±5% Tolerance):** $$1.0,\ 1.1,\ 1.2,\ 1.3,\ 1.5,\ 1.6,\ 1.8,\ 2.0,\ 2.2,\ 2.4,\ 2.7,\ 3.0,\ 3.3,\ 3.6,\ 3.9,\ 4.3,\ 4.7,\ 5.1,\ 5.6,\ 6.2,\ 6.8,\ 7.5,\ 8.2,\ 9.1$$ **Why Preferred Number Series Are Used:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 3-mark trick — students often lose marks here. The answer requires THREE reasons:** 1. **Tolerance Coverage:** The geometric spacing ensures that for any value the actual resistance (within tolerance) lies within the tolerance range of the next standard value. For example, in the E12 series, a $1.0\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor with +10% tolerance reaches $1.1\ \text{k}\Omega$, which is the next preferred value — there are no "gaps" in coverage. 2. **Manufacturing Economy:** Using a limited set of standard values allows manufacturers to produce components in bulk at lower cost, while customers can obtain any desired value with acceptable tolerance. 3. **Logarithmic Distribution:** The geometric progression of values mirrors the **logarithmic nature of human hearing** (decibels), signal levels, and frequency response, making the series natural for electronic design. 4. **Interchangeability:** Standardization allows components from different manufacturers to be used interchangeably without circuit redesign. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams (Module 3):** | # | Common Student Mistake | Marks Lost | KTU Examiner's Comment | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Forgetting to **state the units** (Ω, kΩ, MΩ) when solving color code problems. | 0.5 to 1 mark | "Numerical answer is correct, but units are missing." | | 2 | Confusing **4-band and 5-band** resistor color codes — using only 2 significant digits for a 5-band resistor. | 2 to 3 marks | "Incorrect interpretation of band sequence." | | 3 | Writing **tolerance as a value, not a percentage range** (e.g., writing $R = 4700 + 235$ instead of $4.7\ \text{k}\Omega \pm 5\%$). | 1 mark | "Tolerance expression format is incorrect." | | 4 | Mixing up **active/passive classification** — claiming **diode is purely passive** or **resistor is active**. | 1 to 2 marks | "Definition of active/passive is partially incorrect." | | 5 | Forgetting to mention the **dielectric constant** in the parallel-plate capacitor derivation. | 1 mark | "Derivation incomplete; missing key parameter." | | 6 | Stating the formula for **series resistance** as $R_{eq} = R_1 \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ (which is parallel formula). | 1 to 2 marks | "Series-parallel confusion." | | 7 | Not providing **two applications** of each component type (only one is given). | 1 mark | "Incomplete application list." | | 8 | Writing the **Mnemonic in a jumbled order** that doesn't correspond to the colors. | 0.5 mark | "Mnemonic order is incorrect." | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:** > 1. **Always write the formula first** before substituting values. > 2. **Always include units** in the final answer. > 3. **State the tolerance as a percentage** (e.g., ±5%) and the **actual value range** in the same answer. > 4. **Use proper subsections** (a), (b), (c) in long answers — examiners award partial marks section-wise. > 5. **Draw diagrams** even for theoretical questions — a neat, labeled diagram can fetch 1 to 2 extra marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall. ### 🔹 Active vs. Passive - **Active components** need an external power source and can provide **power gain** (transistor, op-amp, diode). - **Passive components** do not need an external power source and cannot provide power gain (resistor, capacitor, inductor). - **Diode** is a special case — often considered a **separate category** in many textbooks because it can be classified as either active or passive depending on its role in the circuit. ### 🔹 Resistors (R) - Unit: **Ω (Ohm)**, $1\ \text{M}\Omega = 10^6\ \Omega$, $1\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^3\ \Omega$. - **V-I relation:** $V = IR$ (linear). - **Power dissipated:** $P = I^2 R = V^2 / R$ (always positive — energy lost as heat). - **Types:** Fixed (carbon film, metal film, wire wound) and Variable (potentiometer, rheostat, trimpot). - **Color code (4-band):** Digit – Digit – Multiplier – Tolerance. - **Color code (5-band):** Digit – Digit – Digit – Multiplier – Tolerance. - **Mnemonic:** "B.B. ROY of Great Britain had a Very Good Wife" → Black, Brown, Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue, Violet, Grey, White. - **E12 standard values** per decade: 1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, 8.2. ### 🔹 Capacitors (C) - Unit: **F (Farad)**, $1\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$, $1\ \text{nF} = 10^{-9}\ \text{F}$, $1\ \text{pF} = 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$. - **V-Q relation:** $Q = CV$ (linear). - **V-I relation:** $i = C \cdot (dv/dt)$ — current flows only when voltage **changes**. - **Energy stored:** $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} CV^2$ (in electric field). - **Plate capacitor formula:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. - **Types:** Polarized (electrolytic, tantalum) and Non-polarized (ceramic, mica, film). - **Polarity:** Connecting electrolytic capacitors in reverse causes **explosion** — always check the **stripe** marking the negative terminal. ### 🔹 Inductors (L) - Unit: **H (Henry)**, $1\ \text{mH} = 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$, $1\ \mu\text{H} = 10^{-6}\ \text{H}$. - **Flux-current relation:** $\lambda = LI$ (linear). - **V-I relation:** $v = L \cdot (di/dt)$ — voltage appears only when current **changes**. - **Energy stored:** $E_L = \dfrac{1}{2} LI^2$ (in magnetic field). - **Solenoid formula:** $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A}{\ell}$. - **Types:** Air core, iron core, ferrite core, toroidal core. - **Key Specs:** Inductance, DCR (DC Resistance), rated current, SRF (Self-Resonant Frequency), Q-factor. ### 🔹 Quick-Reference Numerical Conversions - $1\ \text{V} = 1\ \text{J/C}$ - $1\ \text{F} = 1\ \text{C/V}$ - $1\ \text{H} = 1\ \text{Wb/A} = 1\ \text{V·s/A}$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ (permittivity of free space) - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} \approx 1.257 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/m}$ (permeability of free space) ### 🔹 Series-Parallel Quick Rules - **Resistors:** Series → Add, Parallel → Reciprocal Add. - **Capacitors:** Series → Reciprocal Add, Parallel → Add (opposite to resistors). - **Inductors:** Same as resistors — Series → Add, Parallel → Reciprocal Add. ### 🔹 Key Identifiers to Memorize - **Resistor symbol** — Zigzag line (American) or rectangle (European). - **Capacitor symbol** — Two parallel lines (non-polarized), one straight + one curved (polarized). - **Inductor symbol** — Series of loops or humps; core indicated by parallel lines beneath. > **Final Exam Mantra:** *"R dissipates, C stores in E-field, L stores in B-field. Color bands = digits. Tolerance = gold/silver = 5%/10%. E12 = 12 values per decade."* — Memorize this and you have 70% of Module 3 covered. ---

topics.title: PN Junction Diode: Principle of Operation, V-I Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PN Junction Diode: Principle of Operation, V-I Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **PN Junction Diode** is a two-terminal, asymmetrically doped semiconductor device formed by fusing a P-type semiconductor (excess holes as majority carriers) with an N-type semiconductor (excess electrons as majority carriers) within a single crystal lattice. It is a unidirectional current-conducting element that exhibits low resistance under **forward bias** and very high resistance under **reverse bias**, governed by the **Schockley Diode Equation**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The device is treated as a *non-linear, active two-terminal* element. Students must clearly distinguish between *static* and *dynamic* (AC) resistance on the V-I curve, a frequently asked 3-mark question in End Semester Evaluations (ESE). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **one-way swing door** in a busy corridor. People can push through easily from one side (Forward Bias), but the door firmly resists entry from the opposite side (Reverse Bias) — unless a sufficiently strong crowd pushes hard enough to break the door (Breakdown Region). A PN junction diode behaves identically: it allows current to flow in one direction only. - **P-side** = the "exit door" side (Anode) - **N-side** = the "entry door" side (Cathode) - **Forward Bias** = the battery is connected to "push" the door open (+ to P, – to N) - **Reverse Bias** = the battery is connected to "hold the door shut" (+ to N, – to P) ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant / Metric | Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Boltzmann Constant ($k$) | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$ | Fundamental | | Charge of Electron ($q$) | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$ | Fundamental | | Silicon Barrier Potential ($V_0$) | **0.7 V** | Standard at 300 K | | Germanium Barrier Potential ($V_0$) | **0.3 V** | Standard at 300 K | | Thermal Voltage ($V_T$) | **25.85 mV** | At $T = 300 \text{ K}$ | | Ideality Factor ($\eta$) | 1 to 2 | 1 = ideal, 2 = heavily recomb. | > [!IMPORTANT] > All standard calculations in KTU 2024 papers assume **Silicon (Si)** unless explicitly stated otherwise, with $V_0 \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ and $T = 300 \text{ K}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Formation of the PN Junction (Step-by-Step Logic) When P-type and N-type semiconductors are joined: 1. **Majority Carrier Diffusion:** Free electrons from the N-side diffuse into the P-side; holes from the P-side diffuse into the N-side. This diffusion is driven by the concentration gradient. 2. **Recombination at the Junction:** Near the metallurgical junction, electrons and holes recombine, annihilating mobile carriers and leaving behind **immobile ionized dopant atoms** (positive ions on the N-side, negative ions on the P-side). 3. **Depletion Region Formation:** The space charge region (called the **depletion region** or **space-charge layer**) becomes depleted of mobile carriers, behaving like an insulating region. 4. **Built-in / Barrier Potential ($V_0$):** An electric field is established across the depletion region by the ionized dopants. This field opposes further diffusion of majority carriers and establishes equilibrium. 5. **Equilibrium:** The diffusion force equals the drift force, and net current across the junction is zero (without external bias). ### 2.2 Operating Modes of a PN Junction Diode The diode has three distinct operating regions: - **Zero Bias (Equilibrium):** No net current; built-in barrier $V_0$ present. - **Forward Bias:** External voltage $V_F$ *reduces* the barrier, allowing massive majority carrier injection once $V_F > V_0$. - **Reverse Bias:** External voltage *increases* the barrier, widening the depletion region; only a tiny reverse saturation current $I_0$ (due to thermally generated minority carriers) flows. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Sheet for ESE — memorize all entries below.** | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = I_0 \left( e^{V / \eta V_T} - 1 \right)$ | **Schockley Diode Equation** (Current) | A; $V$ = applied voltage | | 2 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal Voltage (exact) | V; $T$ in Kelvin | | 3 | $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ | Thermal Voltage (room temp, 300 K) | V; standard approximation | | 4 | $V_T = T / 11600$ | Alternate form of thermal voltage | V; with $T$ in K | | 5 | $W = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \epsilon_s V_b}{q} \left( \dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D} \right) }$ | Depletion width | meters; $V_b$ = total reverse bias | | 6 | $r_f = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = \dfrac{dV}{dI}$ | **Dynamic (AC) Forward Resistance** | $\Omega$ | | 7 | $r_f = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_d}$ | Simplified dynamic resistance | $\Omega$ (for $\eta = 1$) | | 8 | $R_{DC} = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Static (DC) Resistance | $\Omega$ | | 9 | $r_f = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_d \text{ (mA)}}$ | Quick dynamic resistance formula | $\Omega$ | | 10 | $C_T = \dfrac{C_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_0}}}$ | Transition (Junction) Capacitance | F; for reverse-biased diode | **Key Notes for Exam Use:** - $\eta = 1$ for an ideal diode (KTU default unless otherwise stated). - For $V > V_0$ (forward bias), the $-1$ term in the Schockley equation becomes negligible, simplifying to $I \approx I_0 \cdot e^{V / \eta V_T}$. - For $V \ll 0$ (heavy reverse bias), $I \approx -I_0$ (constant reverse saturation current). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The PN junction diode is the **fundamental building block** of virtually every electronic system. Its applications include: - **Rectification:** Half-wave and full-wave rectifiers in DC power supplies. - **Signal Demodulation:** AM/FM radio receivers. - **Voltage Regulation:** Zener diodes (operated in reverse breakdown). - **Switching:** Logic gates, digital circuits, switching power supplies. - **Light Emission:** LEDs (forward-biased PN junctions). - **Photodetection:** Photodiodes and solar cells (reverse-biased PN junctions). - **Oscillators & Frequency Multipliers:** Varactor diodes (using junction capacitance). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Schockley Diode Equation The Schockley diode equation describes the current-voltage relationship of a PN junction. We derive it from first principles: **Step 1:** Consider the diode under forward bias. The applied voltage $V$ reduces the built-in barrier $V_0$. The number of majority carriers crossing the junction follows a Boltzmann distribution proportional to $e^{+V/\eta V_T}$, where $V_T = kT/q$ is the thermal voltage. **Step 2:** The number of minority carriers crossing the junction (which is independent of applied voltage, being limited by thermal generation) is proportional to $e^{-V_0/\eta V_T}$. **Step 3:** The net current is the difference between these two flows, scaled by the reverse saturation current $I_0$: $$I = I_0 \left( e^{+V/\eta V_T} - e^{-V_0/\eta V_T} \right)$$ **Step 4:** At equilibrium ($V = 0$), the net current must be zero. This requires the two terms to be equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, which they are by construction (the second term becomes 1). After imposing this boundary condition, the equation simplifies to the **Schockley Diode Equation**: $$I = I_0 \left( e^{V / \eta V_T} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $V_T = kT/q$ explicitly: $$I = I_0 \left( e^{qV / \eta k T} - 1 \right)$$ **Step 6:** The **ideality factor** $\eta$ accounts for recombination in the depletion region: - $\eta = 1$ → ideal diode (diffusion current dominant, as in low-doped Si). - $\eta = 2$ → significant recombination in the space-charge region. ### 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Voltage $V_T$ **Step 1:** Start with the kinetic theory definition: at thermal equilibrium, the average thermal energy per degree of freedom is $\tfrac{1}{2} kT$. **Step 2:** Equate thermal energy to electrical energy for a single electron crossing a potential $V_T$: $$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = q V_T$$ **Step 3:** The statistical mean kinetic energy across a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution gives an effective energy of $kT$ (not $\tfrac{1}{2}kT$), leading to: $$kT = qV_T$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $V_T$: $$V_T = \frac{kT}{q}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute standard values at $T = 300 \text{ K}$: $$V_T = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$V_T = 2.585 \times 10^{-2} \text{ V} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: V-I Characteristics — Region by Region | Region | Bias Condition | Physical Phenomenon | Current Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | **O → A** (Cut-off) | $V < V_0$ (Forward, below cut-in) | Barrier not overcome; negligible current | $I \approx 0$ ($\mu$A range) | | **A → B** (Forward Conduction) | $V \geq V_0$ (Forward) | Majority carrier injection; exponential rise | $I$ rises exponentially (mA to A) | | **B → C** (Reverse Saturation) | $V < 0$ (Reverse, below breakdown) | Minority carrier drift; constant $I_0$ | $I \approx -I_0$ ($\mu$A or nA) | | **C → D** (Breakdown) | $V \leq -V_{BR}$ (Reverse, beyond breakdown) | Zener/Avalanche multiplication | $I$ rises sharply (mA) | ### 3.4 Boundary Conditions for the Schockley Equation | Condition | Approximation | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | $V \gg V_T$ (e.g., $V > 0.1$ V) | $I \approx I_0 \, e^{V/\eta V_T}$ | The $-1$ term is negligible | | $V \ll -V_T$ (e.g., $V < -0.1$ V) | $I \approx -I_0$ | Exponential term tends to zero | | $V = 0$ | $I = 0$ | Equilibrium | | $V = +\eta V_T \ln 2$ | $I = I_0$ | Useful reference point | ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A silicon PN junction diode has $I_0 = 10 \text{ nA}$ and is operated at 300 K. Calculate the diode current when $V = 0.7 \text{ V}$ (assume $\eta = 1$). **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = 26 \text{ mV} = 0.026 \text{ V}$. **Step 2:** Compute the exponent: $$\frac{V}{\eta V_T} = \frac{0.7}{1 \times 0.026} = 26.923$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential: $$e^{26.923} = 4.81 \times 10^{11}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the Schockley equation: $$I = 10 \times 10^{-9} \times (4.81 \times 10^{11} - 1)$$ $$I \approx 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 4.81 \times 10^{11}$$ $$I \approx 4.81 \times 10^{3} \text{ A} = 4.81 \text{ kA}$$ > [!NOTE] > This very large result reflects the **exponential sensitivity** of diode current to applied voltage. KTU exam problems will typically use $\eta = 2$ or smaller voltages to keep answers realistic. **Re-evaluation with $\eta = 2$:** $$I = 10 \text{ nA} \times e^{0.7 / (2 \times 0.026)} = 10 \text{ nA} \times e^{13.46} = 7.05 \times 10^{5} \times 10^{-9} = 0.705 \text{ mA}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PN Junction Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["P TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR
Holes Majority"]:::pType nodeB["DEPLETION REGION
Immobile Ions + Barrier"]:::depl nodeC["N TYPE SEMICONDUCTOR
Electrons Majority"]:::nType nodeD["FORWARD BIAS
Vf greater than V0"]:::fwd nodeE["REVERSE BIAS
Vr increases barrier"]:::rev nodeF["MAJORITY CARRIER INJECTION
Current rises exponentially"]:::currentF nodeG["MINORITY CARRIER DRIFT
Current is Is minus Io"]:::currentR nodeH["SCHOCKLEY EQUATION
I equals Io times exp"]:::formula nodeI["BREAKDOWN REGION
V less than minus VBR"]:::break nodeJ["ZENER EFFECT
Heavy Doping"]:::zen nodeK["AVALANCHE EFFECT
Light Doping"]:::aval nodeL["V-I OUTPUT CURVE
Exponential forward + breakdown reverse"]:::curve nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeL nodeE --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeL nodeK --> nodeL classDef pType fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 classDef nType fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef depl fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef fwd fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef rev fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef currentF fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef currentR fill:#FAA0A0,stroke:#A52A2A,color:#000 classDef formula fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef break fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef zen fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000 classDef aval fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 classDef curve fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic: Forward and Reverse Bias Configuration ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FWD [FORWARD BIAS] A1["Battery Positive"] --> A2["P Side Anode"] A3["N Side Cathode"] --> A4["Battery Negative"] A2 <--> A3 end subgraph REV [REVERSE BIAS] B1["Battery Positive"] --> B2["N Side Cathode"] B3["P Side Anode"] --> B4["Battery Negative"] B2 <--> B3 end noteA["Barrier reduced
Depletion shrinks
Current flows"] -.-> FWD noteB["Barrier increased
Depletion widens
Negligible current"] -.-> REV ``` ### 4.3 V-I Characteristic Curve (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AXES [AXES] AX["X-AXIS: Voltage V"] AY["Y-AXIS: Current I"] end subgraph ORIGIN [ORIGIN 0,0] O["Equilibrium Point"] end subgraph FORWARD [FORWARD QUADRANT FIRST] F1["Cut-in Voltage Vk around 0.7 V Silicon"] F2["Exponential Rise Region"] F3["Operating Point Q"] end subgraph REVERSE [REVERSE QUADRANT THIRD] R1["Reverse Saturation Io around nA"] R2["Constant Current Plateau"] R3["Breakdown Voltage minus VBR"] R4["Zener or Avalanche Drop"] end O --> F1 F1 --> F2 F2 --> F3 O --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R4 classDef axisStyle fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#8B7D6B,color:#000 classDef forwardStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef reverseStyle fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef originStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 class AX,AY axisStyle class F1,F2,F3 forwardStyle class R1,R2,R3,R4 reverseStyle class O originStyle ``` ### 4.4 Energy Band Diagram (Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ZERO_BIAS [ZERO BIAS EQUILIBRIUM] P1["P side Fermi Level EF near Valence"] N1["N side Fermi Level EF near Conduction"] J1["Junction Barrier V0 = 0.7 V"] end subgraph FWD_BIAS [FORWARD BIAS] P2["P side EF shifts up"] N2["N side EF shifts down"] J2["Reduced Barrier V0 minus V"] end subgraph REV_BIAS [REVERSE BIAS] P3["P side EF shifts down"] N3["N side EF shifts up"] J3["Increased Barrier V0 plus VR"] end ZERO_BIAS --> FWD_BIAS ZERO_BIAS --> REV_BIAS ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Barrier Potential" in a PN junction diode. How does it differ between Silicon and Germanium?* **Model Answer:** The **Barrier Potential** ($V_0$) is the built-in electric potential across the depletion region that opposes further diffusion of majority carriers at equilibrium. It arises from the ionized dopant atoms on either side of the metallurgical junction. For **Silicon (Si)**: $V_0 \approx \mathbf{0.7 \text{ V}}$ For **Germanium (Ge)**: $V_0 \approx \mathbf{0.3 \text{ V}}$ The difference is due to the **bandgap energy** ($E_g$): Silicon has $E_g = 1.1 \text{ eV}$ while Germanium has $E_g = 0.67 \text{ eV}$. A larger bandgap yields a higher barrier potential. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Distinguish between static (DC) resistance and dynamic (AC) resistance of a PN junction diode.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Static Resistance ($R_{DC}$) | Dynamic Resistance ($r_f$) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Ratio of DC voltage to DC current at an operating point | Ratio of small change in voltage to small change in current | | **Formula** | $R_{DC} = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | $r_f = \dfrac{dV}{dI} = \dfrac{\eta V_T}{I_d}$ | | **Value** | Varies (typically large) | Very small (few ohms in forward bias) | | **Application** | DC load-line analysis | Small-signal AC analysis | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Forward-Bias Operation & Quantitative Analysis* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a)** (7 Marks): *With the help of a neat diagram, explain the formation of a PN junction diode. Define the depletion region and barrier potential. Mention the values of barrier potential for Si and Ge at 300 K.* **Model Solution:** When a P-type semiconductor (doped with acceptor impurities like Boron) is joined to an N-type semiconductor (doped with donor impurities like Phosphorus), majority carriers diffuse across the junction due to the concentration gradient. - Holes from P-side diffuse into N-side and recombine with electrons. - Electrons from N-side diffuse into P-side and recombine with holes. This recombination leaves behind a region near the junction devoid of mobile carriers, called the **Depletion Region** (or Space-Charge Region). It contains only **immobile ionized dopant atoms**: - Negative acceptor ions on the P-side - Positive donor ions on the N-side These ions establish an **electric field** and a corresponding **barrier potential** $V_0$ that opposes further diffusion. At equilibrium, the **drift current due to minority carriers** exactly balances the **diffusion current of majority carriers**, and net current is zero. **Barrier Potential values at 300 K:** - **Silicon (Si):** $V_0 \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ - **Germanium (Ge):** $V_0 \approx 0.3 \text{ V}$ *Diagram:* Refer to the P-N junction with depletion region marked (refer to Section 4.1 flowchart for functional representation). --- **Part (b)** (7 Marks): *A silicon PN junction diode has reverse saturation current $I_0 = 2 \mu A$ at 300 K. For an applied forward voltage of 0.6 V, calculate: (i) the diode current assuming $\eta = 1$, and (ii) the dynamic resistance at this operating point. Given $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$.* **Model Solution:** **Given:** $I_0 = 2 \mu A = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$, $V = 0.6 \text{ V}$, $\eta = 1$, $V_T = 26 \text{ mV} = 0.026 \text{ V}$. **(i) Diode Current:** $$I = I_0 \left( e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$$ $$I = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times \left( e^{0.6 / (1 \times 0.026)} - 1 \right)$$ $$I = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times \left( e^{23.077} - 1 \right)$$ $$I = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times \left( 1.025 \times 10^{10} - 1 \right)$$ $$I \approx 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.025 \times 10^{10} = 2.05 \times 10^{4} \text{ A} = 20.5 \text{ kA}$$ > [!NOTE] > The result is unrealistically large because $\eta = 1$ at 0.6 V implies the diode is in extreme conduction. KTU problems may use $\eta = 2$ to yield a more realistic value: > > $$I = 2 \mu A \times e^{0.6 / (2 \times 0.026)} = 2 \mu A \times e^{11.54} = 2 \mu A \times 10378 \approx 20.76 \text{ mA}$$ **(ii) Dynamic Resistance:** $$r_f = \frac{\eta V_T}{I_d} = \frac{1 \times 0.026}{20.5 \times 10^{3}} = 1.27 \times 10^{-6} \ \Omega = 1.27 \ \mu\Omega$$ For $\eta = 2$ case: $r_f = \dfrac{2 \times 0.026}{20.76 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \ \Omega$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *V-I Characteristics & Reverse-Bias Analysis* **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Part (a)** (7 Marks): *Draw and explain the complete V-I characteristics of a PN junction diode. Clearly mark the following regions: (i) Forward bias, (ii) Cut-in voltage, (iii) Reverse saturation current, and (iv) Breakdown region.* **Model Solution:** The V-I characteristics are plotted with forward voltage $V_F$ on the positive x-axis and forward current $I_F$ on the positive y-axis. For reverse operation, the third quadrant shows $V_R$ as negative and $I_R$ as a small negative value. **(i) Forward Bias Region (First Quadrant):** - For $V_F < V_0$ (cut-in voltage), the diode current is negligibly small ($\mu$A range). - At $V_F = V_0$, the diode "turns on" and current rises sharply. - The current follows the Schockley exponential: $I = I_0 \, e^{V/\eta V_T}$. - $V_0 \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$ for Si and $0.3 \text{ V}$ for Ge. **(ii) Cut-in Voltage / Knee Voltage:** - The point on the V-I curve where the diode begins to conduct appreciably. - Below this voltage, the diode is in the **cut-off** region. - $V_{cut-in} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ (Si), $0.3 \text{ V}$ (Ge). **(iii) Reverse Saturation Current (Third Quadrant):** - A small, constant current $I_0$ flows due to **thermally generated minority carriers**. - This is typically in the **$\mu$A (Ge)** or **nA (Si)** range. - It is independent of reverse voltage magnitude (until breakdown). **(iv) Breakdown Region:** - Occurs at the **Breakdown Voltage $V_{BR}$** (e.g., $-50 \text{ V}$). - Current rises sharply with negligible change in voltage. - **Zener Effect** (heavy doping, low $V_{BR}$): Quantum tunneling across the narrow depletion region. - **Avalanche Effect** (light doping, high $V_{BR}$): Impact ionization of carriers by the strong electric field. *Diagram:* Refer to Section 4.3 for the block-level representation of the V-I curve. --- **Part (b)** (7 Marks): *Compare Zener breakdown and Avalanche breakdown mechanisms. State the temperature coefficient behavior of each.* **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Quantum mechanical **tunneling** of valence band electrons through the narrow depletion region to the conduction band | **Impact ionization**: carriers gain enough kinetic energy to create new electron-hole pairs by collision | | **Doping** | Heavy doping on both sides | Light to moderate doping | | **Depletion Width** | Very narrow ($\sim 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$) | Wide ($\sim 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$) | | **Breakdown Voltage** | Low ($< 5 \text{ V}$ typically) | High ($> 6 \text{ V}$ typically) | | **Temperature Coefficient** | **Negative** (breakdown voltage decreases with temperature) | **Positive** (breakdown voltage increases with temperature) | | **Junction Type** | Abrupt PN junction | Linearly graded PN junction | | **Application** | Low-voltage Zener regulators | High-voltage Zener regulators, TVS diodes | **Key Insight:** At around $V_{BR} \approx 5.6 \text{ V}$, both effects occur and the temperature coefficient is approximately **zero** — useful for temperature-stable voltage references. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):** > 1. **Forgetting to convert $V_T$:** Always use $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K. Forgetting to convert to Volts in exponent calculations is the #1 source of mark loss. > 2. **Confusing $I_0$ and $I$:** $I_0$ is the **reverse saturation current** (very small, in nA–$\mu$A), while $I$ is the **forward diode current** (in mA or A). > 3. **Using the wrong $\eta$:** KTU 2024 default is $\eta = 1$ for an ideal diode. Always read the problem statement carefully. > 4. **Mixing static and dynamic resistance:** Static resistance uses $R = V/I$; dynamic resistance uses $r_f = \eta V_T / I$. These are **never equal** except at one specific point. > 5. **Missing the cut-in voltage check:** Always state the cut-in voltage ($0.7 \text{ V}$ for Si) when plotting or explaining V-I characteristics. > 6. **Units in $r_f$ formula:** Use $V_T$ in **Volts** and $I_d$ in **Amperes** to get $r_f$ in **Ohms**. A common error is mixing mA and V. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):** - **PN Junction Diode** is formed by joining P-type and N-type semiconductors in a single crystal. - **Depletion region** is a thin insulating layer near the junction, free of mobile carriers. - **Barrier Potential ($V_0$):** $0.7 \text{ V}$ for Si, $0.3 \text{ V}$ for Ge (at 300 K). - **Forward bias** reduces the barrier → current flows exponentially. - **Reverse bias** increases the barrier → only reverse saturation current $I_0$ flows. - **Cut-in / Knee voltage** is the forward voltage at which the diode starts conducting. - **Schockley Equation:** $I = I_0 \left( e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$. - **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K. - **Dynamic resistance:** $r_f = \eta V_T / I_d$ — small in forward bias. - **Static resistance:** $R_{DC} = V/I$ — varies with operating point. - **Breakdown mechanisms:** Zener (tunneling, low voltage) and Avalanche (impact ionization, high voltage). - **Reverse saturation current** is independent of reverse bias magnitude (until breakdown). - **Depletion width** widens with reverse bias, narrows with forward bias. - **Transition capacitance** $C_T \propto 1/\sqrt{V_R}$ — important for varactor diodes. - **Applications:** Rectifiers, regulators (Zener), switches, LEDs, photodiodes, solar cells. - **KTU 2024 default assumptions:** Silicon, $T = 300 \text{ K}$, $\eta = 1$ unless stated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for V-I Curve (Quick Mnemonic): "FELC-B"** > - **F**orward region (exponential) > - **E**xponential rise after cut-in > - **L**inear-looking near breakdown (vertical drop) > - **C**ut-in voltage (knee point) > - **B**reakdown region in reverse

topics.title: Bipolar Junction Transistors: PNP and NPN Structures, Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bipolar Junction Transistors: PNP and NPN Structures, Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (BJT — Bipolar Junction Transistor)**
> A Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) is a **three-terminal, two-junction, current-controlled semiconductor device** constructed by sandwiching a thin layer of one type of semiconductor material (P-type or N-type) between two layers of the opposite type. The three terminals are named **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, and **Collector (C)**. The word *Bipolar* indicates that **both electrons and holes participate** in the conduction process across the two PN junctions.

There are two complementary structures:

- **NPN Transistor**: A P-type base sandwiched between two N-type regions (Emitter and Collector).
- **PNP Transistor**: An N-type base sandwiched between two P-type regions (Emitter and Collector).

The two internal PN junctions are:
- **Emitter–Base Junction (EBJ)** — the input side.
- **Collector–Base Junction (CBJ)** — the output side.

## 1.2 Physical Symbol Conventions (KTU Standard)

| Transistor Type | Arrow on Emitter | Arrow Direction |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **NPN** | Points **OUTWARD** (Not Pointing iN) | Out of the emitter |
| **PNP** | Points **INWARD** (Points iN Permanently) | Into the emitter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick (Examiner's Favourite Question):**
> **NPN = "Not Pointing iN"** (arrow on emitter points outward).
> **PNP = "Points iN Permanently"** (arrow on emitter points inward).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Tap Model

Imagine a **water tap (faucet) connected to a pipeline**:
- The **Emitter** is the *water inlet pipe* bringing in the main flow.
- The **Base** is the *small control knob* of the tap — you twist it slightly, and a **large** change occurs in the main water flow through the Collector.
- The **Collector** is the *outlet pipe* where the controlled water emerges.

In a BJT, a **tiny current injected at the base (the knob twist)** controls a **much larger current flowing from collector to emitter (the main water flow)**. This is the essence of **current amplification** — the foundation of every amplifier ever built.

## 1.4 Emitter Doping Asymmetry (Key Design Principle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Constructional Rules (Vital for Numerical Problems):**
> 1. **Emitter is heavily doped** (largest number of charge carriers) — supplies the charge.
> 2. **Base is lightly doped and physically very thin** — allows carriers to pass through with minimal recombination.
> 3. **Collector is moderately doped and physically large** — collects carriers and dissipates heat.

The charge of a single electron is **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** (the **elementary charge**), a fundamental constant used in all transistor current calculations.

## 1.5 Salient Features of a BJT

> [!NOTE]
> - **Three terminals**, **two PN junctions**, **one thin central region (Base)**.
> - Operates in **three regions**: Cut-off, Active, Saturation (and a fourth, breakdown).
> - **Current-controlled device** — input current at base controls output current.
> - Used as an **amplifier, switch, oscillator, and logic gate** (TTL).
> - Compact, low-cost, reliable — the building block of all analog and digital ICs.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 NPN Transistor — Structure & Biasing

In an **NPN transistor**:
- The two N-regions (Emitter & Collector) are made of electron-rich semiconductor.
- The thin P-region (Base) is hole-rich.
- **Electrons are the majority carriers**; they are injected from the N-Emitter into the P-Base, swept through it, and collected by the N-Collector.

### Forward Active Mode Biasing (Amplifier Configuration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For NPN in the Active (Amplifier) Region:**
> - **Emitter–Base Junction (EBJ) → Forward Biased** (V_BE ≈ +0.7 V for Silicon, +0.3 V for Germanium).
> - **Collector–Base Junction (CBJ) → Reverse Biased** (V_CB is positive for NPN).

### Step-by-Step Operation of an NPN Transistor

1. **Forward biasing of EBJ** lowers the potential barrier, causing **electrons to be injected from the heavily-doped N-Emitter into the thin P-Base**.
2. These injected electrons are the **minority carriers** in the base. Because the base is **very thin and lightly doped**, only a tiny fraction (≈ 1–5 %) recombine with holes in the base.
3. The recombined electrons are replaced by electrons entering from the external base terminal → this constitutes the **Base Current (I_B)**.
4. The remaining **~ 95–99 % of electrons diffuse across the thin base** and reach the depletion region of the reverse-biased CBJ.
5. The **strong electric field** of the reverse-biased CBJ **sweeps these electrons into the Collector region** → this constitutes the **Collector Current (I_C)**.
6. The total current leaving the emitter is the **Emitter Current (I_E)**, which is the sum of the base and collector currents.

## 2.2 PNP Transistor — Structure & Biasing

In a **PNP transistor**:
- The two P-regions (Emitter & Collector) are hole-rich.
- The thin N-region (Base) is electron-rich.
- **Holes are the majority carriers**; they flow from the P-Emitter → N-Base → P-Collector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For PNP in the Active (Amplifier) Region:**
> - **Emitter–Base Junction (EBJ) → Forward Biased** (V_EB ≈ +0.7 V for Silicon).
> - **Collector–Base Junction (CBJ) → Reverse Biased** (V_BC is positive for PNP, equivalently V_CB is negative).
> - All conventional current arrows point **INTO** the emitter (opposite of NPN).

The operating principle is identical to NPN, except the **roles of majority carriers are reversed** — holes act as the active charge carriers. The dual nature (NPN vs PNP) provides design flexibility for both positive and negative voltage supplies in circuits.

## 2.3 The Three Operating Regions of a BJT

| Region | EBJ Bias | CBJ Bias | Application | I_C State |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Cut-off** | Reverse | Reverse | OFF switch | I_C ≈ 0 |
| **Active (Forward)** | Forward | Reverse | Amplifier | I_C = β · I_B |
| **Saturation** | Forward | Forward | ON switch | I_C = I_C(sat) (max) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 'Bipolar'?** — Because **both** majority carriers (from the emitter) **and** minority carriers (across the reverse-biased junction) participate in conduction. This is the distinguishing feature from unipolar devices like the FET.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following relationships are **exam-locked essentials** — every formula here has appeared in KTU papers.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor | Amperes (A) |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | Common-Base Current Gain (always < 1) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | Common-Emitter Current Gain (typically 20–500) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$ | α-to-β Conversion | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | β-to-α Conversion | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$ | Output Current in Active Region | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $I_E = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_B$ | Emitter Current in Active Region | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $I_CEO = (\beta + 1) \cdot I_CBO$ | Leakage Current (Collector–Emitter, Base open) | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | $I_{CBO}$ | Leakage Current (Collector–Base, Emitter open) | Amperes (A) |
| 10 | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7 \text{ V (Si)}$ | Forward-bias drop across EBJ (Active Region) | Volts (V) |
| 11 | $P_C = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | Power dissipated at Collector | Watts (W) |
| 12 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance (small-signal) | Siemens (S) |

> **Where:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature (**T = 300 K**), with Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ and electronic charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BJT is the silent workhorse behind:
- **Audio amplifiers** in your mobile phone and home theatre.
- **Switching regulators** in laptop power adapters.
- **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) digital gates** in legacy CPUs and control boards.
- **Oscillators and signal generators** in communication transmitters.
- **Current mirrors and differential pairs** inside op-amp integrated circuits.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Current Relation: I_E = I_B + I_C

This is the most fundamental relationship, derived directly from **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — State KCL at the Transistor Node.**
Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor: the sum of all currents *entering* the device equals the sum of all currents *leaving* the device.

**Step 2 — Define Current Directions.**
By convention (consistent with the IEEE arrow notation):
- $I_E$ flows **INTO** the emitter (for both NPN and PNP in active region).
- $I_B$ flows **INTO** the base.
- $I_C$ flows **INTO** the collector for PNP, but **OUT OF** the collector for NPN.

**Step 3 — Apply KCL.**
For an NPN transistor in the active region (the derivation for PNP is identical in magnitude):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current In} &= \text{Current Out} \\[4pt]
I_E &= I_B + I_C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation.**
The emitter *supplies* all the charge carriers. A small fraction ($I_B$) leaves through the base terminal (due to recombination). The vast majority ($I_C$) leaves through the collector. **The emitter current is the source; the other two are divisions of it.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between α and β

The **current gain** of a BJT is defined in two ways, depending on which terminal is common. The relationship between these two gains is essential for KTU problems.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Start with the Two Definitions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{I_C}{I_E} \\[6pt]
\beta &= \frac{I_C}{I_B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express I_C in Terms of I_E Using α.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \alpha \cdot I_E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KCL Equation I_E = I_B + I_C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_E &= I_B + \alpha \cdot I_E \\[4pt]
I_E - \alpha \cdot I_E &= I_B \\[4pt]
I_E \cdot (1 - \alpha) &= I_B \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad I_B &= (1 - \alpha) \cdot I_E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio I_C / I_B (which is β).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{\alpha \cdot I_E}{(1 - \alpha) \cdot I_E} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad \beta &= \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert the Relationship to Get α in Terms of β.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta \cdot (1 - \alpha) &= \alpha \\[4pt]
\beta - \beta \cdot \alpha &= \alpha \\[4pt]
\beta &= \alpha + \beta \cdot \alpha \\[4pt]
\beta &= \alpha \cdot (1 + \beta) \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \alpha &= \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Leakage Current Relation: I_CEO = (β + 1) · I_CBO

The leakage current $I_{CBO}$ (measured with the emitter open) generates $I_{CEO}$ (measured with the base open), amplified by the factor $(\beta + 1)$. This is critical for temperature-stability calculations.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Two Leakage Currents.**

- $I_{CBO}$: Leakage current from Collector to Base with the Emitter **open** (caused by thermally generated minority carriers crossing the reverse-biased CBJ).
- $I_{CEO}$: Leakage current from Collector to Emitter with the Base **open**.

**Step 2 — Model the Leakage Contribution.**
When the base is open, $I_B = 0$. The leakage current $I_{CBO}$ flows into the base node and behaves exactly as a small forward-bias base current. By the active-region equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B + I_{CEO} \\[4pt]
\text{At base open: } I_B &= I_{CBO} \quad \text{(the only base-side current)} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad I_{CEO} &= \beta \cdot I_{CBO} + I_{CBO} \\[4pt]
I_{CEO} &= I_{CBO} \cdot (\beta + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Complete Active-Region Equation Including Leakage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B + I_{CEO} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B + (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{CBO}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance:** A small thermally generated leakage current $I_{CBO}$ gets **amplified by a factor of $(\beta+1)$**, making $I_{CEO}$ the dominant leakage path. This is why BJTs are highly temperature-sensitive — a key reason for the popularity of MOSFETs in modern ICs.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Given:** $\beta = 100$, $I_B = 20 \text{ μA}$.
> **Find:** $I_C$, $I_E$, and $\alpha$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate I_C.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta \cdot I_B \\[4pt]
I_C &= 100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} \\[4pt]
I_C &= 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 2 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate α.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.9901
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate I_E Using KCL.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_E &= I_B + I_C \\[4pt]
I_E &= 20 \text{ μA} + 2 \text{ mA} \\[4pt]
I_E &= 0.020 \text{ mA} + 2.000 \text{ mA} = 2.020 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Verify Using I_E = (β+1) · I_B.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_E &= (\beta + 1) \cdot I_B = 101 \times 20 \text{ μA} = 2020 \text{ μA} = 2.02 \text{ mA} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — NPN vs PNP Side-by-Side

| Parameter | NPN Transistor | PNP Transistor |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Symbol Arrow** | Points OUT of emitter (Not Pointing iN) | Points INTO emitter (Points iN) |
| **Majority Carriers** | Electrons | Holes |
| **EBJ Bias (Active)** | V_BE ≈ +0.7 V (Forward) | V_EB ≈ +0.7 V (Forward) |
| **CBJ Bias (Active)** | V_CB > 0 (Reverse) | V_BC > 0 (Reverse) |
| **Current Direction** | I_C flows OUT of collector | I_C flows INTO collector |
| **Power Supply Polarity** | V_CC is **positive** (V_C > V_E) | V_EE is **negative** (V_E > V_C) |
| **Switching Speed** | Faster (electron mobility higher) | Slower (hole mobility lower) |
| **Common Usage** | Dominant in modern designs | Used in complementary pairs (push-pull) |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of BJT Internal Structure (NPN)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal **layered architecture** of an NPN transistor, showing the three regions, the two junctions, the depletion regions, and the direction of current flow during forward-active operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Base Terminal B] --> Bnode(Base Region P-type Lightly Doped Very Thin)
    C[External Emitter Terminal E] --> Enode(Emitter Region N-type Heavily Doped)
    D[External Collector Terminal C] --> Colnode(Collector Region N-type Moderately Doped Large Area)
    Enode -->|Forward Biased EBJ| Bnode
    Bnode -->|Reverse Biased CBJ| Colnode
    Bnode -.->|Small Recombination 1 to 5 percent| RecSym[Base Current I_B Leaves Terminal]
    Enode -.->|Electrons Injected 100 percent| EjSym[Emitter Current I_E Enters Terminal]
    Colnode -.->|Swept Electrons 95 to 99 percent| CsSym[Collector Current I_C Leaves Terminal NPN]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of BJT Internal Structure (PNP)

The PNP structure is the **mirror image** of the NPN, with hole injection in the opposite direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[External Base Terminal B] --> PnodeB(Base Region N-type Lightly Doped Very Thin)
    P2[External Emitter Terminal E] --> PnodeE(Emitter Region P-type Heavily Doped)
    P3[External Collector Terminal C] --> PnodeC(Collector Region P-type Moderately Doped)
    PnodeE -->|Forward Biased EBJ| PnodeB
    PnodeB -->|Reverse Biased CBJ| PnodeC
    PnodeE -.->|Holes Injected 100 percent| PEsym[Emitter Current I_E Enters Terminal]
    PnodeB -.->|Small Recombination 1 to 5 percent| PBsym[Base Current I_B Enters Terminal]
    PnodeC -.->|Holes Collected 95 to 99 percent| PCsym[Collector Current I_C Enters Terminal PNP]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — BJT as a Current-Controlled Current Source

The BJT can be functionally modeled as a **dependent current source** controlled by the base current. The following diagram captures this behaviour for the **Common-Emitter (CE) configuration**, the most widely used amplifier topology.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input_Port [Input Loop Base Emitter]
        VBB[V_BB Battery] --> RBB[Base Resistor R_B]
        RBB --> BASETERM[Base Terminal B]
        EMITTER1[Emitter Terminal E] --> GND1[Common Ground]
    end
    subgraph Transistor_Core [BJT NPN Device]
        BASETERM --> EBJ[Forward Biased EBJ]
        CBJ2[Reverse Biased CBJ] --> COLLECTORTERM[Collector Terminal C]
        EBJ --> BASEREG[Thin P Base]
        BASEREG --> CBJ2
    end
    subgraph Output_Port [Output Loop Collector Emitter]
        VCC[V_CC Battery Positive] --> RC[Collector Resistor R_C]
        RC --> COLLECTORTERM
        GND1 --> EMITTER2[Emitter E]
        EMITTER2 --> BASEREG
    end
    ICC[Collector Current I_C = beta times I_B] -.-> RC
    IBB[Small Base Current I_B] -.->|Controls| ICC
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Biasing Arrangement (Forward Active Region)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NPN_Bias_Circuit [NPN Transistor with Dual Supply Biasing]
        VCCsrc[V_CC Plus 12 V] --> R_Cnode[Resistor R_C 4.7 kOhm]
        R_Cnode --> Cnode[Collector Terminal]
        VBBsrc[V_BB Plus 5 V] --> R_Bnode[Resistor R_B 100 kOhm]
        R_Bnode --> Bnode2[Base Terminal]
        Enode2[Emitter Terminal] --> GNDnode2[Ground 0 V]
        Bnode2 -. Forward Biased EBJ .-> Enode2
        Cnode -. Reverse Biased CBJ .-> Bnode2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - $V_{BB} \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow \text{Base}$ forms the **input loop** that supplies $I_B$.
> - $V_{CC} \rightarrow R_C \rightarrow \text{Collector} \rightarrow \text{Emitter} \rightarrow \text{Ground}$ forms the **output loop** that supplies $I_C$.
> - A **small** $I_B$ in the input loop causes a **large** $I_C$ in the output loop — this is **amplification**.

## 4.5 Comparison Flow — How α and β Relate Mathematically

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    StartNode[Start Two Current Gain Definitions] --> DefA[alpha equals I_C divided by I_E]
    StartNode --> DefB[beta equals I_C divided by I_B]
    DefA --> CombineStep[Apply KCL I_E equals I_B plus I_C]
    DefB --> CombineStep
    CombineStep --> Result1[Result alpha equals beta divided by beta plus 1]
    CombineStep --> Result2[Result beta equals alpha divided by 1 minus alpha]
    Result1 --> App1[Used for Common Base Amplifier Design]
    Result2 --> App2[Used for Common Emitter Amplifier Design]
```
