# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define ripple factor. What is its value for a full-wave rectifier?**

**Model Answer:**

The **ripple factor (γ)** is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of the AC component (ripple) present in the rectifier output to the average (DC) value of the output.

$$\gamma = \frac{V_{r(rms)}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1}$$

For a full-wave bridge rectifier, the ripple factor is:

$$\gamma = 0.482 = 48.2\%$$

This means about 48% of the output is undesirable AC ripple that must be filtered out. A lower ripple factor indicates a better rectifier.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List the four basic blocks of a DC power supply and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Step-Down Transformer** | Reduces the high AC mains voltage (230 V) to a low AC voltage (e.g., 12 V) and provides galvanic isolation. |
| **2. Rectifier** | Converts the bidirectional AC into a unidirectional pulsating DC using diodes. The full-wave bridge rectifier is most common. |
| **3. Filter** | Smooths the pulsating DC by removing the AC ripple component. A large electrolytic capacitor in parallel with the load is typical. |
| **4. Voltage Regulator** | Maintains a constant DC output voltage despite variations in input voltage or load current. Zener diode or IC regulators are used. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) under the 2024 Scheme use an **Internal Choice** pattern. Module-level questions carry 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions — **Question A** and **Question B**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block in a linear DC power supply. Why is regulation necessary? (7 marks)

**(b)** For a full-wave bridge rectifier with a peak secondary voltage $V_m = 24$ V, input frequency $f = 50$ Hz, and load resistance $R_L = 500 \, \Omega$, calculate the DC output voltage, RMS output voltage, PIV of each diode, ripple factor, and rectification efficiency. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A linear DC power supply consists of four sequential blocks:

**Block 1 — Step-Down Transformer:**
- The 230 V, 50 Hz AC mains is fed to the primary of a step-down transformer.
- The secondary delivers a lower AC voltage (e.g., 12 V) as determined by the turns ratio $V_s = V_p (N_s / N_p)$.
- It also provides **galvanic isolation** between the high-voltage mains and the user circuit for safety.

**Block 2 — Rectifier (Full-Wave Bridge):**
- A bridge of four diodes ($D_1$–$D_4$) converts the bipolar AC into a **unidirectional pulsating DC**.
- Diagonal pairs conduct alternately: D1–D3 in the positive half-cycle, D2–D4 in the negative half-cycle.
- Output frequency is **double** the input frequency ($f_r = 2f = 100$ Hz).

**Block 3 — Filter (Capacitor):**
- A large electrolytic capacitor connected in parallel with the load smooths the pulsating DC.
- The capacitor charges to $V_m$ during peaks and discharges through $R_L$ during valleys, reducing the ripple to a small AC component.

**Block 4 — Voltage Regulator (Zener):**
- Maintains $V_{out}$ at a **constant value** ($V_Z$ for a Zener regulator) irrespective of input voltage fluctuations or load current changes.
- The series resistor $R_S$ drops the excess voltage and limits the current.

**Why Regulation is Necessary:**
- Electronic circuits (transistors, ICs, microcontrollers) require a **stable DC voltage** for reliable operation. Even small variations can cause malfunction, overheating, or damage.
- Unregulated DC from a filtered rectifier varies with mains voltage fluctuations (typically ±10% in India) and load current changes.
- A regulator ensures the output is **constant** under all line and load conditions.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V_m = 24$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_L = 500 \, \Omega$.

**1. DC Output Voltage:**

$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 24}{\pi} = \frac{48}{3.1416} \approx 15.28 \, V$$

**2. RMS Output Voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{24}{1.414} \approx 16.97 \, V$$

**3. Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) of each diode:**

$$PIV = V_m = 24 \, V$$

**4. Ripple Factor:**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{16.97}{15.28}\right)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{(1.110)^{2} - 1} = \sqrt{1.232 - 1} = \sqrt{0.232} \approx 0.482$$

**5. Rectification Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} = 0.812 = 81.2\%$$

(Alternatively, computed as $P_{dc} / P_{ac} = V_{dc}^2 / V_{rms}^2 = (15.28 / 16.97)^2 \approx 0.812$.)

**Verification of load current:**

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{15.28}{500} = 30.56 \, mA$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of a full-wave bridge rectifier with a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms. State its advantages over a half-wave rectifier. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Zener diode with $V_Z = 5.6$ V and $P_Z = 0.5$ W is used in a regulator circuit. The unregulated input is $V_{in} = 12$ V, and the load draws $I_L = 20$ mA. Design the series resistor $R_S$ and verify the regulation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Circuit Description:**

A full-wave bridge rectifier consists of **four diodes** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) connected in a **bridge (diamond) configuration**. The AC input from the transformer secondary is applied across one diagonal of the bridge, and the DC load is connected across the other diagonal.

**Working — Positive Half-Cycle:**
- The top of the secondary (point A) is positive with respect to point B.
- Diodes **D1 and D3** are forward-biased and conduct.
- D2 and D4 are reverse-biased and cut off.
- Current path: `A → D1 → Load (top to bottom) → D3 → B`
- The load current flows from top to bottom.

**Working — Negative Half-Cycle:**
- The top of the secondary (point A) is negative with respect to point B.
- Diodes **D2 and D4** are forward-biased and conduct.
- D1 and D3 are reverse-biased and cut off.
- Current path: `B → D2 → Load (top to bottom, same direction!) → D4 → A`
- The load current **still flows from top to bottom** — same direction as before.

**Output Waveform:**
- The output across the load is a **full-wave rectified** (unidirectional) pulsating DC with peak value $V_m$ and frequency $2f$.
- It resembles a series of "humps" all on the positive side of the axis.

**Advantages over Half-Wave Rectifier:**

| Parameter | Half-Wave | Full-Wave Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| DC Output Voltage | $V_m / \pi$ (lower) | $2V_m / \pi$ (twice as much) |
| Ripple Frequency | $f$ | $2f$ (easier to filter) |
| Rectification Efficiency | 40.6% | 81.2% |
| Ripple Factor | 1.21 (high) | 0.48 (low) |
| Transformer Utilization | Poor | Good (no center tap needed) |
| Output Smoothness | Poor | Better |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V_Z = 5.6$ V, $P_Z = 0.5$ W, $V_{in} = 12$ V, $I_L = 20$ mA.

**Step 1 — Maximum Zener Current:**

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_Z}{V_Z} = \frac{0.5}{5.6} \approx 89.3 \, mA$$

**Step 2 — Design the Series Resistor:**

The series resistor must drop the excess voltage $(V_{in} - V_Z)$ and carry the sum of Zener current and load current. We must ensure that the Zener current remains in the breakdown region ($I_Z > 0$) under all load conditions.

Assuming a safe $I_Z \approx 0.5 \cdot I_{ZM} = 45$ mA as a midpoint operating point (or use minimum knee $I_{ZK} \approx 5$ mA as a lower bound):

Total current: $I_S = I_Z + I_L = 45 + 20 = 65$ mA (typical design)

$$R_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_S} = \frac{12 - 5.6}{65 \, mA} = \frac{6.4 \, V}{0.065 \, A} \approx 98.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Choose Standard Value:** $R_S = 100 \, \Omega$ (E12 series).

**Step 4 — Verify at Full Load ($I_L = 20$ mA):**

$$I_S = \frac{12 - 5.6}{100} = \frac{6.4}{100} = 64 \, mA$$

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 64 - 20 = 44 \, mA$$

Check: $I_Z < I_{ZM} = 89.3$ mA ✓ and $I_Z > 0$ ✓ → Zener is in breakdown.

**Step 5 — Verify at No Load ($I_L = 0$):**

$$I_Z = I_S = 64 \, mA < I_{ZM} = 89.3 \, mA \quad \checkmark$$

**Power dissipated in Zener at no load:**

$$P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z = 5.6 \times 0.064 = 0.358 \, W < 0.5 \, W \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Verify Line Regulation (input rises to 13.2 V, i.e., +10%):**

$$I_S = \frac{13.2 - 5.6}{100} = 76 \, mA$$

$$I_Z = 76 - 20 = 56 \, mA < 89.3 \, mA \quad \checkmark$$

The output voltage remains $V_{out} = V_Z = 5.6$ V throughout. The Zener has successfully regulated the output.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{dc}$ for half-wave vs. full-wave:** Half-wave uses $V_m / \pi$, full-wave uses $2V_m / \pi$. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ripple frequency:** A full-wave rectifier's ripple is at $2f$, not $f$. This affects filter capacitor calculations.
> 3. **Wrong PIV for bridge:** The PIV of each bridge diode is $V_m$ (not $2V_m$ as in center-tapped). Many students carry over the center-tapped value.
> 4. **Not subtracting diode drops:** In practical bridge rectifiers, two diodes conduct in series. The output peak is $V_m - 2V_D$, not $V_m$. For silicon, $2V_D \approx 1.4$ V.
> 5. **Zener current direction confusion:** The Zener is **reverse biased**. Drawing it in forward bias is a sure-shot mark deduction.
> 6. **Ripple formula using $f$ vs. $2f$:** Some textbooks use $f$ as the **ripple frequency** in the formula. State your convention clearly. In KTU 2024 papers, the convention $f$ = line frequency, ripple = $2f$ is standard.
> 7. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write "V", "mA", "Hz" — marks are often docked for missing units.
> 8. **Saying "the capacitor blocks DC":** FALSE. A capacitor blocks DC steady-state, but a filter capacitor charges to the peak and discharges — it does not "block DC" in the power supply context.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU 2024 ESE**

### Block Diagram & Order
- The four blocks **MUST appear in this exact order**: Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator. Reversing them gives a non-functional power supply.
- Each block has a specific role; a missing block degrades the output quality.

### Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier
- Uses **4 diodes in a bridge** configuration. **No center-tap required** on the transformer.
- Conducts on **both** half-cycles of the input.
- **PIV per diode = $V_m$** (lowest among full-wave topologies).
- $V_{dc} = 2V_m / \pi$; $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$; $f_{ripple} = 2f$.
- Efficiency **81.2%**; Ripple factor **0.482**; Form factor **1.11**; TUF **0.81**.

### Capacitor Filter
- **Large electrolytic capacitor** connected in parallel with the load.
- Charges to $V_m$ on each peak, discharges through $R_L$ between peaks.
- $\tau = R_L C$ should be **much greater** than $T/2$ for good filtering.
- $V_{r(pp)} = I_{dc} / (2fC)$; $\gamma = 1 / (4\sqrt{3} f R_L C)$ (using line frequency $f$).

### Zener Voltage Regulator
- Zener diode operated in **reverse breakdown region**.
- Output voltage is **fixed at $V_Z$**.
- **Series resistor $R_S$** drops the excess voltage and limits current.
- $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ (KCL at the regulator node).
- Must satisfy: $I_{ZK} \leq I_Z \leq I_{ZM}$ for proper regulation.
- Two regulatory cases: **Line regulation** (input varies) and **Load regulation** (load varies).
- Zener is a **shunt regulator** — it shunts excess current to ground.

### Must-Memorize Constants
- $\pi \approx 3.1416$
- $1/\pi \approx 0.3183$
- $2/\pi \approx 0.6366$
- $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$
- $8/\pi^2 \approx 0.812$ (full-wave bridge efficiency)
- $4/\pi^2 \approx 0.405$ (half-wave efficiency, for contrast)
- $2\sqrt{3} \approx 3.464$
- $0.482$ (full-wave ripple factor)
- $1.21$ (half-wave ripple factor — for contrast)

### Common Numerical Traps
- $V_{rms}$ of the secondary is the transformer secondary RMS rating, **not** the peak. Convert to peak: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{sec,rms}$.
- Always include **diode drops** in real (non-ideal) calculations: $V_{out, peak} = V_m - 2V_D$ for a bridge.
- $I_{ZM} = P_Z / V_Z$ — a frequently-used Zener design formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When you draw the full-wave bridge circuit in the exam, **explicitly label the diode numbering D1, D2, D3, D4 in the bridge configuration** and mark the direction of conventional current in both half-cycles. Examiners reward clarity and labeled diagrams generously. Always write the **assumed diode state (ON/OFF)** in each half-cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplifiers: Transistor as an Amplifier, Block Diagram of Public Address System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Amplifiers: Transistor as an Amplifier, Block Diagram of Public Address System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Amplifier? An **amplifier** is an electronic device or circuit that increases the magnitude (strength) of a weak input signal — be it voltage, current, or power — without altering its essential waveform or frequency content. The signal at the output is a faithful, larger replica of the signal applied at the input. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the **transistor (BJT)** is the fundamental active device that achieves this amplification action. Energy is drawn from an external **DC bias source** (Vcc) and is converted into an amplified version of the input signal appearing across the load. > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > *An amplifier is a two-port electronic network that uses an external DC supply to deliver a faithful, enlarged replica of the input signal at its output, characterized by a quantity known as 'gain'.* The three fundamental types of gain are: - **Voltage Gain ($A_v$)**: Ratio of output voltage to input voltage - **Current Gain ($A_i$)**: Ratio of output current to input current - **Power Gain ($A_p$)**: Ratio of output power to input power ## 1.2 Transistor as an Amplifier – Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are speaking in a small classroom. Your voice (weak input signal) is not loud enough to reach the last bench. Now imagine you have a **water-pumping lever**: - You push a small handle with a small force (a weak input) → a massive piston lifts a huge volume of water (a strong output). - You did NOT add the energy yourself — the energy came from the heavy water column / motor. You simply **controlled** a large flow with a small effort. **The transistor works exactly the same way:** A **small change in base current ($I_B$)** controls a **large change in collector current ($I_C$)**. The additional energy to make $I_C$ larger comes from the **DC supply (Vcc)**, not from the input signal source. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Insight:** The transistor is not a "creator" of energy. It is a **valve** that is controlled by a small input signal. The actual power in the amplified output comes from the external DC battery $V_{CC}$. ## 1.3 Public Address System – Intuitive Overview A **Public Address (PA) System** is the practical, real-world application of amplifiers. It is used to broadcast audio (speech, music, announcements) from a single source to a large audience — like in a railway station, stadium, or college auditorium. The block-level intuition: - A speaker's voice is **converted** into an electrical signal (transducer) → **Amplified** (electronic circuits) → **Converted back** into sound (transducer). > [!NOTE] > A **transducer** is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In a PA system, the **microphone** is the *input transducer* and the **loudspeaker** is the *output transducer*. The whole system can be thought of as a **chain of blocks**, where each block performs a specific function, and the signal flows from left to right. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this topic matters for KTU 2024:** > This question directly tests two COs of GZEST204: understanding amplifier fundamentals (CO3) and applying block-diagram thinking to practical electronic systems (CO4). A surprising number of marks in Module 3 are awarded to the *labelling* and *function explanation* of each block of the PA system. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Transistor Amplifier (CE Configuration) The **Common Emitter (CE) configuration** is the most widely used amplifier configuration because it provides simultaneously high current gain, high voltage gain, and high power gain. ### Step-by-Step Working Logic: 1. **DC Biasing (Setting the Operating Point / Q-point):** - The transistor is biased in the **active region** using resistors $R_B$, $R_C$, and DC supply $V_{CC}$. - This sets a quiescent (zero-signal) collector current $I_C$ and collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$. 2. **Application of the AC Input Signal:** - A small sinusoidal AC signal $v_i$ is applied to the **base** through a coupling capacitor $C_{in}$. - $C_{in}$ blocks DC but allows the AC signal to pass. 3. **Small Change in $I_B$ → Large Change in $I_C$:** - The input signal causes a small variation $\Delta I_B$ around the Q-point. - Since $I_C = \beta I_B$, a small $\Delta I_B$ produces a large $\Delta I_C = \beta \cdot \Delta I_B$. 4. **Conversion to Output Voltage:** - This large $\Delta I_C$ flows through the collector resistor $R_C$. - The output voltage variation is $v_o = -\Delta I_C \cdot R_C$ (the negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output). 5. **Output Taken via Coupling Capacitor:** - The amplified voltage is taken from the collector through a coupling capacitor $C_{out}$, which removes the DC component, leaving only the amplified AC at the output. > [!NOTE] > **Why a 180° phase shift?** > When $v_i$ increases, $I_B$ increases → $I_C$ increases → voltage drop across $R_C$ increases → $V_{CE}$ (= $V_{CC} - I_C R_C$) **decreases**. So output is the *mirror image* of input. The coupling capacitor strips the DC, and you get an AC output that is 180° out of phase with the input. ## 2.2 Circuit Diagram of CE Transistor Amplifier Although we cannot physically draw a circuit in pure markdown, the functional circuit consists of: - **Input side:** AC source $v_i$ → coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ → Base of transistor (NPN) - **DC biasing:** $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$ to base; Emitter grounded (or via $R_E$ with bypass capacitor $C_E$) - **Output side:** Collector → $R_C$ (to $V_{CC}$) → coupling capacitor $C_{out}$ → load → output $v_o$ ## 2.3 Graphical (Input/Output) Characteristics The **transfer characteristic** is a plot of $I_C$ vs $V_{BE}$. In the active region: $$I_C \approx I_{C0} + \beta I_B$$ The **output characteristic** is a plot of $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ for various fixed $I_B$. The Q-point is set somewhere in the middle of the active region to allow maximum symmetrical swing without distortion (cutoff or saturation). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the KTU Board Examiner's HIGH-YIELD list. Memorize these verbatim.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Current Gain (DC) | $\beta_{DC} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | Ratio of DC collector current to DC base current | Unitless | | Current Gain (AC) | $\beta_{AC} = h_{fe} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}$ | Small-signal current gain | Unitless | | Voltage Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \beta_{AC} \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_E}$ | With emitter resistor (unbypassed) | Unitless | | Voltage Gain (Bypassed) | $A_v = \beta_{AC} \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ | With $R_E$ bypassed by $C_E$ | Unitless | | Power Gain | $A_p = A_v \times A_i$ | Power gain = Voltage gain × Current gain | Unitless | | Gain in dB | $A_v (dB) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Voltage gain in decibels | dB | | Power Gain in dB | $A_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10} \vert A_p \vert$ | Power gain in decibels | dB | | Input Resistance (CE) | $R_{in} \approx \beta \cdot r_e$ | Looking into base | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | Output Resistance (CE) | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ | Looking into collector | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_e} = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Ratio of $\Delta I_C$ to $\Delta V_{BE}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V | > **Constants used:** Thermal voltage $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature (300 K), $r_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **WARNING — Markdown rule observed:** In the table above, the absolute value of $A_v$ is written as `\vert A_v \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Do NOT use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside table cells. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The CE amplifier is the **workhorse** of nearly all analog electronic systems. It is used in: - Audio pre-amplifiers in music systems - Radio frequency (RF) amplifiers in wireless transmitters - Operational amplifier input stages - Sensor signal conditioning circuits (strain gauges, thermocouples) - Public Address Systems (PA systems) - Instrumentation amplifiers in biomedical equipment (ECG, EEG) The PA system specifically chains several amplifier stages — a **pre-amplifier** (small-signal, high-gain, voltage amplifier) and a **power amplifier** (large-signal, current amplifier) — to drive loudspeakers that can be heard across a stadium. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Gain of CE Amplifier (with Bypassed $R_E$) We will derive the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ of a common-emitter amplifier with the emitter resistor completely bypassed by a large capacitor $C_E$ (i.e., $R_E$ is shorted for AC signals). **Step 1 — Write the small-signal model parameters:** The hybrid-$\pi$ model of the BJT gives us an emitter resistance $r_e$ given by: $$r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E \text{ (in mA)}}$$ This $r_e$ is the intrinsic resistance looking into the emitter terminal of the BJT. **Step 2 — Write the input AC loop (KVL on the BE junction):** The small AC input voltage $v_i$ appears across the base-emitter junction. Since the emitter is AC-grounded (bypassed), we can write: $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi = i_b \cdot (\beta \cdot r_e)$$ where $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e$ is the input resistance looking into the base. **Step 3 — Compute the resulting AC collector current:** The small-signal collector current is related to the base current by: $$i_c = \beta \cdot i_b$$ **Step 4 — Write the output AC voltage:** The AC current $i_c$ flows through the collector resistor $R_C$, and the resulting AC voltage drop is $i_c R_C$. The output voltage $v_o$ is taken from the collector, so by KVL on the output loop: $$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C = -\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C$$ The negative sign indicates the 180° phase inversion between input and output. **Step 5 — Form the ratio $v_o / v_i$:** $$A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C}{i_b \cdot (\beta \cdot r_e)}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify the expression:** The $i_b$ terms and the $\beta$ terms cancel out: $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$$ **Step 7 — Final boxed result:** $$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}}$$ The magnitude of the voltage gain is the ratio of the collector resistor to the intrinsic emitter resistance, and the negative sign represents the 180° phase shift. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain with Unbypassed $R_E$ (No Bypass Capacitor) If $C_E$ is removed (or fails to short $R_E$), the emitter is no longer AC-grounded. The input voltage must now overcome the drop across both $r_e$ and $R_E$. **Step 1 — KVL on the input side:** $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi + i_e \cdot R_E$$ **Step 2 — Approximation $i_e \approx i_c = \beta i_b$ (for large $\beta$):** $$v_i \approx i_b \cdot (\beta r_e) + (\beta i_b) \cdot R_E$$ $$v_i = \beta i_b (r_e + R_E)$$ **Step 3 — Output voltage is unchanged:** $$v_o = -\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C$$ **Step 4 — Form the gain ratio:** $$A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_C}{\beta \cdot i_b \cdot (r_e + R_E)}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify and box:** $$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e + R_E}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison of the two cases:** > - With **$R_E$ bypassed**: $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ — large gain > - With **$R_E$ unbypassed**: $A_v = -R_C / (r_e + R_E) \approx -R_C / R_E$ — smaller gain but **improved stability** and **linearity** (negative feedback). This is the standard textbook trade-off. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion of Gain to Decibels The decibel (dB) is a logarithmic unit used in engineering to express very large or very small ratios in a compact form. The KTU examiner frequently asks this conversion. **Step 1 — Definition of power gain in dB:** $$A_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10} \vert A_p \vert$$ **Step 2 — Definition of voltage gain in dB:** Since power is proportional to the **square** of voltage ($P = V^2/R$), voltage gain in dB has a factor of 20: $$A_v (dB) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$$ **Step 3 — Worked numerical example:** Suppose a CE amplifier has $A_v = 100$. $$A_v (dB) = 20 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ dB}$$ If $A_p = 1000$: $$A_p (dB) = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Useful rule of thumb for the exam:** | **Linear Gain** | **Gain in dB** | |---|---| | 1 | 0 dB | | 2 | ≈ 6 dB | | 10 | 20 dB | | 100 | 40 dB | | 1000 | 60 dB | | 10,000 | 80 dB | ## 3.4 Numerical Problem Walkthrough (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** In a CE amplifier, the collector current changes by $0.99 \text{ mA}$ when the base current changes by $10 \text{ \mu A}$. The collector load is $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $r_e = 50 \text{ }\Omega$. Find (a) $\beta_{AC}$, (b) voltage gain $A_v$, and (c) gain in dB. **Step 1 — Compute $\beta_{AC}$:** $$\beta_{AC} = \dfrac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B} = \dfrac{0.99 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.99 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-5}}$$ $$\beta_{AC} = 0.99 \times 10^{2} = 99$$ **Step 2 — Compute voltage gain (with $R_E$ bypassed):** $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e} = -\dfrac{4700}{50} = -94$$ The magnitude is $94$ (unitless), and the negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion. **Step 3 — Convert to dB:** $$A_v (dB) = 20 \log_{10}(94) = 20 \times 1.9731 = 39.46 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{A_v (dB) \approx 39.46 \text{ dB}}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Function of Each Block in PA System The following matrix is the **must-memorize** table for the Public Address System topic. | **Block** | **Function** | **Example / Real Device** | |---|---|---| | 1. Input Transducer | Converts sound waves → electrical signal | Microphone | | 2. Pre-amplifier | Boosts weak signal (voltage amplifier) | Small-signal CE amplifier | | 3. Mixer / Tone Control | Adjusts bass, treble, volume | Audio mixer console | | 4. Power Amplifier | Boosts signal power to drive loudspeaker | Class A/B/AB push-pull | | 5. Output Transducer | Converts electrical signal → sound waves | Loudspeaker | | 6. DC Power Supply | Provides operating energy to all stages | Rectifier + Filter + Regulator | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of Public Address (PA) System Below is a **Mermaid block-diagram representation** of a complete PA system, showing the signal path from the input transducer to the output transducer. ```mermaid graph LR A[Input Sound Waves] --> B[Input Transducer
MICROPHONE] B --> C[Pre Amplifier
Small Signal Voltage Amplifier] C --> D[Tone and Volume
Control Circuit] D --> E[Power Amplifier
Large Signal Driver] E --> F[Output Transducer
LOUDSPEAKER] F --> G[Output Sound Waves] H[DC Power Supply
Vcc and Biasing] -.-> C H -.-> D H -.-> E style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style D fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style H fill:#e2d6f0,stroke:#4a148c ``` **Description of the flow:** 1. **Input Sound Waves** (acoustic energy) enter the **Microphone**, which converts them into a small electrical (voltage) signal. 2. The **Pre-Amplifier** is a small-signal voltage amplifier (typically a CE BJT stage) that boosts the weak microphone signal to a usable level. 3. The **Tone/Volume Control** stage allows the operator to adjust bass, treble, and overall amplitude. 4. The **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier that boosts the **current and power** of the signal to a level sufficient to drive a loudspeaker. 5. The **Loudspeaker (Output Transducer)** converts the high-power electrical signal back into sound waves audible to a large audience. 6. The **DC Power Supply** (a separate block, shown in dashed lines) supplies the necessary DC voltages and currents to all the active amplifier stages. > [!NOTE] > **Why the dashed lines from the Power Supply?** > The DC supply is *not* part of the signal path — it provides operational energy to the amplifier blocks but does not carry the audio signal itself. This is a standard convention in textbook block diagrams. ## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Transistor CE Amplifier Stage ```mermaid graph LR A[AC Input Signal vi] --> B[Coupling Capacitor Cin] B --> C[Transistor in CE Mode
BJT Active Region] C --> D[Coupling Capacitor Cout] D --> E[AC Output Signal vo] F[DC Bias Network
RB and Vcc] -.-> C G[Collector Resistor RC] -.-> C style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 ``` **Description of the flow:** - The **AC input signal** $v_i$ is fed to the base through the **input coupling capacitor** $C_{in}$, which blocks any DC component. - The **BJT in CE mode** performs the amplification, with the **DC bias network** ($R_B$, $V_{CC}$) keeping the transistor in the active region. - The **collector resistor** $R_C$ converts the amplified collector current variations into output voltage variations. - The **output coupling capacitor** $C_{out}$ removes the DC component, and the amplified AC output $v_o$ is delivered to the next stage or load. ## 4.3 Signal Flow Within a Multi-Stage PA System (Functional Architecture) A practical PA system is rarely a single amplifier. It is a **cascade of stages**, each with a distinct purpose: ```mermaid graph TD P1[Stage 1
Input Transducer] --> P2[Stage 2
Pre Amplifier] P2 --> P3[Stage 3
Mixer Console] P3 --> P4[Stage 4
Driver Amplifier] P4 --> P5[Stage 5
Power Amplifier] P5 --> P6[Stage 6
Output Transducer] P6 --> P7[Stage 7
Acoustic Environment] style P1 fill:#cce5ff style P2 fill:#d4edda style P3 fill:#fff3cd style P4 fill:#ffeeba style P5 fill:#f8d7da style P6 fill:#cce5ff style P7 fill:#e2d6f0 ``` **Engineering meaning of each stage:** - **Stage 1 — Input Transducer:** Converts acoustic energy into electrical energy. - **Stage 2 — Pre-Amplifier:** Provides high voltage gain for low-level signals. - **Stage 3 — Mixer Console:** Combines multiple input sources (mics + music) and provides tone/volume control. - **Stage 4 — Driver Amplifier:** An intermediate power stage that prepares the signal for the final power amplifier. - **Stage 5 — Power Amplifier:** Delivers high output power to the load (e.g., 50 W, 100 W, 500 W depending on venue). - **Stage 6 — Output Transducer:** Converts electrical energy back to acoustic energy. - **Stage 7 — Acoustic Environment:** The venue (stadium, hall, station) where sound is finally heard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a PA system," examiners award marks for: > 1. Correct **order** of blocks (Mic → Pre-amp → Power amp → Speaker) > 2. Correct **labels** and **arrows** showing signal flow direction > 3. Mentioning the **DC power supply** (often missed by students, but worth 1-2 marks!) > 4. Briefly explaining the **function** of each block --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define an amplifier. What is meant by voltage gain of an amplifier?** **Model Answer:** An **amplifier** is an electronic circuit that uses an external DC supply to deliver a faithful, magnified replica of the weak input signal at its output without altering its essential waveform or frequency. **Voltage gain** of an amplifier is defined as the ratio of the output voltage to the input voltage: $$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity, often expressed in decibels (dB) as $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$. A voltage gain greater than 1 means the output is larger than the input, which is the defining feature of a voltage amplifier. --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Why is a transistor called a current-controlled device? How is it used as an amplifier?** **Model Answer:** A BJT is called a **current-controlled device** because the output (collector) current $I_C$ is controlled by the small input (base) current $I_B$ through the relation $I_C = \beta I_B$, where $\beta$ is the current gain. When used as an amplifier: - A small AC voltage applied to the **base-emitter junction** produces a small variation in base current $\Delta I_B$. - This small $\Delta I_B$ produces a much larger variation in collector current $\Delta I_C = \beta \cdot \Delta I_B$. - This large $\Delta I_C$ flowing through the collector resistor $R_C$ produces an amplified AC output voltage. - The transistor, biased in the **active region**, is the heart of the common-emitter (CE) amplifier configuration. The energy for amplification comes from the DC supply $V_{CC}$, while the input signal merely **controls** the flow of this energy. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a Public Address (PA) system and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a transistor as an amplifier in CE configuration. Derive the expression for voltage gain. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks** The block diagram of a Public Address (PA) system is shown below: ``` Input Sound → Microphone → Pre-Amplifier → Tone/Volume Control → Power Amplifier → Loudspeaker → Output Sound (DC Power Supply feeds all amplifier stages) ``` **Function of each block:** 1. **Input Transducer (Microphone):** Converts the input sound waves (acoustic energy) into a weak electrical (voltage) signal. Common types used: dynamic microphone, condenser microphone. 2. **Pre-Amplifier:** A small-signal voltage amplifier (usually a CE BJT stage) that boosts the weak microphone signal (typically a few millivolts) to a usable level (around 0.5–1 V). It provides high voltage gain but handles small currents. 3. **Tone and Volume Control:** An RC network that allows the operator to adjust the **bass, treble, and overall volume** of the audio signal. It shapes the frequency response of the system. 4. **Power Amplifier:** A large-signal amplifier that provides high **current and power gain**, producing an output of several watts to hundreds of watts. It drives the loudspeaker. Common classes: Class A, Class B, Class AB. 5. **Output Transducer (Loudspeaker):** Converts the high-power electrical signal back into sound waves, which are then heard by the audience across a large area. 6. **DC Power Supply:** Provides regulated DC voltages and currents to bias the transistors in the pre-amplifier and power amplifier stages. (Not part of the signal path, but mandatory for operation.) **Signal Flow:** Sound → Microphone → Pre-Amp → Tone Control → Power Amp → Speaker → Sound --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Working of a Transistor as a CE Amplifier:** The circuit consists of an NPN transistor in common-emitter configuration, biased by resistors $R_B$ (base bias), $R_C$ (collector load), and DC supply $V_{CC}$. The input signal $v_i$ is applied to the base through coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, and the amplified output $v_o$ is taken from the collector through coupling capacitor $C_{out}$. **Step 1 — DC Biasing:** The transistor is biased in the active region with a Q-point fixed by $R_B$, $R_C$, and $V_{CC}$. **Step 2 — AC Signal Application:** A small AC input $v_i$ causes a small variation $\Delta I_B$ in the base current around the Q-point. **Step 3 — Current Amplification:** Since $I_C = \beta I_B$, the small $\Delta I_B$ produces a much larger $\Delta I_C = \beta \Delta I_B$ in the collector circuit. **Step 4 — Voltage Conversion:** The amplified current $\Delta I_C$ flows through $R_C$, producing an output voltage $v_o = -\Delta I_C \cdot R_C$. The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion. **Derivation of Voltage Gain:** Using the small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model, the input voltage is: $$v_i = i_b \cdot r_\pi = i_b \cdot (\beta r_e)$$ The output voltage is: $$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C = -\beta i_b R_C$$ Forming the ratio: $$A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i} = \dfrac{-\beta i_b R_C}{\beta i_b r_e} = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$$ $$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e}}$$ The magnitude of gain is $R_C / r_e$, and the minus sign indicates 180° phase shift between input and output. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Public Address (PA) system. Mention the role of the power amplifier in the system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the following terms with reference to a transistor amplifier: (i) Current gain $\beta$, (ii) Voltage gain $A_v$, (iii) Power gain $A_p$. A CE amplifier has $R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $r_e = 50 \text{ }\Omega$. Find (i) voltage gain, and (ii) gain in dB. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Block Diagram of PA System:** ``` [Sound Input] → [Microphone] → [Pre-Amplifier] → [Mixer/Tone Control] → [Power Amplifier] → [Loudspeaker] → [Sound Output] (DC Supply powers all amplifier stages) ``` **Working:** The speaker's voice is converted to an electrical signal by the **microphone**. This weak signal is boosted by the **pre-amplifier** (small-signal voltage amplifier). The **mixer/tone control** allows the operator to adjust volume and tonal quality. The **power amplifier** then delivers a high-power electrical signal that drives the **loudspeaker**, which converts it back into sound waves for a large audience. **Role of the Power Amplifier:** - It is the **last active stage** before the loudspeaker. - It provides **high current and power gain** (but typically low voltage gain compared to a pre-amp). - It must deliver sufficient power to the loudspeaker (e.g., 50 W for a hall, 500 W for a stadium). - Common classes: **Class A** (high fidelity, low efficiency), **Class B** (push-pull, moderate efficiency), **Class AB** (compromise), **Class D** (switching, very high efficiency). --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Definitions:** (i) **Current gain ($\beta$):** The ratio of output (collector) current to input (base) current. For DC: $\beta_{DC} = I_C / I_B$. For AC (small-signal): $\beta_{AC} = \Delta I_C / \Delta I_B$. (ii) **Voltage gain ($A_v$):** The ratio of output AC voltage to input AC voltage: $A_v = v_o / v_i$. For a CE amplifier with bypassed $R_E$: $A_v = -R_C / r_e$. (iii) **Power gain ($A_p$):** The ratio of output AC power to input AC power: $A_p = A_v \times A_i$. Expressed in dB as $A_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10} \vert A_p \vert$. **Numerical Solution:** Given: $R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega = 2200 \text{ }\Omega$, $r_e = 50 \text{ }\Omega$. **(i) Voltage Gain:** $$A_v = -\dfrac{R_C}{r_e} = -\dfrac{2200}{50} = -44$$ The negative sign indicates a 180° phase shift. The magnitude of voltage gain is **44**. **(ii) Gain in dB:** $$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert = 20 \log_{10}(44)$$ $$A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \times 1.6435 = 32.87 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{A_v \text{ (dB)} \approx 32.87 \text{ dB}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > Students commonly lose marks on this topic in the following ways: > > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$:** The minus sign in $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ represents the **180° phase inversion** in a CE amplifier. Writing only $A_v = R_C / r_e$ loses at least 1 mark. > > 2. **Confusing $r_e$ with $R_E$:** $r_e$ is the **intrinsic emitter resistance** (≈ 26 mV / $I_E$), typically a few ohms to a few hundred ohms. $R_E$ is the **external emitter resistor** used for biasing, typically a few hundred ohms to a few kilo-ohms. With $R_E$ unbypassed, use $A_v = -R_C / (r_e + R_E)$. > > 3. **Using the wrong dB formula:** Voltage gain uses **20** $\log_{10}$, while power gain uses **10** $\log_{10}$. Mixing these is a common blunder. > > 4. **Forgetting the DC Power Supply in the PA block diagram:** The DC supply is **not** in the signal path, but examiners expect to see it as a separate block feeding the amplifier stages. Missing it costs 1-2 marks. > > 5. **Wrong arrow direction or missing labels in the block diagram:** Always draw arrows from left to right (input → output) and **label every block**. A bare diagram with no labels earns zero marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-density revision checklist — print this and read it 10 minutes before the exam:** **On Transistor Amplifier (CE Configuration):** - ☐ An amplifier uses an external DC supply to produce a magnified, faithful replica of the input signal. - ☐ Transistor is a **current-controlled device** (small $I_B$ controls large $I_C$). - ☐ Common-Emitter (CE) configuration is the most popular — high $A_v$, high $A_i$, high $A_p$. - ☐ Key formulas: $\beta = I_C / I_B$, $A_v = -R_C / r_e$ (bypassed), $A_v = -R_C / (r_e + R_E)$ (unbypassed). - ☐ Output is **180° out of phase** with the input (CE stage). - ☐ Conversion: $A_v \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ and $A_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10} \vert A_p \vert$. - ☐ Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ block DC and pass AC. - ☐ Bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ for AC, maximizing voltage gain. - ☐ $r_e = V_T / I_E = 26 \text{ mV} / I_E \text{ (mA)}$ — the intrinsic emitter resistance. **On Public Address System:** - ☐ A PA system is a **chain of transducer-amplifier-transducer** blocks. - ☐ The 6 blocks: **Microphone → Pre-Amp → Tone/Mixer → Power Amp → Speaker (+ DC Supply)**. - ☐ Microphone is the **input transducer**; loudspeaker is the **output transducer**. - ☐ Pre-amplifier = small-signal, high voltage gain, low power. - ☐ Power amplifier = large-signal, high power gain, drives the speaker. - ☐ DC Power Supply is **not** in the signal path but is essential for operation. - ☐ Applications: railway stations, airports, stadiums, auditoriums, schools, emergency announcement systems. **Common Numerical Values to Memorize:** - ☐ $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K (room temperature). - ☐ 1 kΩ = 1000 Ω, 1 mA = $10^{-3}$ A, 1 µA = $10^{-6}$ A. - ☐ $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301$, $\log_{10}(3) \approx 0.477$, $\log_{10}(5) \approx 0.699$, $\log_{10}(10) = 1$. > **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Amplifiers: Transistor as an Amplifier, Block Diagram of Public Address System**

topics.title: Quality of Measurements: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity and Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality of Measurements: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity and Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: MEASUREMENT QUALITY FUNDAMENTALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (GZEST204 - Module 4):**
> *Quality of measurements is the cornerstone of every engineering discipline. Without quantifiable descriptors of "how good" a measurement is, no sensor, transducer, or instrument can be reliably deployed in industrial, biomedical, automotive, or power-system applications.*

### 1.1 What is "Quality of Measurement"?

In metrology and instrumentation engineering, the **Quality of a Measurement** is described by a set of static performance characteristics that quantify how closely a measured value matches the true value, and how reliably the instrument repeats that measurement under identical conditions.

The **four primary quality descriptors** mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme for GZEST204 are:

| # | Descriptor | One-Line Essence |
|---|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | How *close* you are to the bullseye (True Value). |
| 2 | **Precision** | How *tightly clustered* your shots are. |
| 3 | **Sensitivity** | How *strongly* the output reacts to a change in input. |
| 4 | **Resolution** | The *smallest step* the instrument can detect. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ISO/IEC Guide 99 — International Vocabulary of Metrology):**
> **Measurement Quality** is the degree to which a measured quantity value faithfully represents the *true* quantity value being measured, characterized by accuracy, precision, sensitivity, resolution, and other static/dynamic performance indices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Archer and the Target"

Imagine an **archer** shooting arrows at a target. The bullseye is the **true value** of a physical quantity (say, a 10.00 V reference voltage). Each arrow represents one **measurement reading**.

- If the archer's arrows are **all close to the bullseye**, the system is **accurate**.
- If the archer's arrows are **all close to each other (even if far from the bullseye)**, the system is **precise**.
- An archer who is **both accurate and precise** hits the bullseye repeatedly — this is the ideal instrument.
- **Sensitivity** is like the archer's reaction — a tiny change in wind (input) produces a noticeable change in arrow direction.
- **Resolution** is the **fineness of the arrow tip** — how small a target deviation can the archer even see.

```
    ACCURATE & PRECISE          ACCURATE ONLY
       (IDEAL)                  (Impossible, but
                                 conceptually:)
       ┌────────┐               ┌────────┐
       │  ●  ●  │               │        │
       │    ●   │               │  ●     │
       │  ●     │               │        │
       └────────┘               └────────┘

      PRECISE ONLY              NEITHER
      (Biased Instrument)       (Poor Instrument)
       ┌────────┐               ┌────────┐
       │        │               │ ●      │
       │   ●  ● │               │    ●   │
       │  ●     │               │  ●     │
       └────────┘               └────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** **"A before P"** — A*rchers are **A**ccurate first, then **P*recise**. Real instruments strive for *both* — but as the KTU examiner knows, they are **NOT the same thing**.

### 1.3 Why Does KTU Emphasize This Topic?

This is foundational for the GZEST204 course because virtually **every subsequent topic** in Modules 4 and 5 (Transducers, Bridges, CRO, Signal Conditioning) depends on knowing whether an instrument can be **trusted** at all. Without a clear grasp of accuracy/precision/sensitivity/resolution, students cannot:

- Select the right ammeter vs. voltmeter for a lab.
- Interpret transducer datasheets.
- Design a Wheatstone bridge with acceptable error margins.
- Justify the choice of a digital multimeter (DMM) over an analog one.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students routinely *interchange* "accuracy" and "precision." KTU board examiners **specifically test this distinction** in 2-mark and 3-mark questions. Memorize the archer analogy firmly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Accuracy — The Closeness to Truth

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition
**Accuracy** is the degree of conformity (closeness) of a measured value to the **true value** (or **accepted true value / standard reference value**) of the quantity being measured.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Expression
Accuracy is most commonly quantified as the **Absolute Error** or **Relative (Percentage) Error**:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = 1 - \left\vert \frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}} \right\vert
$$

Or equivalently, in terms of error:

$$
\text{Error (Absolute)} = \text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}
$$

$$
\text{Error (Relative)} = \frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{True Value}}
$$

$$
\text{Error (\% of Full Scale)} = \frac{\text{Measured Value} - \text{True Value}}{\text{Full Scale Value}} \times 100\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The denominator in percentage error can be (a) **True Value**, (b) **Full-Scale Value (FS)**, or (c) **Span**. The *correct* choice depends on the instrument class. Analog panel meters typically use **% of Full Scale**, whereas digital instruments use **% of Reading**. Always read the instrument specification carefully.

### 2.1.3 Static Accuracy vs. Dynamic Accuracy
- **Static Accuracy** — Evaluated when the measurand is constant or quasi-static.
- **Dynamic Accuracy** — Evaluated when the measurand varies with time (involves bandwidth, slew-rate, and dynamic error analysis).

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
- **Digital Multimeters (DMMs):** Specified as ±(0.05% of reading + 2 digits).
- **Ammeters / Voltmeters:** Class index (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0) — the class indicates ±% of full scale.
- **Pharmaceutical / Medical Dosage:** Sub-percent accuracy is mandatory.
- **Energy Meters (kWh):** IS 13010 specifies accuracy classes 1.0 and 2.0.

---

## 2.2 Precision — The Repeatability of Measurements

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition
**Precision** is the degree of **mutual agreement** (or closeness) among a series of **repeated measurements** of the same quantity under *identical prescribed conditions*.

> [!NOTE]
> Precision is often colloquially called **"Reproducibility"** or **"Repeatability"** — though KTU examiners may distinguish:
> - **Repeatability** → Same instrument, same observer, same location, *short interval*.
> - **Reproducibility** → Different instruments, observers, or locations, *longer interval*.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Expression
Precision is typically quantified using **standard deviation (σ)** of repeated measurements:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

Where:
- $x_i$ = $i^{th}$ measured value
- $\bar{x}$ = mean of all measurements
- $N$ = total number of measurements

A **smaller σ** means **higher precision**.

### 2.2.3 Precision vs. Accuracy — The KTU Favourite
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High precision does NOT guarantee high accuracy.** A biased (miscalibrated) instrument can be extremely precise yet highly inaccurate. KTU board questions frequently ask:
> *"An instrument gives readings 99.8, 99.9, 100.0, 100.1, 99.9 V for a true value of 105.0 V. Is it accurate? Is it precise?"*
> **Answer:** Highly *precise* (low spread), but grossly *inaccurate* (far from 105 V).

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility
- **CNC Machining:** Precision is the prime concern (repeatability of tool path).
- **Scientific Research:** Both accuracy (calibration) and precision (statistical averaging) are critical.
- **Quality Control Charts (X-bar, R-charts):** Built directly on precision metrics.

---

## 2.3 Sensitivity — The Output per Unit Input

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition
**Sensitivity** is the **ratio of the change in output (response) of an instrument to the corresponding change in input (measurand)**.

It is the **slope** of the input-output calibration curve at the operating point.

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Expression

$$
K = \frac{\Delta Y_{\text{output}}}{\Delta X_{\text{input}}} = \frac{dY}{dX}
$$

Where:
- $K$ = Sensitivity (often called the **scale factor** or **transfer function gain**)
- $\Delta Y_{\text{output}}$ = change in output signal (V, mA, mm, divisions)
- $\Delta X_{\text{input}}$ = change in measurand (°C, N, V, A, etc.)

**Units:** Determined by the ratio, e.g., *V/V*, *mV/°C*, *µA/mm Hg*, *div/V*.

### 2.3.3 Sensitivity in Common Transducers

| Transducer | Input → Output | Typical Sensitivity |
|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Thermocouple Type K | Temperature (°C) → mV | ~**41 µV/°C** |
| RTD (PT100) | Temperature (°C) → Ω | ~**0.385 Ω/°C** |
| Strain Gauge | Strain (ε) → ΔR/R | Gauge Factor **GF ≈ 2.0** |
| LVDT | Displacement (mm) → mV | ~**mV/mm** (linear range) |
| Piezoelectric | Force (N) → V | ~**pC/N** (charge), V/N (voltage) |
| Photodiode | Light (W/m²) → A | ~**0.5 A/W** |

### 2.3.4 Sensitivity vs. Range
- **High Sensitivity** ⇒ the instrument can detect *small* input changes.
- But it may **saturate** quickly if the range is limited.
- Sensitivity is a *local* property (slope at a point) — it is **not** the same as **span (range)**.

### 2.3.5 Real-World Utility
- Designing **amplifier stages** (e.g., charge amplifier for piezo sensors).
- Selecting **ADC bit resolution** to match sensor sensitivity.
- Determining **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** requirements.

---

## 2.4 Resolution — The Smallest Detectable Step

### 2.4.1 Formal Definition
**Resolution** is the **smallest measurable increment** or **smallest difference in input that produces a detectable change in output**. It is the *fineness* of the measurement.

### 2.4.2 Mathematical Expression
For a **digital instrument** with $n$ bits over a full scale $V_{FS}$:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

For a **general instrument**:

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{\Delta Y_{\min}}{K}
$$

where $\Delta Y_{\min}$ is the smallest output change the instrument can register (e.g., 1 LSB, 1 division, 1 count).

### 2.4.3 Worked Examples of Resolution
- A **12-bit ADC** with 0–10 V range:
  Resolution = $10 / 2^{12}$ = $10 / 4096$ ≈ **2.44 mV**.
- A **3½ digit DMM** (max display 1999) on 20 V range:
  Resolution = $20 / 1999$ ≈ **10 mV** (i.e., 0.01 V).
- A **moving-coil ammeter** with 100 divisions on a 1 mA range:
  Resolution = $1 / 100$ = **0.01 mA = 10 µA** (assuming 1-division readability).

### 2.4.4 Resolution vs. Sensitivity — The Subtle Distinction
> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusion:** Many students think *Resolution = Sensitivity*. They are **different**:
> - **Resolution** is the *smallest step* the instrument **can detect** (a discrete-quantization property).
> - **Sensitivity** is the *slope* of the response (a continuous property).
> A voltmeter may have **high sensitivity** (many V/div) but **poor resolution** (coarse scale, only 1 V steps visible).
> Conversely, a **high-resolution** DMM may have modest sensitivity if its full-scale is small.

### 2.4.5 Real-World Utility
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** µm-level resolution for diagnosis.
- **Vernier Caliper:** 0.02 mm resolution vs. ruler's 1 mm.
- **Optical Encoders:** 10,000 pulses/rev ⇒ 0.036° resolution.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates ALL must-know formulas for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Interpretation | Notes / Caveats |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $E_a = M - T$ | Same as measurand | $M$ = Measured, $T$ = True |
| 2 | Relative Error | $E_r = \dfrac{M - T}{T}$ | Dimensionless (or per-unit) | Often expressed in ppm |
| 3 | Percentage Error (% of True) | $E_\% = \dfrac{M - T}{T} \times 100$ | **%** | For resistors, instruments |
| 4 | Percentage Error (% of FS) | $E_{FS} = \dfrac{M - T}{FS} \times 100$ | **%** | For analog panel meters |
| 5 | Accuracy | $A = 1 - \vert E_r \vert$ | Per-unit or % | $A = 1$ is ideal |
| 6 | Standard Deviation (Precision) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N - 1}}$ | Same as measurand | Smaller $\sigma$ = higher precision |
| 7 | Variance | $\sigma^2$ | (Unit)² | — |
| 8 | Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ | Same as measurand | Best estimate of true value |
| 9 | Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | Output/Input units | Slope of calibration curve |
| 10 | Resolution (Digital) | $R = \dfrac{FS}{2^n}$ | Same as measurand | $n$ = number of bits |
| 11 | Resolution (Analog) | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS}}{\text{Number of divisions}}$ | Same as measurand | With 1-division readability |
| 12 | Span / Range | $\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Same as measurand | Often confused with sensitivity |
| 13 | Threshold | Smallest $X$ that produces a detectable $Y$ | Same as measurand | Below this, no response |
| 14 | Dead Zone / Dead Band | Max $X$ change that produces no $Y$ change | Same as measurand | Hysteresis-related |
| 15 | Linearity % | $\dfrac{\text{Max deviation from straight line}}{FS} \times 100$ | **%** | Independent-linearity, etc. |
| 16 | Hysteresis Error | $\dfrac{\Delta Y_{H}}{FS} \times 100$ | **%** | $\Delta Y_H$ = output diff at same input, up vs. down |
| 17 | Repeatability Error | $\dfrac{\sigma}{FS} \times 100$ | **%** | Or $3\sigma / FS \times 100$ (99.7% confidence) |
| 18 | Combined / Total Error | $\sqrt{E_{a1}^2 + E_{a2}^2 + \cdots}$ (RSS) | Same as measurand | Used when independent errors add in quadrature |
| 19 | Tolerance | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100$ | **%** | For components: 1%, 5%, 10% |
| 20 | Drift | $\dfrac{\Delta Y / \Delta t}{FS}$ | Unit/Time | Zero drift, span drift |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Pattern:** *"A 4½ digit DMM has 20,000 counts full scale. The true voltage is 12.000 V. The meter reads 11.98 V. Calculate the percentage error."* — Use the *% of Reading* formula and ensure you use the **measured** value (11.98) if the question demands *relative to reading*, or 12.000 if *relative to true*.

---

## 2.6 Relationship Between the Four Descriptors — A Conceptual Map

```
                    MEASUREMENT QUALITY
                           │
        ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
        │                  │                  │
   CORRECTNESS        REPEATABILITY       RESPONSIVENESS
        │                  │                  │
   ┌────┴────┐        ┌────┴────┐        ┌────┴────┐
   │         │        │         │        │         │
ACCURACY  ERROR   PRECISION  STABILITY SENSITIVITY RESOLUTION
(Static)  (Static) (Statistical) (Drift)  (Slope)   (Step)
```

- **Accuracy ↔ Error** are *inversely* related.
- **Precision ↔ Standard Deviation** are *inversely* related.
- **Sensitivity ↔ Slope of calibration curve** (a property of the *transfer function*).
- **Resolution ↔ Smallest measurable step** (a property of the *output stage / ADC / scale*).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **mathematical machinery** behind the four quality descriptors, derives auxiliary metrics, and walks through canonical KTU-style problem types. **All steps are explicitly shown.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Measurements to Standard Deviation

**Starting Premise:** A set of $N$ independent measurements of the same physical quantity yields values $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N$. We wish to extract (a) the *best estimate* of the true value and (b) a *measure of dispersion* (precision indicator).

### Step 1 — Define the Arithmetic Mean
The arithmetic mean is the *best linear unbiased estimator* of the true value under the assumption of independent, identically distributed random errors:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

**Logic:** The mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations. To see this, let $E = \sum (x_i - m)^2$ and differentiate with respect to $m$:

$$
\frac{dE}{dm} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - m) = 0
$$

Solving for $m$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - N \cdot m = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \bar{x}
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Sample Variance
Because we are using a *sample* (not the entire population) and the mean $\bar{x}$ was estimated from the same sample, one degree of freedom is "lost." Hence the divisor $(N - 1)$ (Bessel's correction):

$$
s^2 = \frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

### Step 3 — Take the Square Root to get Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{s^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If errors are *Gaussian* (normal distribution), then:
> - $\pm 1\sigma$ covers $\approx$ **68.27%** of measurements.
> - $\pm 2\sigma$ covers $\approx$ **95.45%** of measurements.
> - $\pm 3\sigma$ covers $\approx$ **99.73%** of measurements.
> This is why KTU examiner-accepted *repeatability* often uses $3\sigma$ as the precision index.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity from a Linear Calibration Curve

**Premise:** An instrument's output $y$ is related to its input $x$ by the calibration equation:

$$
y = a + b \cdot x
$$

Where $a$ = zero-offset (intercept) and $b$ = slope.

### Step 1 — Compute the Slope (Sensitivity)
By definition of sensitivity:

$$
K = \frac{dy}{dx}
$$

For a *linear* system, $dy/dx$ is constant:

$$
K = b
$$

### Step 2 — Generalize to a Non-Linear Sensor
For a non-linear sensor (e.g., a thermocouple at very high $T$):

$$
y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \cdots
$$

The local sensitivity at operating point $x_0$ is:

$$
K(x_0) = \left. \frac{dy}{dx} \right\vert_{x=x_0} = a_1 + 2 a_2 x_0 + 3 a_3 x_0^2 + \cdots
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Implication:** *Sensitivity is not constant for a non-linear transducer.* This is why transducer datasheets always list sensitivity as a function of operating range, e.g., *"Type K thermocouple: 41 µV/°C at 25 °C, 39 µV/°C at 500 °C."*

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Resolution of an N-bit ADC

**Premise:** A unipolar ADC with $n$ bits maps an analog input range $[0, V_{FS}]$ to digital codes $[0, 2^n - 1]$.

### Step 1 — Number of Quantization Steps
There are $2^n$ distinct codes. Hence the number of *intervals* (steps) is:

$$
N_{\text{steps}} = 2^n
$$

### Step 2 — Resolution (1 LSB)

$$
1\,\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
$$

This is the **smallest input change** that the ADC can resolve. For example, $n = 8$, $V_{FS} = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$
1\,\text{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} \approx 19.53\,\text{mV}
$$

### Step 3 — Quantization Noise
The inherent RMS quantization noise is:

$$
V_{Q,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n \cdot \sqrt{12}} = \frac{1\,\text{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

This places a **theoretical SNR limit** of:

$$
\text{SNR}_{Q} = 6.02\,n + 1.76\;\;\text{dB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this formula** — KTU frequently asks: *"A 16-bit ADC has full-scale 10 V. Find its resolution and theoretical SNR."* Answer: $10/2^{16} = 152.6\,\mu\text{V}$ and $\text{SNR} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 98.08$ dB.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Combined Error (Root-Sum-Square Method)

**Premise:** An instrument has *multiple independent sources* of error: $\pm e_1, \pm e_2, \dots, \pm e_n$. We want a *combined* uncertainty $E_{\text{total}}$.

### Step 1 — Statistical Independence Assumption
For *independent*, *uncorrelated* errors with random sign:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{e_1^2 + e_2^2 + e_3^2 + \cdots + e_n^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Arithmetic Sum (Pessimistic Bound)
If the question demands *worst-case* (all errors additive and same sign):

$$
E_{\text{worst}} = \vert e_1 \vert + \vert e_2 \vert + \cdots + \vert e_n \vert
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Distinction:** RSS (root-sum-square) is used for *random* errors. Arithmetic sum is used for *systematic/maximum* errors. Many KTU solutions erroneously use arithmetic sum even for random components — read the question carefully!

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example
A DMM spec: ±(0.05% of reading + 2 digits). Range = 200 V. Reading = 150.0 V.

Reading-error term: $0.0005 \times 150.0 = 0.075$ V
Count-error term: $2 \times 0.1$ V = $0.2$ V (resolution of 200 V range)

Combined RSS (treating as independent):

$$
E_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{0.075^2 + 0.2^2} = \sqrt{0.005625 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.045625} \approx 0.2136\,\text{V}
$$

Worst-case (arithmetic) sum:

$$
E_{\text{worst}} = 0.075 + 0.2 = 0.275\,\text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The manufacturer's spec (0.275 V) is the *worst-case bound*. The 1$\sigma$ RSS value (0.2136 V) is the *statistical* bound.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Tolerance, Accuracy, and Resolution — When Resolution Limits Accuracy

**Premise:** A digital instrument's accuracy is bounded *below* by its resolution.

### Step 1 — Resolution Bound
The smallest reading uncertainty is $\pm 1$ LSB, so:

$$
\vert \text{Error} \vert \geq \frac{1}{2} \times \text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

### Step 2 — Practical Implication
A 10-bit voltmeter with 5 V FS has resolution = 4.88 mV. Its *best possible* accuracy (ignoring calibration error) is:

$$
A_{\max} = 1 - \frac{1}{2^{n+1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2048} \approx 99.95\,\%
$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 1 — Full Worked Solution (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A voltmeter reads 49.6 V on a 100 V range. The true value (from a standard) is 50.0 V. The instrument has 100 divisions on its scale. Calculate:
1. Absolute error
2. Percentage error (% of true value)
3. Percentage error (% of full scale)
4. Accuracy
5. Resolution (assuming 1-division readability)

**Solution:**

**(1) Absolute Error:**

$$
E_a = M - T = 49.6 - 50.0 = -0.4\,\text{V}
$$

**(2) Percentage Error (% of True):**

$$
E_{\%,T} = \frac{49.6 - 50.0}{50.0} \times 100 = \frac{-0.4}{50.0} \times 100 = -0.8\,\%
$$

**(3) Percentage Error (% of Full Scale):**

$$
E_{\%,FS} = \frac{49.6 - 50.0}{100} \times 100 = \frac{-0.4}{100} \times 100 = -0.4\,\%
$$

**(4) Accuracy:**

$$
A = 1 - \left\vert \frac{M - T}{T} \right\vert = 1 - \left\vert -0.008 \right\vert = 0.992 = 99.2\,\%
$$

Or in terms of full scale:

$$
A_{FS} = 1 - 0.004 = 0.996 = 99.6\,\%
$$

**(5) Resolution:**

$$
R = \frac{\text{FS}}{\text{Divisions}} = \frac{100}{100} = 1\,\text{V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The instrument can only resolve 1 V changes. A reading of "49.6" is an interpolation; the *actual* guaranteed measurement is 49 V (or 50 V, with uncertainty).

---

## 3.7 Numerical Problem 2 — Precision Analysis

**Problem:** A pressure transducer gives 10 repeated readings (in kPa): 101.2, 101.5, 101.3, 101.4, 101.6, 101.3, 101.5, 101.4, 101.3, 101.5. Calculate the mean, standard deviation, and comment on precision.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Mean**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{10}(101.2 + 101.5 + 101.3 + 101.4 + 101.6 + 101.3 + 101.5 + 101.4 + 101.3 + 101.5)
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1014.0}{10} = 101.40\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Deviations from Mean**

| Reading $x_i$ | Deviation $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| 101.2 | $-0.20$ | $0.0400$ |
| 101.5 | $+0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 101.3 | $-0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 101.4 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 101.6 | $+0.20$ | $0.0400$ |
| 101.3 | $-0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 101.5 | $+0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 101.4 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 101.3 | $-0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 101.5 | $+0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$0.1400$** |

**Step 3: Variance**

$$
s^2 = \frac{0.1400}{10 - 1} = \frac{0.1400}{9} \approx 0.01556\,\text{kPa}^2
$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.01556} \approx 0.1247\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Precision Comment**
- $\sigma \approx 0.125$ kPa on a mean of 101.4 kPa ⇒ coefficient of variation = $0.125/101.4 \times 1000 = 1.23$ ‰.
- **Comment:** *The transducer is highly precise (low dispersion).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Further Note on Accuracy:** If the *true* pressure was $100.0$ kPa, then bias = $101.4 - 100.0 = 1.4$ kPa. This is a *systematic* (not random) error ⇒ the transducer is **precise but not accurate** (it has a calibration offset of 1.4 kPa).

---

## 3.8 Numerical Problem 3 — Sensitivity Computation

**Problem:** A load cell has the calibration equation $V_{\text{out}} = 0.002\,F + 0.05$ (V), where $F$ is the applied force in newtons. Find:
1. The sensitivity
2. The output when $F = 500$ N
3. The output when $F = 1000$ N
4. The force corresponding to $V_{\text{out}} = 1.05$ V

**Solution:**

**(1) Sensitivity:** $K = 0.002$ V/N = 2 mV/N.

**(2) At $F = 500$ N:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 0.002 \times 500 + 0.05 = 1.00 + 0.05 = 1.05\,\text{V}
$$

**(3) At $F = 1000$ N:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 0.002 \times 1000 + 0.05 = 2.00 + 0.05 = 2.05\,\text{V}
$$

**(4) Force for $V_{\text{out}} = 1.05$ V:**

$$
1.05 = 0.002 F + 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.002 F = 1.00 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; F = 500\,\text{N}
$$

---

## 3.9 Numerical Problem 4 — Resolution of a Digital Voltmeter

**Problem:** A 3½ digit DMM has ranges 200 mV, 2 V, 20 V, 200 V, 1000 V. The full-scale count is 1999. On the 20 V range, a voltage of 12.34 V is measured. Determine:
1. The resolution on the 20 V range
2. The absolute accuracy if the manufacturer's spec is ±(0.1% of reading + 3 digits)

**Solution:**

**(1) Resolution:** With full-scale count 1999 on a 20 V range:

$$
R = \frac{20}{1999} = 0.01\,\text{V} = 10\,\text{mV}
$$

Wait — the 20 V range *full-scale* is 20 V, but the max count is 1999. So 1 digit = 0.01 V. Resolution = **10 mV**.

**(2) Accuracy Spec Evaluation:**
- Reading-error term: $0.001 \times 12.34 = 0.01234$ V
- Count-error term: $3 \times 0.01 = 0.03$ V
- **Total** (worst case): $0.01234 + 0.03 = 0.04234$ V
- **Total** (RSS): $\sqrt{0.01234^2 + 0.03^2} = \sqrt{0.000152 + 0.0009} = \sqrt{0.001052} \approx 0.0324$ V

So the reading is $12.34 \pm 0.042$ V (worst case) or $12.34 \pm 0.032$ V (statistical).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section renders the conceptual architecture of measurement quality as a **functional block-level architecture flow** in Mermaid. The diagrams map the *interactions* between the four quality descriptors, the calibration chain, and the instrument's internal stages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams instead of physical drawings?** The four quality descriptors are *abstract* properties of an instrument, not physical shapes. A block-level functional flow best captures the logical interactions in a way students can reproduce in an answer script.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchy of Measurement Quality Descriptors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurement System] --> B[Static Performance]
    A --> C[Dynamic Performance]
    B --> D[Accuracy]
    B --> E[Precision]
    B --> F[Sensitivity]
    B --> G[Resolution]
    D --> D1[Defined by: True Value Reference]
    E --> E1[Defined by: Repeatability and Standard Deviation]
    F --> F1[Defined by: Slope of Calibration Curve]
    G --> G1[Defined by: Smallest Detectable Step]
    D1 --> H[Quality Metric Computed]
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[Instrument Datasheet Specification]
    I --> J[Application Suitability Decision]
    C --> K[Bandwidth Slew Rate Response Time]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Laboratory Industrial Medical Aerospace Decision]
```

**Reading Guide:** This flowchart traces the *provenance* of each quality descriptor, from the abstract notion of "static performance" down to the practical decision of *where* to deploy the instrument.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Instrument Internal Signal Chain with Quality Descriptors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Physical Measurand<br/>Temperature Pressure Force] --> S1[Sensor Element<br/>Transducer]
    S1 --> S2[Signal Conditioning<br/>Amplifier Filter]
    S2 --> S3[ADC<br/>Digitization]
    S3 --> S4[Display Output]
    S1 --> Q1[Sensitivity K<br/>V per unit input]
    S2 --> Q2[Linearity Hysteresis Drift]
    S3 --> Q3[Resolution<br/>1 LSB equals FS over 2 to the n]
    S4 --> Q4[Readability<br/>Display Digits and Counts]
    Q1 --> R[Overall Measurement Quality]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    Q4 --> R
    R --> F[Accuracy and Precision<br/>Quantified Metrics]
```

**Reading Guide:** Each internal stage of an instrument contributes to one or more quality descriptors. Note that **resolution is fixed at the ADC stage**, while **sensitivity is set at the transducer stage**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree: Choosing Between Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Select an Instrument] --> Q1{What is the<br/>Smallest Input<br/>Change of Interest}
    Q1 -->|Very Small| H1[Prioritize High Resolution<br/>and High Sensitivity]
    Q1 -->|Large| L1[Standard Resolution Sufficient]
    H1 --> Q2{Is the True Value<br/>Accurately Known}
    Q2 -->|Yes Critical| P1[Prioritize High Accuracy<br/>Low Error and Calibrated]
    Q2 -->|No Just Trend Needed| P2[Prioritize Precision<br/>Low Standard Deviation]
    P1 --> End[Choose Calibrated Digital<br/>Instrument with Traceability]
    P2 --> End
    L1 --> End
```

**Reading Guide:** This decision tree is **directly applicable to KTU lab viva** questions. *"Should I use a 3½ digit or 4½ digit DMM?"* — Walk the tree: smallest change → resolution; known reference → accuracy; trend → precision.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Calibration Curve & Sensitivity Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Calibration[Ideal Linear Calibration]
        I1[Input x equals 0<br/>Output y equals a] -->|Slope K| I2[Input x equals x1<br/>Output y equals a plus K times x1]
        I2 -->|Slope K| I3[Input x equals x2<br/>Output y equals a plus K times x2]
    end
    I1 --> D[Intercept a equals Zero Offset]
    I2 --> D2[Sensitivity K equals dy by dx<br/>Constant for Linear Sensor]
    I3 --> D2
    D2 --> Note[Higher K means Higher Sensitivity<br/>Resolution equals 1 LSB at Output Stage]
    Note --> Out[Calibrated Output Y<br/>Equals a plus K times X]
```

**Reading Guide:** For a linear transducer, the *slope* of the calibration line is the sensitivity. The *intercept* is the zero-offset (which is corrected during zero-calibration). Resolution is set downstream by the smallest output step the read-out can distinguish.

---

## 4.5 ASCII Schematic — Error Distribution Histogram (Visual Concept)

```
            Gaussian Error Distribution (Precision σ)
            
   Count ▲
         │       ╭──────╮
         │      ╱        ╲
         │     ╱          ╲
         │    ╱            ╲
         │   ╱              ╲
         │  ╱                ╲
         │ ╱                  ╲
         │╱                    ╲
         └──────────────────────────▶ Deviation from True Value
              -3σ  -2σ  -1σ  μ  +1σ  +2σ  +3σ
              
         μ = True Value
         σ = Standard Deviation (Precision Index)
         
   68.3% of readings fall in  μ ± σ       → HIGH PRECISION
   95.5% of readings fall in  μ ± 2σ      → MODERATE PRECISION
   99.7% of readings fall in  μ ± 3σ      → LOW PRECISION
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cue for Exams:** If the KTU question shows a diagram with shots clustered tightly around a point, that is *precision*. If they are around the bullseye, that is *accuracy*. **Both** clusters around the bullseye = ideal.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Inter-Relationship of Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Measurement Error] --> S[Systematic Error<br/>Bias and Zero Offset Drift]
    E --> R[Random Error<br/>Statistical Scatter]
    E --> G[Gross Error<br/>Human Mistakes and Transcription]
    S --> S1[Correctable by Calibration]
    R --> R1[Reducible by Averaging<br/>Standard Deviation Decreases with 1 over root N]
    G --> G1[Eliminated by Procedure and Training]
    S1 --> Q[Overall Quality]
    R1 --> Q
    G1 --> Q
    Q --> Out[Trustworthy Measurement]
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the **error taxonomy** the KTU syllabus implicitly references. Notice that **systematic** errors affect *accuracy* (bias), **random** errors affect *precision* (scatter), and **gross** errors are *not* a quality metric — they are *avoidance issues*.
