# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Distribution Note (GZEST204 ESE):**
> - **Part A (Short Answer, 2–3 marks):** Direct definition or short computation.
> - **Part B (Long Answer, 14 marks):** Typically *two* sub-parts (7 + 7) with internal choice.
> - **Module 4 Weightage:** Approximately 20% of the syllabus — expect 1 Part-A + 1 Part-B question from this module.

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define the following with one example each: (a) Accuracy, (b) Precision, (c) Resolution, (d) Sensitivity.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Accuracy:** The degree of closeness of a measured value to the true (or accepted) value of the measurand.
*Example:* A voltmeter reads 49.9 V when the true voltage is 50.0 V — the reading is accurate to within 0.1 V (i.e., 0.2% error).

**(b) Precision:** The degree of mutual agreement (closeness) among a series of repeated measurements of the same quantity under identical conditions. Mathematically, precision is inversely related to the **standard deviation** $\sigma$ of the readings.
*Example:* A DMM gives readings 50.01, 50.02, 50.01, 50.02 V — these are highly precise (very low spread).

**(c) Resolution:** The smallest measurable increment (or step) that an instrument can detect or display. For an $n$-bit digital instrument, $\text{Resolution} = V_{FS}/2^n$.
*Example:* A 3½ digit DMM on 20 V range has 0.01 V (10 mV) resolution.

**(d) Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in output to the change in input (slope of calibration curve), $K = \Delta Y / \Delta X$.
*Example:* A Type-K thermocouple has a sensitivity of approximately 41 µV/°C.

> [!TIP]
> **Keywords to include for full marks:** *True value, standard deviation, smallest step, slope* — these are the examiner's check-words.

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** A digital voltmeter on its 20 V range reads 12.46 V. The true value is 12.50 V. Calculate the (i) absolute error, (ii) percentage error (relative to true value), and (iii) percentage error (relative to full scale).

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Absolute Error:**

$$
E_a = M - T = 12.46 - 12.50 = -0.04\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) % Error (relative to true value):**

$$
E_{\%,T} = \frac{-0.04}{12.50} \times 100 = -0.32\,\%
$$

**(iii) % Error (relative to full scale):**

$$
E_{\%,FS} = \frac{-0.04}{20} \times 100 = -0.20\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The instrument is more accurately described as **0.32% inaccuracy** (relative to true) or **0.20%** (relative to full scale) — KTU accepts either, but you **must** state which one.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS) — Internal Choice (A or B)

### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the four quality parameters of measurement: Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, and Resolution. Distinguish clearly between accuracy and precision with a suitable example.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] A strain gauge with a nominal resistance of 120 Ω and gauge factor 2.0 is bonded to a steel beam. The beam is loaded such that the strain $\varepsilon = 800\,\mu\varepsilon$. The change in resistance is measured by a Wheatstone bridge with all four arms equal. The bridge is excited by 5 V DC. Calculate: (i) the output voltage of the bridge, and (ii) the sensitivity of the gauge in V/V.

---

**Model Solution to Q (a):**

**1. Accuracy:** Defined as the closeness of a measured value to the *true* or *standard* value. Quantified by *absolute error*, *relative error*, or *percentage error*. A high-accuracy instrument has low bias. Real-world examples: precision calipers (0.02 mm), 6½ digit DMMs.

**2. Precision:** The closeness of agreement among repeated measurements under identical conditions. Quantified by *standard deviation* $\sigma$ or *variance* $\sigma^2$. A precise instrument may be *biased* (inaccurate). Real-world example: a CNC machine that repeatedly cuts at the *same* wrong dimension.

**3. Sensitivity:** Ratio $\Delta Y / \Delta X$. It is the slope of the calibration curve. It characterizes the *strength* of an instrument's response. For a Type-K thermocouple: 41 µV/°C; for a strain gauge: $V_R / R \cdot GF$.

**4. Resolution:** The smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output. For digital systems: $V_{FS}/2^n$ (1 LSB). For analog: 1 division of the scale. Resolution is the **theoretical limit** of an instrument.

**Distinction (Accuracy vs. Precision):**
- **Accuracy** → *closeness to truth* (systematic, bias-driven).
- **Precision** → *closeness among readings* (random, statistical).
- A *precise but inaccurate* instrument has good repeatability but a constant offset. A *accurate but imprecise* instrument has high scatter but is *unbiased on average*.
- **Example:** Five readings: 49.8, 49.9, 50.0, 50.1, 50.2 V, with true value 50.0 V ⇒ **accurate and precise**. Five readings: 52.0, 52.1, 52.0, 52.0, 52.0 V, with true value 50.0 V ⇒ **precise but inaccurate** (offset of 2.0 V).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** Students who write *"accuracy and precision are the same"* or *"accuracy means how close the readings are to each other"* will lose **3 of 7 marks** outright. The examiner looks for the *bullseye vs. cluster* distinction explicitly.

---

**Model Solution to Q (b):**

**Given:**
- $R = 120$ Ω
- Gauge Factor $GF = 2.0$
- Strain $\varepsilon = 800\,\mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$
- Bridge excitation $V_{ex} = 5$ V DC
- All four arms of the bridge are nominally equal.

**Step 1: Find the change in gauge resistance.**

The strain-gauge equation:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 2.0 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Delta R = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 0.192\,\Omega
$$

So the active arm becomes $R + \Delta R = 120.192$ Ω.

**Step 2: Bridge Output Voltage (Quarter-Bridge Configuration).**

For a Wheatstone bridge with one active arm:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \quad \text{(for small } \Delta R\text{)}
$$

$$
V_{\text{out}} = \frac{5}{4} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.25 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.0\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3: Sensitivity in V/V.**

Sensitivity (normalized to excitation) is:

$$
S = \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{ex} \cdot \varepsilon}
$$

$$
S = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.5\,\text{V/V per unit strain}
$$

Or, more commonly, the gauge sensitivity in terms of $\Delta R / R$ is:

$$
S_{\text{gauge}} = \frac{V_{\text{out}} / V_{ex}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.0004}{800 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.5\,\text{(per unit strain)}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $V_{\text{out}} = 2.0$ mV
- (ii) Sensitivity = 0.5 V/V per unit strain (or equivalently $\frac{1}{4} \cdot GF = 0.5$)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students forget to *convert* strain to dimensionless form. Writing $S = 2.0 \text{ mV} / 5 \text{ V} = 0.0004$ V/V is **not** sensitivity — that is normalized output. Sensitivity is normalized output per unit *input* (strain), giving 0.5 V/V/ε.

---

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] A temperature transducer produces an output $V = 0.01\,T + 0.05$ (volts) where $T$ is the temperature in °C. Determine: (i) the sensitivity, (ii) the output at $T = 200$ °C, (iii) the temperature that produces an output of 2.55 V, and (iv) the resolution if the output is read by a 4½ digit DMM on a 5 V range.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Explain the different types of errors in measurement (systematic, random, gross) with examples. How does each type affect accuracy and precision? Compute the standard deviation of the following set of pressure readings (in kPa): 102.1, 102.4, 102.2, 102.3, 102.5, 102.2, 102.4, 102.3.

---

**Model Solution to Q (a):**

Given: $V = 0.01\,T + 0.05$.

**(i) Sensitivity:**

$$
K = \frac{dV}{dT} = 0.01\,\text{V/°C} = 10\,\text{mV/°C}
$$

**(ii) Output at $T = 200$ °C:**

$$
V = 0.01 \times 200 + 0.05 = 2.00 + 0.05 = 2.05\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Temperature for $V = 2.55$ V:**

$$
2.55 = 0.01\,T + 0.05 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.01\,T = 2.50 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T = 250\,\text{°C}
$$

**(iv) Resolution on a 4½ digit DMM, 5 V range:**

A 4½ digit DMM has 19,999 counts (max display 1.9999 on the lowest decade, scaled). On a 5 V range, full scale = 5.000 V, with 1 digit = 0.001 V = 1 mV.

$$
R_{\text{DMM}} = 0.001\,\text{V}
$$

To find the **temperature resolution** of the complete system:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\Delta V}{K} = \frac{0.001}{0.01} = 0.1\,\text{°C}
$$

So the system can resolve **0.1 °C** changes in temperature.

---

**Model Solution to Q (b):**

**1. Types of Errors:**

**(i) Systematic Errors:** Repeatable, predictable deviations from the true value. Caused by calibration error, zero-drift, environmental factors, instrument loading.
- *Affects Accuracy* (bias).
- *Example:* A miscalibrated voltmeter that reads 2% high across its entire range.

**(ii) Random Errors:** Unpredictable, statistical fluctuations due to noise, electrical interference, observer parallax.
- *Affects Precision* (scatter).
- *Example:* A sensitive balance giving 100.01, 100.03, 99.98 mg for the same mass.

**(iii) Gross Errors:** Human mistakes like misreading, transcription error, instrument misuse.
- *Not a quality metric* — to be avoided.
- *Example:* Reading 12.4 V as 124 V by misplacing the decimal.

**2. Standard Deviation Calculation:**

**Step 1: Mean**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{102.1 + 102.4 + 102.2 + 102.3 + 102.5 + 102.2 + 102.4 + 102.3}{8}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{818.4}{8} = 102.30\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Deviations & Squared Deviations**

| $x_i$ (kPa) | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|-------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 102.1 | $-0.20$ | $0.0400$ |
| 102.4 | $+0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 102.2 | $-0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 102.3 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| 102.5 | $+0.20$ | $0.0400$ |
| 102.2 | $-0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 102.4 | $+0.10$ | $0.0100$ |
| 102.3 | $0.00$ | $0.0000$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$0.1200$** |

**Step 3: Variance**

$$
s^2 = \frac{0.1200}{8 - 1} = \frac{0.1200}{7} \approx 0.01714\,\text{kPa}^2
$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.01714} \approx 0.1309\,\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

- $\sigma \approx 0.13$ kPa on a mean of 102.30 kPa.
- Coefficient of variation = $\frac{0.131}{102.30} \times 100\% = 0.128\%$.
- The transducer is **highly precise** (low random scatter).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** 
> 1. **Bessel's correction** ($N-1$ divisor) — students using $N$ instead of $N-1$ get **0 marks** for the SD computation. Always use $N-1$ for sample data.
> 2. **Units** — leave $\sigma$ in kPa (same as data). Do NOT write "kPa²" for $\sigma$; that is variance. (Variance has units squared; SD has the same units as the data.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall & Common Mistakes Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Quality-of-Measurement Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy and precision** — KTU boards almost always include a 2-mark question to test this. Memorize the *bullseye* analogy.
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** — Use $N-1$, not $N$, in the standard deviation formula.
> 3. **Mixing up the % error denominator** — Always state whether it is *% of true value* or *% of full scale*.
> 4. **Resolution = Sensitivity misconception** — They are not the same. Resolution is a *step size*; sensitivity is a *slope*.
> 5. **Ignoring units in sensitivity** — Always state sensitivity with its **units**, e.g., 10 mV/°C, not just "10".
> 6. **Writing sensitivity in V/V** when the input is non-electrical — Sensitivity can have *any* units, e.g., V/N, A/kPa, mV/°C.
> 7. **Forgetting the "linear region"** for non-linear sensors — Sensitivity is *local*; quote the operating point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Revision Before Walking into the Exam Hall:**

- ✅ **Accuracy** = *closeness to true value*. Error metrics: absolute, relative, % of FS.
- ✅ **Precision** = *closeness among repeated readings*. Metric: standard deviation $\sigma$.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** = *output per unit input*. Metric: slope of calibration curve, $K = \Delta Y / \Delta X$.
- ✅ **Resolution** = *smallest detectable step*. For digital: $V_{FS}/2^n$. For analog: 1 division.
- ✅ Accuracy and precision are **independent** — high precision does not imply high accuracy (and vice versa).
- ✅ **Systematic errors** → hurt *accuracy*. **Random errors** → hurt *precision*. **Gross errors** → neither — they are mistakes.
- ✅ Standard deviation uses **Bessel's correction**: divide by $N - 1$, not $N$.
- ✅ Coefficient of variation: $CV = \sigma / \bar{x} \times 100\%$ — a *dimensionless* precision index.
- ✅ Sensitivity of a linear system is **constant**; for non-linear systems it is **local** (at the operating point).
- ✅ Resolution of an $n$-bit ADC = $V_{FS} / 2^n$. SNR $= 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- ✅ Combine *random* errors by **RSS** (root-sum-square). Combine *worst-case* errors by **arithmetic sum**.
- ✅ Accuracy is bounded below by resolution: $|E| \geq 1/2 \cdot \text{Resolution}$.
- ✅ **Memorize** the following standard relationships:
  - 1σ = 68.3% confidence (Gaussian)
  - 2σ = 95.5% confidence
  - 3σ = 99.7% confidence
- ✅ Always **state units** in sensitivity (mV/°C, V/N, A/kPa, etc.) and **state the reference** in % error (true vs. FS).
- ✅ The four quality descriptors map onto instrument stages: **Sensitivity** (transducer) → **Linearity/Hysteresis** (signal conditioning) → **Resolution** (ADC) → **Readability** (display).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Note (GZEST204 - Module 4):**
> *Students who can (1) state the four quality descriptors with one-line definitions, (2) compute % error and standard deviation correctly with proper formulas, (3) compute ADC resolution and sensitivity from a calibration equation, and (4) distinguish accuracy from precision with a numerical example will comfortably score 100% on any Module-4 question. Focus your revision on the "Standard Deviation" derivation and the "Sensitivity from Calibration Curve" derivation — these are the two highest-weight derivations in the ESE.*

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZEST204 - BASIC ELECTRICAL & ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensors: Working Principle and Applications - Pressure (Strain Gauge, Bourden Gauge), Temperature (RTD, Thermocouple), Proximity (Capacitive Sensor, Ultrasonic Sensor), Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SENSOR (KTU Definition):** A sensor is a physical device that detects, measures, and responds to a physical stimulus (such as pressure, temperature, light, motion, or proximity) and converts it into an electrical signal that can be read by an observer or by an instrument.

A sensor is essentially the **"eyes and ears"** of any electronic/electrical system. Just as human skin converts heat into nerve impulses our brain understands, a sensor converts physical phenomena into voltages or currents an electronic circuit can understand.

### 1.2 Classification of Sensors Covered in Module 4

| Stimulus (Input) | Sensor Type | Physical Quantity Measured |
|------------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| Mechanical Force | **Strain Gauge** | Pressure / Force / Weight |
| Fluid Pressure | **Bourdon Gauge** | Pressure of gases/liquids |
| Heat | **RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)** | Temperature |
| Heat (Voltage Mode) | **Thermocouple** | Temperature |
| Distance / Presence | **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** | Position / Object detection |
| Distance / Presence | **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Distance via sound echo |
| Vibration / Motion | **Accelerometer** | Acceleration / Vibration |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies for Each Sensor

### 🔹 Strain Gauge — "The Tiny Whisper of Stretch"
Imagine pulling a rubber band. The longer it gets, the thinner it becomes, and the harder it is for current to pass through it. A strain gauge works on the same principle: when a metal foil is stretched, its electrical resistance changes. This tiny change in resistance is a measure of how much force is being applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle (Strain Gauge):** Converts mechanical deformation (strain, $\varepsilon$) into a change in electrical resistance ($R$).

### 🔹 Bourdon Gauge — "The Curved Tube That Straightens Under Pressure"
Think of a thin, curved paper straw. If you blow air into it from one end while keeping the other end sealed, the straw tends to straighten out because the internal pressure pushes the walls outward. A Bourdon gauge uses a curved metal tube that tries to straighten when pressurized, and the tiny straightening motion is converted into a pointer deflection on a dial.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle (Bourdon Gauge):** A curved metallic tube tends to straighten when subjected to internal fluid pressure; this mechanical displacement is read on a calibrated dial.

### 🔹 RTD — "Metal Wire That Whispers Hotter = More Resistance"
Almost every metal conducts electricity *better* when cold and *worse* when hot. Platinum, the noble metal, behaves very predictably in this regard. An RTD exploits this relationship: a known current is passed through a platinum wire, and the voltage drop across it tells us the temperature using the **linear relationship**:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

### 🔹 Thermocouple — "Two Metals, One Hot Junction, A Tiny Voltage"
Hold a piece of iron wire and a piece of copper wire together, twist them at one end, and heat the twisted joint. You will measure a small DC voltage (in millivolts) across the free ends! This is the **Seebeck Effect**. Different metal pairs produce different voltages for the same temperature difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Temperature for Thermocouples:** $T_{ref} = 0^\circ C$ (ice point). The output voltage is a function of $T_{hot} - T_{cold}$.

### 🔹 Capacitive Proximity Sensor — "Detecting Without Touching"
Remember rubbing a balloon on your hair and then holding it near tiny paper bits — they jump toward the balloon! A capacitive proximity sensor creates a small electric field; when any object (metal, plastic, wood, liquid) enters this field, it disturbs the capacitance. The sensor detects this disturbance and outputs a switching signal.

### 🔹 Ultrasonic Sensor — "Bat Echoes for Machines"
A bat screams inaudible sound (ultrasound) and listens for the echo to know the distance of insects. An ultrasonic sensor emits a high-frequency sound pulse (typically **40 kHz**) and measures the time it takes for the echo to return. Distance is then calculated using the simple formula:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the speed of sound in air ($\approx 343$ m/s at $20^\circ C$).

### 🔹 Accelerometer — "A Tiny Mass on a Tiny Spring"
Imagine a small ball attached to a spring inside a box. When you shake the box, the ball lags behind, stretching the spring. The amount of stretch is a direct measure of acceleration. Modern MEMS accelerometers use microscopic silicon structures that behave exactly like this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Accelerometer Working Principle:** Based on **Newton's Second Law**, $F = ma$. The displacement of a proof mass suspended on a spring is proportional to acceleration.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|----------------------|--------|----------------|
| Speed of sound in air (at 20°C) | $v$ | **343 m/s** |
| Standard reference temperature | $T_0$ | **0 °C = 273.15 K** |
| Temperature coefficient of Platinum | $\alpha_{Pt}$ | **3.85 × 10⁻³ /°C** |
| Temperature coefficient of Copper | $\alpha_{Cu}$ | **3.93 × 10⁻³ /°C** |
| Standard RTD resistance at 0°C | $R_0$ (Pt100) | **100 Ω** |
| Standard RTD resistance at 0°C | $R_0$ (Pt1000) | **1000 Ω** |
| Standard Seebeck coefficient (K-type) | $S$ | **~41 µV/°C** |
| Ultrasonic operating frequency | $f$ | **40 kHz** |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 STRAIN GAUGE — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil (typically **constantan**, a copper-nickel alloy) arranged in a zigzag pattern and bonded to a flexible backing. When the underlying material (the *substrate*) experiences strain, the foil stretches or compresses, changing its length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$, which in turn changes its electrical resistance.

### Governing Equation — Derivation
The resistance of a conductor is given by:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

When strained by a small amount:
- Length changes by $dL$, so $L \rightarrow L + dL$
- Area changes by $dA$, so $A \rightarrow A - dA$
- Resistivity changes slightly by $d\rho$

Taking the differential:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

By definition, **axial strain** is:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L}$$

And **Poisson's ratio** gives lateral strain:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

The **Gauge Factor (GF)** is defined as:

$$GF = \frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For metallic strain gauges, **GF ≈ 2.0** (typically 1.8 to 2.2). For semiconductor gauges, GF can be as high as 100–200.

### Wheatstone Bridge (Strain Gauge Readout)
A single strain gauge gives a tiny resistance change (milliohms). To convert this into a measurable voltage, a **Wheatstone bridge** is used:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right]$$

For a single active gauge ($R_1 = R + \Delta R$, others equal to $R$):

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

### Applications
- Weighing scales (load cells)
- Pressure transducers
- Torque measurement in rotating shafts
- Structural health monitoring of bridges and aircraft

---

## 2.2 BOURDON GAUGE — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
A Bourdon tube is a curved, flattened, closed-end metal tube (elliptical cross-section). When pressurized fluid enters the open end, the internal pressure tries to make the cross-section more circular. Since the tube is curved, this shape change forces the tube to **straighten slightly**. The closed (free) end moves by a small displacement, which is mechanically amplified by a linkage to drive a pointer.

### Pressure–Deflection Relationship
The deflection $d$ of the free end of a C-type Bourdon tube is approximately:

$$d = K \cdot P \cdot \frac{a^3 \cdot b \cdot \theta}{E \cdot t^2}$$

where:
- $P$ = applied pressure
- $a$ = minor axis of tube cross-section
- $b$ = major axis
- $\theta$ = curvature angle
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $E$ = Young's modulus
- $K$ = constant of proportionality

### Applications
- Industrial pressure gauges
- Boiler pressure monitoring
- Hydraulic and pneumatic systems
- Gas cylinder pressure indicators

---

## 2.3 RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
The resistance of a pure metal increases approximately linearly with temperature due to increased lattice vibration scattering of conduction electrons.

### Linear Approximation Equation

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

### Callendar–Van Dusen Equation (Higher Accuracy)

For $T \geq 0°C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2\right]$$

For $T < 0°C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3\right]$$

For standard **Pt100 (ITS-90)**: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$

### Why Platinum?
- Chemically inert
- Linear and stable
- High melting point (1768°C)
- Available in pure form

### Applications
- Industrial process control
- Laboratory temperature measurement
- HVAC systems
- Cryogenic applications (with proper design)

---

## 2.4 THERMOCOUPLE — Deep Theory

### Working Principle — Seebeck Effect
When two dissimilar metals (say Chromel and Alumel) are joined at one end (the *hot junction*) and the other ends are kept at a reference temperature (the *cold junction*), a small DC voltage (EMF) is produced. This is the **Seebeck Effect**.

### Governing Equation

$$E = \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT$$

For small temperature ranges (linear approximation):

$$E \approx S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

where $S$ is the **Seebeck coefficient** (in µV/°C).

### Common Thermocouple Types

| Type | Metals (+ / −) | Range (°C) | Sensitivity |
|------|----------------|-----------|-------------|
| **K** | Chromel / Alumel | −200 to +1260 | ~41 µV/°C |
| **J** | Iron / Constantan | 0 to +760 | ~50 µV/°C |
| **T** | Copper / Constantan | −200 to +370 | ~40 µV/°C |
| **R** | Pt−13%Rh / Pt | 0 to +1480 | ~10 µV/°C |
| **S** | Pt−10%Rh / Pt | 0 to +1480 | ~10 µV/°C |
| **B** | Pt−30%Rh / Pt−6%Rh | 0 to +1700 | ~9 µV/°C |

### Cold Junction Compensation (CJC)
Since thermocouples measure *differential* temperature, the cold junction must be held at a known temperature (usually 0°C) or electronically compensated. Modern instruments use a semiconductor temperature sensor (like LM35) at the cold junction to add an equivalent voltage.

### Applications
- Furnace temperature monitoring
- Exhaust gas temperature in vehicles
- Industrial ovens and kilns
- Power plant boiler monitoring

---

## 2.5 CAPACITIVE PROXIMITY SENSOR — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
A capacitive sensor forms one plate of a capacitor. The target object forms the other plate (or alters the dielectric). As the target approaches, the capacitance changes:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of dielectric
- $A$ = effective plate area
- $d$ = distance between plates

For a cylindrical capacitive proximity sensor, the change in capacitance as a target approaches is approximately:

$$\Delta C \approx C_0 \cdot \frac{x}{d_0}$$

where $x$ is the displacement of the target and $d_0$ is the nominal gap.

### Advantages
- Can detect **non-metallic** objects (plastic, wood, liquid, paper)
- High sensitivity at small distances
- No contact → no wear

### Applications
- Liquid level sensing
- Touch screens
- Material thickness measurement
- Object detection on conveyor belts

---

## 2.6 ULTRASONIC SENSOR — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
The sensor contains a piezoelectric transducer that emits a **40 kHz** sound pulse. The pulse travels through air, reflects off the target, and returns to the sensor. By measuring the round-trip time $t$, the distance is calculated.

### Distance Equation

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

The speed of sound in air depends on temperature:

$$v = 331 + 0.6 T \quad (\text{m/s, with } T \text{ in °C})$$

At 20°C: $v = 331 + 0.6(20) = 343$ m/s.

### Resolution and Range
- Typical range: 2 cm to 400 cm
- Resolution: ~0.3 cm
- Beam angle: ~15° (cone-shaped)

### Applications
- Parking sensors in cars
- Robotic obstacle avoidance
- Liquid level measurement in tanks
- Object counting on production lines

---

## 2.7 ACCELEROMETER — Deep Theory

### Working Principle
A proof mass $m$ is suspended by a spring (stiffness $k$) inside a frame. When the frame accelerates, the mass tends to stay at rest (Newton's First Law), causing relative displacement $x$ between the mass and the frame.

### Governing Equation
At equilibrium under acceleration $a$:

$$k \cdot x = m \cdot a \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{m}{k} \cdot a$$

This displacement is measured using a capacitive, piezoresistive, or piezoelectric method.

### MEMS Capacitive Accelerometer
The proof mass forms one plate of a differential capacitor. Acceleration tilts the mass, changing $C_1$ and $C_2$ oppositely. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1 + C_2} \propto a$$

### Sensitivity Specification
A typical MEMS accelerometer (e.g., ADXL345) has:
- Range: ±2g to ±16g
- Sensitivity: 256 LSB/g (at ±2g mode)
- Bandwidth: 0.5 Hz to 1.6 kHz

### Applications
- Smartphones (screen rotation, step counting)
- Vehicle airbag deployment systems
- Industrial vibration monitoring
- Gaming controllers
- Drones and robotics

---

## 2.8 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Sensor | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|--------|---------|-----------|------|
| 1 | Strain Gauge | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | GF, $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Strain Gauge (Bridge) | $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $V_{ex}$, GF, $\varepsilon$ | Volts |
| 3 | Bourdon | $d = K \cdot P \cdot a^3 b \theta / (E t^2)$ | tube geometry, P | meters |
| 4 | RTD (Linear) | $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ | $R_0$, $\alpha$, T | Ω |
| 5 | RTD (Quadratic) | $R_T = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | A, B constants | Ω |
| 6 | Thermocouple | $E = S \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | S in µV/°C | Volts |
| 7 | Capacitive | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | A, d, $\varepsilon_r$ | Farads |
| 8 | Ultrasonic | $d = v \cdot t / 2$ | v = 343 m/s, t | meters |
| 9 | Speed of sound | $v = 331 + 0.6T$ | T in °C | m/s |
| 10 | Accelerometer | $x = (m/k) \cdot a$ | m, k, a | meters |
| 11 | Newton's 2nd Law | $F = m \cdot a$ | m, a | Newtons |
| 12 | Strain Definition | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | length change | dimensionless |
| 13 | Wheatstone | $V_{out} = V_{ex}[R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4)]$ | four arms | Volts |
| 14 | Gauge Factor | $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (d\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ | Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | Seebeck Voltage | $E = \int (S_A - S_B) dT$ | Seebeck coefficients | Volts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gauge Factor of a Strain Gauge

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between fractional change in resistance and mechanical strain.

**Given:**
- A conductor of length $L$, area $A$, resistivity $\rho$
- Resistance: $R = \rho L / A$
- Axial strain: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial}$

**Step 1 — Start with the resistance equation:**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides (a standard trick for fractional changes):**

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the strain term:**

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Express area change in terms of length change using Poisson's ratio:**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $D$, $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, so:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dD}{D} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - (-2\nu \varepsilon) = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon(1 + 2\nu)$$

**Step 7 — Define Gauge Factor as the ratio of fractional resistance change to strain:**

$$GF = \frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{GF = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical value for constantan (typical strain gauge material):**
- Poisson's ratio: $\nu \approx 0.33$
- Piezoresistive term for metals: $(d\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon \approx 0$ to 0.4
- Therefore: $GF \approx 1 + 2(0.33) + 0.3 = \mathbf{1.96 \approx 2.0}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RTD Resistance at a Given Temperature

**Objective:** Calculate the resistance of a Pt100 RTD at 100°C.

**Given:**
- $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ (at 0°C)
- $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\ /\ ^\circ C$ (platinum, IEC 60751)
- $T_0 = 0^\circ C$
- $T = 100^\circ C$

**Step 1 — Apply the linear RTD equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3}) (100 - 0)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the product:**

$$R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + 0.385\right]$$

$$R_{100} = 100 \times 1.385$$

$$\boxed{R_{100} = 138.5\ \Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using quadratic Callendar–Van Dusen equation:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + AT + BT^2\right]$$
$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3},\ B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})(100) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(100)^2\right]$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \left[1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775\right]$$
$$R_{100} = 100 \times 1.38506 = 138.506\ \Omega$$

Both methods agree to within rounding — confirms the linear approximation is good for $0 \leq T \leq 100°C$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Distance Measurement with Ultrasonic Sensor

**Objective:** An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of 5.8 ms. Find the distance to the target. (Temperature = 20°C)

**Given:**
- $t = 5.8$ ms $= 5.8 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $T = 20°C$

**Step 1 — Calculate the speed of sound at 20°C:**

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 331 + 12 = 343\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the distance equation:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$d = \frac{343 \times 5.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$343 \times 5.8 = 1989.4$$
$$1989.4 \times 10^{-3} = 1.9894\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 2 (round trip):**

$$d = \frac{1.9894}{2} = 0.9947\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 0.995\ \text{m} \approx 1\ \text{m}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Voltage of Strain Gauge Wheatstone Bridge

**Objective:** A strain gauge with $GF = 2.0$ is subjected to a strain $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$. The bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Find the output voltage.

**Given:**
- $GF = 2.0$
- $\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$
- $V_{ex} = 10$ V
- Single active arm in a quarter-bridge

**Step 1 — Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Bridge output for a single active arm:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{10}{4} \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to millivolts:**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 2.5\ \text{mV}}$$

This tiny voltage is why strain gauge signals need **instrumentation amplifiers** with high gain and excellent noise rejection.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Accelerometer Sensitivity

**Objective:** An accelerometer has a proof mass $m = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg and a spring constant $k = 1$ N/m. Find the displacement for $a = 1g = 9.81\ m/s^2$.

**Given:**
- $m = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg = 0.5 mg
- $k = 1$ N/m
- $a = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law on the proof mass:**

$$F = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2 — The spring force balances the inertial force:**

$$k \cdot x = m \cdot a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$:**

$$x = \frac{m \cdot a}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$x = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 9.81}{1}$$

$$\boxed{x = 4.905 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 4.905\ \mu\text{m}}$$

This incredibly small displacement (about 5 micrometres) is why MEMS accelerometers need very sensitive capacitive or piezoresistive readout electronics.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Guide

| Application | Best Sensor | Reason |
|-------------|-------------|--------|
| Measuring car tyre pressure | **Strain Gauge** | High accuracy, small size, reliable |
| Industrial gas pressure (high) | **Bourdon Gauge** | Robust, no power needed, simple |
| Furnace temperature >1000°C | **Thermocouple (K/R type)** | High temperature range, fast response |
| Precise lab temperature | **RTD (Pt100)** | High accuracy and stability |
| Detecting water level in a tank | **Capacitive Sensor** | Works with non-metallic liquids |
| Car reverse parking assist | **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Works in dark, measures 0.2–4 m |
| Smartphone screen rotation | **MEMS Accelerometer** | Tiny size, low power, fast response |
| Airbag deployment | **MEMS Accelerometer** | Fast response (<1 ms), reliable |
| Touch screen on phone | **Capacitive Sensor** | Detects finger without pressure |
| Detecting a wooden block on conveyor | **Capacitive Sensor** | Works with non-metals (inductive cannot) |
| Weighbridge for trucks | **Strain Gauge Load Cell** | High capacity, accurate |
| Distance to a wall in a robot | **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Measures 5 cm to several meters |
| Engine exhaust temperature | **Thermocouple (K-type)** | Withstands >800°C, rugged |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Block Diagram of a Complete Sensor-Based Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSICAL QUANTITY] --> B[SENSOR ELEMENT]
    B --> C[TRANSDUCER STAGE]
    C --> D[CONDITIONING AMPLIFIER]
    D --> E[ADC]
    E --> F[MICROCONTROLLER]
    F --> G[DISPLAY]
    F --> H[ACTUATOR / ALARM]
    B -.-> B1["Strain Gauge / RTD / Thermocouple / Ultrasonic / Accelerometer"]
    D -.-> D1["Wheatstone Bridge / Op-Amp / Filter"]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Strain Gauge in a Wheatstone Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Bridge [Wheatstone Bridge]
        R1[R1] --- NodeTop((+))
        R2[R2] --- NodeTop
        R3[R3] --- NodeBot((-))
        R4[R4] --- NodeBot
    end
    Vex[Vex DC Excitation] --> NodeTop
    NodeBot --> GND[GND]
    NodeTop --> Vout[Vout = Vex/4 times deltaR by R]
    NodeBot --> Vout
    R1 -. ACTIVE GAUGE .-> R1
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Bourdon Gauge — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pressurized Fluid Inlet] --> B[Fixed Open End]
    B --> C[Curved Elliptical Tube]
    C --> D[Free Closed End Moves]
    D --> E[Linkage and Gear Train]
    E --> F[Pointer on Calibrated Dial]
    F --> G[Pressure Reading]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: RTD Measurement Circuit (3-Wire Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Current Source I] --> L1[Lead Wire 1]
    L1 --> R0[Pt100 RTD Element]
    R0 --> L2[Lead Wire 2]
    L2 --> GND
    A --> L3[Lead Wire 3 - Compensation]
    L3 --> R0
    V[Voltmeter reads V across R0] -.-> T[Convert R to T using Callendar-Van Dusen]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Thermocouple Circuit with Cold Junction Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Junction T_hot] --> B[Metal A Chromel]
    B --> C[Metal B Alumel]
    C --> D[Cold Junction T_ref]
    D --> E[CJC Circuit with LM35]
    E --> F[Op-Amp Summing Stage]
    F --> G[ADC to Microcontroller]
    G --> H[Display in Degree C]
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Capacitive Proximity Sensor — Operating Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator generates AC] --> B[Drives Sense Plate]
    B --> C[Electric Field extends outward]
    C --> D{Object Enters Field?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Capacitance Changes]
    D -- No --> F[Nominal Capacitance C0]
    E --> G[Detector Circuit triggers]
    F --> H[Output stays OFF]
    G --> I[Output Switch ON]
```

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7: Ultrasonic Sensor — Time-of-Flight Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller triggers pulse] --> B[Tx emits 40 kHz burst]
    B --> C[Sound travels to target]
    C --> D[Target reflects echo]
    D --> E[Rx receives echo]
    E --> F[Timer measures round trip t]
    F --> G[Compute d equals v times t divided by 2]
    G --> H[Output distance in cm]
```

---

## 4.8 Diagram 8: MEMS Accelerometer — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Acceleration a] --> B[Frame moves with a]
    B --> C[Proof Mass lags behind]
    C --> D[Differential Capacitor C1 and C2]
    D --> E[Interface ASIC amplifies delta]
    E --> F[ADC converts to digital]
    F --> G[I2C or SPI to host]
    G --> H[Acceleration value in g or m per s squared]
```

---

## 4.9 Diagram 9: Comparison Flow — Contact vs Non-Contact Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sensors] --> B{Physical Contact?}
    B -- YES --> C[Strain Gauge, RTD, Thermocouple, Bourdon]
    B -- NO --> D[Capacitive, Ultrasonic, Inductive]
    C --> E[Touch the measurand directly]
    D --> F[Use field, sound, or radiation]
    E --> G[Can suffer wear, contamination]
    F --> H[Long life, hygienic, works in hazardous areas]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a sensor. List any four types of sensors with the physical quantity they measure.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sensor:** A sensor is a device that detects a physical stimulus (such as heat, light, pressure, motion) and converts it into an electrical signal that can be measured or recorded.

**Four Types of Sensors:**

| # | Sensor | Physical Quantity |
|---|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Strain Gauge** | Force / Pressure / Weight |
| 2 | **RTD** | Temperature |
| 3 | **Thermocouple** | Temperature |
| 4 | **Capacitive Proximity Sensor** | Position / Presence / Level |
| 5 | **Ultrasonic Sensor** | Distance |
| 6 | **Accelerometer** | Acceleration / Vibration |

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Seebeck Effect. Name the two metals used in a K-type thermocouple and mention its typical sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Seebeck Effect:** When two dissimilar metals are joined at one end (hot junction) and the other ends are maintained at a different (reference) temperature, a small DC voltage (EMF) is generated that is proportional to the temperature difference.

- **K-type metals:** Chromel (Ni-Cr alloy) and Alumel (Ni-Al alloy)
- **Sensitivity:** approximately **41 µV/°C**
- **Range:** −200°C to +1260°C

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of a **strain gauge** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for **Gauge Factor (GF)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil (usually constantan) arranged in a zigzag pattern on a flexible backing. It is bonded to the surface of the structure whose strain is to be measured. When the structure deforms under load, the foil stretches or compresses, changing its length, cross-sectional area, and hence its electrical resistance. This change in resistance, measured using a Wheatstone bridge, is proportional to the strain.

**Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.2 of these notes)

**Derivation of Gauge Factor:**

Given $R = \rho L / A$:

Taking natural log and differentiating:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

Using $\varepsilon = dL/L$ and $dA/A = -2\nu \varepsilon$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon (1 + 2\nu)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GF = \frac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

For constantan, GF ≈ 2.0.

---

**Q. (b)** A strain gauge has $GF = 2.1$ and is mounted on a steel beam. The gauge resistance is $120\ \Omega$. The beam is loaded such that the strain produced is $800\ \mu\varepsilon$. The gauge is connected as one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $GF = 2.1$
- $R = 120\ \Omega$
- $\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$
- $V_{ex} = 10$ V
- Single active arm

**Step 1 — Fractional change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} = 1680 \times 10^{-6} = 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — Change in resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 120 \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2016\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output voltage (single active arm):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{10}{4} \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate:**

$$V_{out} = 2.5 \times 1.68 \times 10^{-3} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 4.2\ \text{mV}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of an **RTD** and a **thermocouple** for temperature measurement. Compare them in terms of range, accuracy, sensitivity, and cost. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector):**
- A pure metal wire (typically platinum) whose resistance increases linearly with temperature.
- Uses equation: $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$
- Standard: Pt100 (100 Ω at 0°C)
- Readout: Measure voltage drop across RTD when a known current flows; convert to R, then to T.

**Thermocouple:**
- Two dissimilar metals joined at hot junction; open ends at cold junction.
- Generates EMF by Seebeck effect: $E = S (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$
- Requires Cold Junction Compensation (CJC).
- Output is in millivolts — needs amplification.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RTD (Pt100) | Thermocouple (K-type) |
|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Range** | −200 to +850 °C | −200 to +1260 °C |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 °C (high) | ±2 °C (moderate) |
| **Sensitivity** | ~0.385 Ω/°C | ~41 µV/°C |
| **Linearity** | Excellent | Moderate (needs polynomial) |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Response Time** | Slower (thermal mass) | Faster |
| **Self-Heat Error** | Yes (needs low excitation current) | No |
| **Drift** | Very low | Higher over time |

---

**Q. (b)** An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of $t = 6\ \text{ms}$ to a wall. The ambient temperature is $T = 30^\circ C$. Find the distance to the wall. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $t = 6$ ms $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $T = 30°C$

**Step 1 — Speed of sound at 30°C:**

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Distance equation:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$d = \frac{349 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$d = \frac{2.094}{2} = 1.047\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 1.047\ \text{m} \approx 1.05\ \text{m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in ultrasonic distance** — The most common mistake. Echo travels *to and back*, so the *total* distance is $v \cdot t$, but the *one-way* distance is $v \cdot t / 2$. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Mixing units** — Convert ms to seconds; convert µV to V; convert µε to ε before calculation.
> 3. **Using $v = 343$ m/s without noting temperature** — At 20°C, v = 343 m/s, but at other temperatures use $v = 331 + 0.6T$. KTU may give temperature to test this.
> 4. **Forgetting Cold Junction Compensation** in thermocouple problems — Always mention CJC in theory questions.
> 5. **Gauge Factor confusion** — Some students write $GF = 1 + \nu$; the correct expression includes the piezoresistive term.
> 6. **Confusing Bourdon with Bellows/Bellows Gauge** — Bourdon is a *curved tube*, not a bellows. Mention the elliptical cross-section and tendency to become circular.
> 7. **Capacitive vs Inductive sensors** — KTU often asks: "Can a capacitive sensor detect plastic?" Answer: YES (most plastics have ε_r > 1). Inductive cannot detect non-metals.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Strain Gauge:** Metallic foil; resistance changes with strain; Gauge Factor GF ≈ 2.0 for metals, 100+ for semiconductors; needs Wheatstone bridge.
- [x] **Bourdon Gauge:** Curved elliptical tube straightens under pressure; purely mechanical, no power needed; used in industrial pressure gauges.
- [x] **RTD:** Platinum wire (Pt100 = 100 Ω at 0°C); linear $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha \Delta T]$; high accuracy, narrow range.
- [x] **Thermocouple:** Two dissimilar metals, Seebeck Effect; K-type (Chromel-Alumel) most common; needs Cold Junction Compensation.
- [x] **Capacitive Sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; detects metals and non-metals; used in touchscreens and level sensing.
- [x] **Ultrasonic Sensor:** 40 kHz burst; $d = vt/2$; speed of sound depends on temperature: $v = 331 + 0.6T$.
- [x] **Accelerometer:** Proof mass on spring; $x = (m/k) \cdot a$; MEMS version uses differential capacitor; range ±2g to ±16g typical.
- [x] **Wheatstone Bridge equation** must be remembered: $V_{out} = V_{ex}[R_1/(R_1+R_2) - R_3/(R_3+R_4)]$.
- [x] **Standard values:** Pt100 = 100 Ω at 0°C, $R_0$ for Pt1000 = 1000 Ω, $v$ at 20°C = 343 m/s, $g$ = 9.81 m/s².
- [x] **Gauge Factor formula** $GF = 1 + 2\nu + (d\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ is the most-asked derivation in KTU exams.
- [x] **RTD linear equation** $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ appears in nearly every KTU paper.
- [x] **Ultrasonic distance** $d = v \cdot t / 2$ — don't forget the divide-by-2.
- [x] **Thermocouple equation** $E = S (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ with K-type sensitivity ≈ 41 µV/°C.
- [x] **Capacitive sensor** can detect *non-metallic* objects (this is its main advantage over inductive).
- [x] **Bourdon tube** uses an *elliptical* cross-section that tries to become *circular* under pressure.

### 🎯 Most Important KTU-Frequent Questions
1. Derive Gauge Factor — *asked in almost every KTU paper*
2. Compare RTD and Thermocouple — *guaranteed question*
3. Ultrasonic distance calculation — *numerical, 7 marks*
4. Working principle of Bourdon/strain gauge — *7-mark theory*
5. Working principle of accelerometer — *7-mark theory*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Internet of Things (IoT)

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a globally interconnected network of physical objects ("things") that are embedded with **sensors, software, actuators, and communication modules** which enable these objects to **collect, transmit, and exchange data** over the internet without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard):** *"IoT is a network of physical devices, vehicles, home appliances, and other items embedded with electronics, software, sensors, actuators, and connectivity which enables these objects to connect and exchange data."*

The formal ITU (International Telecommunication Union) definition, adopted in **Recommendation ITU-T Y.2060 (2012)**, is:

> *"A global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology of IoT

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Thing** | Any physical or virtual object capable of being identified and integrated into a communication network. |
| **Sensor** | A device that detects physical changes (temperature, light, motion) and converts them into electrical signals. |
| **Actuator** | A device that converts electrical signals into a physical action (e.g., switching ON an LED). |
| **Gateway** | A bridge that connects sensor nodes to the internet/cloud using protocols like **Wi-Fi, ZigBee, or LoRa**. |
| **Cloud** | A remote server infrastructure that stores, processes, and analyses IoT data. |
| **M2M** | Machine-to-Machine communication — direct data exchange between devices without human input. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "IoT as the Human Nervous System"

Imagine the human body:
- Your **skin and eyes** act as *sensors* (collecting light, temperature, pressure).
- Your **spinal cord and brain** act as the *gateway and cloud* (processing the data).
- Your **muscles** act as *actuators* (responding by moving).
- The **nervous system** is the *communication network* connecting everything.

In the same way, IoT connects everyday devices — lights, cars, parking lots, refrigerators — to a "digital nervous system" that senses, thinks, and acts. The only difference is that this "body" is a **smart city**, **smart home**, or **smart factory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Student Takeaway:** IoT = **Sense → Collect → Transmit → Analyze → Act**. This is the *fundamental loop* you must remember for every KTU question.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard parameters are frequently used in KTU numerical problems on IoT:
> - **Standard Operating Voltage of IoT Nodes:** **3.3 V** or **5 V DC**
> - **Industrial Sensor Supply:** **24 V DC** (IEEE 1451 standard for smart transducers)
> - **Common Wireless Frequencies:** **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi/ZigBee), **868/915 MHz** (LoRa), **5 GHz** (Wi-Fi 5/6)
> - **Minimum Data Rate considered in IoT planning:** **100 kbps** (IEEE 802.15.4 baseline)
> - **Cloud Latency Target (Smart City):** **< 100 ms** (3GPP TS 22.261 standard for ultra-reliable low-latency communication)
> - **Power consumption of a typical wireless sensor node:** **~1 mW to 100 mW** in sleep/active mode

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Operational Cycle of IoT

The IoT ecosystem operates as a **closed-loop, four-stage process**. KTU questions often ask students to "explain the working of IoT" or "describe the sensing-to-action pipeline." The four stages are:

**Stage 1 — Sensing / Data Acquisition**
- Physical sensors (temperature, light, IR, ultrasonic) detect real-world parameters.
- These sensors convert physical quantities into analog electrical signals.
- An **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** digitizes the signal.
- Example: An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** detects ambient light; a **PIR (Passive Infrared)** sensor detects motion.

**Stage 2 — Data Transmission / Communication**
- The digitized data is transmitted via wired or wireless mediums.
- **Communication Protocols used:**
  - Short-range: **Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC, RFID**
  - Medium-range: **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**
  - Long-range / LPWAN: **LoRa, NB-IoT, SigFox**
  - Cellular: **4G LTE, 5G**
- The data is sent to a **gateway**, which routes it to the cloud.

**Stage 3 — Data Processing / Cloud Computing**
- Cloud servers receive the raw data.
- **Data analytics, AI/ML algorithms**, and databases process the data to extract meaningful insights.
- Decisions are computed (e.g., "Streetlight should dim to 40% brightness because no pedestrian is detected").

**Stage 4 — Actuation / Response**
- The processed decision is sent back through the gateway to the **actuator**.
- The actuator performs a physical action (turning on a streetlight, opening a barrier, displaying a message on a screen).

### 2.2 The 5-Layer Architecture of IoT (KTU High-Yield)

The **standard 5-layer IoT architecture** is the most frequently asked diagram in KTU exams:

**Layer 1 — Perception / Sensor Layer (Physical Layer)**
- The "eyes and ears" of the IoT system.
- Contains **sensors, actuators, RFID tags, and readers**.
- Responsible for collecting raw data from the environment.

**Layer 2 — Network / Connectivity Layer**
- Acts as the "nervous system" — transmits data.
- Uses technologies like **Wi-Fi, ZigBee, 3G/4G/5G, LoRa, and Ethernet**.
- Handles **routing, addressing, and data integrity**.

**Layer 3 — Middleware / Processing / Edge Layer**
- The "translator" — receives raw data, filters it, processes it, and decides whether to forward to the cloud or handle locally (edge computing).
- Performs **data aggregation, filtering, and device management**.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer**
- The "user interface" — provides application-specific services to the end user.
- Examples: **Smart home apps, traffic dashboards, energy monitoring systems**.

**Layer 5 — Business Layer (Management Layer)**
- The "brain" — manages the entire IoT system, builds business models, monitors performance, and applies analytics to improve services.
- Handles **privacy, security, ROI calculations, and decision-making**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative KTU-Approved Architecture:** Some textbooks (and KTU questions) present a **3-layer architecture**:
> - **Perception Layer** → **Network Layer** → **Application Layer**
> 
> Always mention the **5-layer model in detail** for 7-mark questions and the **3-layer model** as a quick summary.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of IoT

IoT is the **backbone of Industry 4.0** and modern smart infrastructure:
- **Energy Savings:** Smart street lighting reduces municipal energy bills by up to **60–80%**.
- **Traffic Management:** Smart parking reduces average search time by **~30%** in dense urban areas.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** IoT sensors in industrial motors predict failures before they occur.
- **Healthcare:** Remote patient monitoring reduces hospital readmission rates.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are routinely used in KTU numerical problems on IoT. Memorize the **units and boundary conditions**.

| Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| **Power Consumption of an IoT Node** | $P = V \times I$ | $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amps, $P$ in Watts. Typical IoT node: **3.3 V × 0.05 A = 0.165 W**. |
| **Energy Consumption per Day** | $E = P \times t$ | $P$ in Watts, $t$ in hours. Units: **Wh (Watt-hours)**. |
| **Sensor Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | To avoid aliasing, sample at least twice the highest signal frequency. |
| **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \times \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio. $C$ in **bits per second (bps)**. |
| **Streetlight Energy Savings** | $\Delta E = n \times (P_{baseline} - P_{smart}) \times t$ | $n$ = number of lights, $P$ in Watts, $t$ in hours. Result in **Wh**. |
| **Smart Parking Search Time Reduction** | $\eta = \frac{T_{without} - T_{with}}{T_{without}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ = efficiency improvement in **%**. |
| **Wireless Range (Friis Equation — Free Space)** | $P_r = P_t \times G_t \times G_r \times \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P_r$ received power, $P_t$ transmitted power, $G$ antenna gains, $\lambda$ wavelength, $d$ distance. |
| **Cost-Benefit Payback Period** | $T_{payback} = \frac{C_{install}}{S_{annual}}$ | $C_{install}$ = installation cost, $S_{annual}$ = annual savings. Result in **years**. |
| **Daily Energy of a System** | $E_{daily} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times t_i$ | Sum of power × time for each operating state (e.g., bright, dim, off). |

### 2.5 The "Why" and "How" Behind Smart City IoT

**Why is IoT critical for Smart Cities?**
By 2050, **~68% of the world's population** will live in urban areas (UN estimate). Traditional infrastructure (manual switches, fixed-timer streetlights, human-driven parking enforcement) cannot scale. IoT provides **automation, data-driven decisions, and remote control**.

**How does it work?**
Every "thing" (streetlight, parking slot, traffic signal) gets a **unique ID (IPv6 addressing)**, a **sensor** to perceive, a **microcontroller** (e.g., ESP32, Arduino) to think locally, and a **communication module** to talk to the cloud.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Models & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** and solved numerical models that are highly representative of KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) questions. No step is skipped.

### 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Energy Savings Calculation for Smart Street Lighting

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A municipality installs **200 smart LED streetlights** on a 2 km road. Each LED operates at **P_normal = 60 W** when at full brightness and **P_dim = 20 W** in dim mode. Using a motion sensor system, the lights run in **bright mode for 4 hours** (peak traffic) and **dim mode for 8 hours** (low traffic) daily. They are completely **OFF for 12 hours** (daytime). Compare the daily energy consumption with a **conventional HPSV (High-Pressure Sodium Vapour) system** running at **150 W continuously for 12 hours nightly**.

**Step 1 — Identify the daily operating profile of the smart LED system:**

The smart LED system operates in three states per day:
- State A: Bright mode for 4 hours
- State B: Dim mode for 8 hours
- State C: OFF for 12 hours

**Step 2 — Calculate the daily energy of ONE smart LED streetlight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED, one} &= (P_{bright} \times t_{bright}) + (P_{dim} \times t_{dim}) + (P_{off} \times t_{off}) \\
&= (60 \times 4) + (20 \times 8) + (0 \times 12) \\
&= 240 + 160 + 0 \\
&= 400 \text{ Wh/day per light}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total daily energy for 200 smart LED streetlights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{LED, total} &= n \times E_{LED, one} \\
&= 200 \times 400 \\
&= 80{,}000 \text{ Wh/day} \\
&= 80 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the daily energy of the conventional HPSV system (per light):**

The HPSV lamp runs at 150 W for 12 continuous hours nightly and is OFF during the day for 12 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{HPSV, one} &= P_{HPSV} \times t_{night} \\
&= 150 \times 12 \\
&= 1800 \text{ Wh/day per light}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the total daily energy for 200 HPSV streetlights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{HPSV, total} &= 200 \times 1800 \\
&= 360{,}000 \text{ Wh/day} \\
&= 360 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the absolute daily energy saved:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{daily} &= E_{HPSV, total} - E_{LED, total} \\
&= 360 - 80 \\
&= 280 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the percentage energy savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{savings} &= \frac{\Delta E_{daily}}{E_{HPSV, total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{280}{360} \times 100\% \\
&= 77.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Annual savings assuming ₹8 per kWh:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{annual} &= \Delta E_{daily} \times 365 \times \text{Rate} \\
&= 280 \times 365 \times 8 \\
&= 817{,}600 \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The smart LED system saves **280 kWh per day** (≈ **77.78% savings**), translating to approximately **₹8.17 lakh per year** in electricity cost savings for the municipality.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Smart Parking Occupancy Detection Using Ultrasonic Sensor

**Problem Statement:**
A shopping mall has **500 parking slots**, each fitted with an **ultrasonic sensor** (HC-SR04 type) mounted on the ceiling. The sensor works on the **time-of-flight principle**: it emits an ultrasound burst, and the time taken to receive the echo is measured. The sensor's speed of sound assumption is **v = 343 m/s**. If the measured echo time is **t = 4.5 ms** for one slot, determine:
- (i) The distance from the sensor to the ground/obstruction.
- (ii) The binary occupancy status (assume threshold: occupied if distance < 1.5 m).
- (iii) The number of free slots if 327 slots report "occupied."

**Step 1 — Recall the Time-of-Flight equation:**

The distance is given by half the total travel time (since the sound travels to the object and back):

$$
d = \frac{v \times t}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

Given: $v = 343 \text{ m/s}$ and $t = 4.5 \text{ ms} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{343 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.5435}{2} \\
&= 0.77175 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the occupancy threshold logic:**

The threshold for an "occupied" slot is **d < 1.5 m** (since a car's height brings the measured distance well below 1.5 m when present).

Here, $d = 0.77175 \text{ m} < 1.5 \text{ m}$ → The slot is **OCCUPIED**.

**Step 4 — Calculate the number of free slots:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{free} &= N_{total} - N_{occupied} \\
&= 500 - 327 \\
&= 173 \text{ slots}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the parking occupancy efficiency (informational):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{occ} &= \frac{N_{occupied}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{327}{500} \times 100\% \\
&= 65.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The distance is **0.772 m**, the slot is **occupied**, and there are **173 free slots** (occupancy = **65.4%**).

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Wireless Link Budget for Smart Streetlight Communication

**Problem Statement:**
A LoRa gateway communicates with a smart streetlight node located **d = 500 m** away. The transmitted power is **P_t = 14 dBm**, the antenna gains are **G_t = G_r = 2 dBi**, and the operating frequency is **f = 868 MHz**. Calculate the **received power (P_r)** using the free-space Friis equation (in dB form). Comment on whether the signal is receivable if the receiver sensitivity is **−110 dBm**.

**Step 1 — Convert all values to dB/dBm form:**

We have $P_t = 14$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi. The path loss $PL_{dB}$ is to be computed.

**Step 2 — Calculate the wavelength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{868 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.3456 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the free-space path loss in linear scale first:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL &= \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 \\
&= \left(\frac{4 \pi \times 500}{0.3456}\right)^2 \\
&= (18170.05)^2 \\
&= 3.301 \times 10^8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert path loss to dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PL_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}(3.301 \times 10^8) \\
&= 10 \times (8.518) \\
&= 85.18 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Friis transmission equation in dB form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r (dBm) &= P_t (dBm) + G_t (dBi) + G_r (dBi) - PL_{dB} \\
&= 14 + 2 + 2 - 85.18 \\
&= -67.18 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with receiver sensitivity:**

Since $P_r = -67.18$ dBm and receiver sensitivity is $-110$ dBm, the condition is:

$$
-67.18 \text{ dBm} \gg -110 \text{ dBm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Signal is STRONGLY RECEIVABLE}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Comment:** A received power **42.82 dB above** the sensitivity floor means the link has an excellent **fade margin**, ensuring reliable communication even in adverse weather or with minor obstructions.

**Final Answer:** $P_r = -67.18$ dBm → Signal is **easily detectable** (margin of **42.82 dB** above sensitivity).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Smart vs Conventional City Infrastructure

| Parameter | Conventional System | IoT-Based Smart System | Improvement |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| Streetlight Operation | Manual ON/OFF, fixed timers | Motion + LDR sensor-based adaptive dimming | **~70–80%** energy saved |
| Parking Enforcement | Manual巡查 by personnel | Ultrasonic/IR sensors with cloud dashboard | **~30%** time saved per driver |
| Fault Detection | Reported by citizens (delayed) | Real-time automatic alerts | Detection time: **days → seconds** |
| Data Logging | Paper registers / no data | Continuous cloud-based analytics | **100%** data availability |
| Maintenance Cost | High (frequent physical checks) | Low (predictive maintenance) | **~40%** OPEX reduction |
| User Experience | Poor (no real-time info) | Excellent (mobile app guidance) | Significantly improved |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the Internet of Things (IoT). List any FOUR key components of an IoT system.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of interconnected physical devices embedded with **sensors, software, and communication technologies** that enable them to collect, transmit, and exchange data over the internet without human intervention.

The **four key components** of an IoT system are:

1. **Sensors / Actuators** — to sense physical parameters and perform actions.
2. **Connectivity** — Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, or cellular networks for data transmission.
3. **Gateway / Microcontroller** — local processing unit (e.g., ESP32, Arduino).
4. **Cloud / Data Processing Unit** — stores, analyses, and visualises the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Mentioning a *real-world example* (e.g., smart streetlight) instantly earns a **bonus mark**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between the Perception Layer and the Network Layer in IoT architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Perception Layer | Network Layer |
|---------|------------------|---------------|
| **Position** | Bottommost (Layer 1) | Second layer |
| **Function** | Senses and collects data from the physical world | Transmits the collected data to the cloud/gateway |
| **Components** | Sensors, actuators, RFID tags, cameras | Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, routers, gateways |
| **Data Type** | Analog or digital raw signals | Packets of digital data |
| **Example** | A DHT11 temperature sensor reading room temperature | The ESP32 module sending that data over Wi-Fi to a cloud server |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The Perception Layer **senses**, the Network Layer **communicates**. KTU examiners love this distinction.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture of IoT**. Describe the function of each layer in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design and explain an **IoT-based smart street lighting system** for a 2 km municipal road. Include the sensors used, communication technology, and energy-saving calculations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **five-layer architecture of IoT** is the most widely accepted reference model for IoT systems. Each layer performs a specific role:

**1. Perception Layer (Physical / Sensor Layer):**
This is the bottommost layer that interacts directly with the physical environment. It consists of **sensors** (LDR, PIR, temperature, gas), **actuators** (relays, motors), **RFID tags**, and **barcode readers**. Its primary role is to **detect, collect, and identify** physical parameters.

**2. Network Layer (Connectivity / Transport Layer):**
This layer handles **data transmission** between the perception layer and the processing layers. It uses technologies such as **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4), LoRaWAN, Bluetooth, 3G/4G/5G, and Ethernet**. It also performs **routing, addressing, and ensures data integrity**.

**3. Middleware Layer (Processing / Edge Layer):**
This layer provides **data analytics, filtering, aggregation, and device management**. It may also handle **edge computing** — processing data close to the source before forwarding to the cloud. Protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, and HTTP** operate here.

**4. Application Layer:**
This layer delivers **application-specific services** to the user, such as **smart home control apps, traffic dashboards, industrial monitoring software, and health tracking apps**. It is the layer the end user directly interacts with.

**5. Business Layer (Management Layer):**
The topmost layer manages the **entire IoT system**, including **business models, revenue generation, privacy, security, application effectiveness, and strategic decisions**. It uses analytics output to improve services and ROI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Diagram:** Draw the block diagram from **Section 4.1** for full 7 marks. Always **label the layers vertically** and **arrow upwards** from Perception to Business Layer.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Design of IoT-based Smart Street Lighting System:**

**System Overview:**
The system is designed for a **2 km municipal road with 200 smart LED streetlights** spaced 10 m apart. Each light is equipped with sensors and a wireless module that connects to a central cloud server.

**Sensors Used in Each Light Unit:**

| Sensor | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** | Detects ambient sunlight to automatically switch ON/OFF at dusk/dawn. |
| **PIR Motion Sensor** | Detects presence of pedestrians or vehicles within a 10 m radius. |
| **Current Sensor (ACS712)** | Monitors LED current for fault detection. |

**Communication Technology:**
**LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network)** is preferred for streetlight communication because it offers:
- Long range (up to 5 km in urban areas)
- Low power consumption
- Low data rate suitable for sensor telemetry
- A single gateway can handle **thousands of nodes**

**Control Logic (Microcontroller: ESP32):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } LDR_{value} < \text{threshold}_{dark} \text{ AND PIR}_{status} = 0 \Rightarrow \text{LED at 30\% brightness (DIM)} \\
\text{If } LDR_{value} < \text{threshold}_{dark} \text{ AND PIR}_{status} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{LED at 100\% brightness (BRIGHT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy-Saving Calculation:**

Assume: 200 lights, $P_{bright} = 60$ W, $P_{dim} = 20$ W, 4 hours bright, 8 hours dim, 12 hours OFF daily.

Energy saved per day (vs. 150 W HPSV running 12 hours):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 200 \times \left[(150 \times 12) - \left((60 \times 4) + (20 \times 8)\right)\right] / 1000 \\
&= 200 \times \left[1800 - 400\right] / 1000 \\
&= 200 \times 1.4 \\
&= 280 \text{ kWh/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annual Savings:** $\Delta E \times 365 = 280 \times 365 = 102{,}200$ **kWh/year**, which at ₹8/kWh = **₹8,17,600/year** (≈ ₹8.18 lakhs).

**Additional Features:**
- **Real-time fault detection** — current sensor reports abnormal LED current to the cloud.
- **Centralized dashboard** — municipal officer sees the live status of all 200 lights.
- **Predictive maintenance** — alerts sent when LED efficiency drops.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What is a **Smart City**? List **FOUR major challenges** in traditional urban infrastructure that IoT can solve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an **IoT-based Smart Parking System** for a 500-slot shopping mall. Explain the working, components, and data flow. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **Smart City** is an urban area that uses **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)** and **IoT infrastructure** to manage city assets efficiently — including transportation, lighting, water, waste, and public services — with the goal of improving **quality of life, sustainability, and economic viability**.

**Four Major Challenges Solved by IoT:**

**1. Energy Inefficiency in Street Lighting:**
Traditional streetlights operate on fixed timers or manual switching, wasting energy during low-traffic hours. **IoT-enabled smart streetlights with motion sensors** dynamically dim or brighten, achieving **~70–80% energy savings**.

**2. Traffic Congestion and Parking Woes:**
Drivers in dense urban areas spend an average of **~17 hours per week** searching for parking. **IoT-based smart parking systems** with ultrasonic sensors guide drivers to free slots, reducing search time by **~30%**.

**3. Inefficient Waste Management:**
Conventional waste collection follows fixed schedules regardless of bin fill level. **IoT-enabled smart bins** with ultrasonic fill-level sensors alert the municipality **only when bins are 80% full**, optimizing collection routes.

**4. Public Safety and Surveillance:**
Manual policing is limited. **IoT-based CCTV, gunshot detection, and air quality sensors** provide real-time monitoring, enabling faster emergency response.

**Additional Challenges (Bonus Marks):**
- Water leakage detection
- Pollution monitoring
- Smart grid energy distribution

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Design of IoT-based Smart Parking System for a 500-Slot Mall:**

**System Objective:**
Provide real-time information on parking slot availability to drivers via a **mobile app**, reduce search time, and automate entry/exit barriers.

**Components Used:**

| Component | Quantity | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor** | 500 (one per slot) | Detects presence/absence of a vehicle using time-of-flight. |
| **Arduino Nano / ESP32** | 50 (one per 10 slots) | Aggregates sensor data from a cluster of 10 slots. |
| **Raspberry Pi Gateway** | 1 (per floor) | Collects data from all clusters, forwards to cloud. |
| **MQTT Broker (Cloud)** | 1 | Lightweight message protocol for IoT data transfer. |
| **Mobile App (Flutter/React)** | 1 | Displays live parking map with free/occupied slots. |
| **Servo Motor / Barrier** | 2 (entry + exit) | Automates entry/exit with RFID or QR code. |

**Working Principle — Time-of-Flight Distance Calculation:**

Each ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz burst. The echo is received after time $t$, and the distance is:

$$
d = \frac{v \times t}{2} = \frac{343 \times t}{2}
$$

**Occupancy Decision Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } d < 1.5 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{Slot OCCUPIED} \\
\text{If } d \geq 1.5 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{Slot FREE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Data Flow:**
1. Ultrasonic sensor → Cluster Arduino (per 10 slots)
2. Cluster Arduino → Raspberry Pi Gateway (per floor)
3. Raspberry Pi → Cloud MQTT Broker (over Wi-Fi/Ethernet)
4. Cloud → Mobile App (via WebSocket)
5. Cloud → LED Display at mall entrance (showing free count)
6. Mobile App → Driver → Navigates to free slot

**Sample Performance Metrics:**

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total slots | 500 |
| Average search time (traditional) | 12 minutes |
| Average search time (smart system) | 3.5 minutes |
| Time saved per driver | **~70%** |
| False positives (due to swinging objects) | Handled by software debouncing + averaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Aliasing Safeguard:** The system takes **3 consecutive distance readings** within 200 ms and uses the **median value** to avoid false occupancy detection due to swinging bags or pedestrians passing below the sensor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "IoT" with "Internet"** — IoT is a *network of physical objects*; it is not the same as a web-based information system.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the diagram in the architecture question** — a missing block diagram will cost you at least **2 to 3 marks** out of 7.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the application layer in the 5-layer model** — students often miss Layer 5 (Business Layer), and examiners specifically test for it.
> 4. **In numerical problems, always show units** (Wh, kWh, dBm) — missing units lead to **1-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Do NOT use the same sensor for both sensing and actuation** — sensors *sense*, actuators *act*. KTU examiners will deduct marks if these terms are used interchangeably.
> 6. **Always state the communication protocol** in design questions (Wi-Fi, LoRa, ZigBee) — vague answers like "it sends data to the cloud" are penalized.

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU exam.

**✅ Must-Remember Definitions:**
- IoT = network of physical objects with sensors, software, and connectivity.
- Smart City = urban area using IoT/ICT for efficient resource management.

**✅ Must-Remember 5-Layer Architecture (in order):**
- Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → **Business** (don't forget the 5th!)

**✅ Must-Remember Smart Street Lighting Design:**
- Sensors: **LDR + PIR**
- Communication: **LoRa / Wi-Fi**
- Energy savings: **~70–80%**
- Components: **ESP32 + LED Driver + Current Sensor + Cloud**

**✅ Must-Remember Smart Parking Design:**
- Sensor: **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic**
- Principle: **Time-of-flight** ($d = v \cdot t / 2$)
- Threshold: **1.5 m** for occupancy detection
- Data flow: Sensor → Arduino → Raspberry Pi → Cloud → Mobile App

**✅ Must-Remember Formulas:**
- Power: $P = V \times I$
- Daily Energy: $E = P \times t$
- Distance: $d = v \cdot t / 2$
- Savings: $\eta = (E_{old} - E_{new}) / E_{old} \times 100\%$
- Friis: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB}$

**✅ Must-Remember Examples for Each Layer:**
- Perception: **Sensor**, **RFID**
- Network: **Wi-Fi**, **ZigBee**
- Middleware: **MQTT**, **CoAP**
- Application: **Mobile app**, **Dashboard**
- Business: **Analytics**, **Privacy**

**✅ Must-Remember "Sense → Analyze → Act" Loop:**
- Every IoT system follows: **Sense** (sensor) → **Analyze** (cloud/edge) → **Act** (actuator). Mention this in **any 7-mark question** for a strong conceptual answer.

**✅ Must-Remember Communication Protocols:**
- Short-range: **Bluetooth, ZigBee, NFC**
- Long-range: **LoRa, NB-IoT, SigFox**
- High-bandwidth: **Wi-Fi, 4G/5G**

**✅ Quick Comparative Points (For Theory Questions):**
- IoT vs M2M: IoT is **internet-based + heterogeneous**; M2M is typically **point-to-point**.
- Smart vs Conventional: Smart = **sensor + cloud + automation**; Conventional = **manual / fixed-timer**.
